text:
In this module, we’ll start with some basics. What is life insurance? What is risk? Viewing life insurance basics through the eyes of potential insurance customers, you’ll learn about:

Risk and risk management

Regulation of insurance

Life insurance as a contract

The value exchange in an insurance transaction

Here you’ll meet Hector Garcia, a young married man and expectant father. Follow Hector as he begins his journey into the world of insurance. In this lesson, Hector learns basic insurance terminology, how insurance companies manage risks, who’s insurable, and how underwriters determine his degree of risk (mức độ rủi ro).

Section List:

Learning Objectives

Risky Business

Types of Risk

Risk Management

Life Insurance Terminology

Insurable Risks

Underwriters and Underwriting

Risk Classes

End of Lesson Questions

After studying this lesson, you will be able to:

Distinguish between speculative risk (rủi ro đầu cơ) and pure risk (rủi ro thuần túy).

Define basic insurance terminology (thuật ngữ bảo hiểm cơ bản).

Describe the characteristics of insurable risks.

Explain the role of underwriters and how they assess specific risks.

Define antiselection (lựa chọn bất lợi) and give examples of two factors that can increase or decrease the likelihood that an individual will suffer a loss.

Identify four risk classes (hạng mức rủi ro) for proposed insureds (người được đề xuất bảo hiểm).

Identify four risk management techniques (4 kỹ thuật quản lý rủi ro).

Assume you're applying for a life insuranc. During his presentation, the insurance agent makes the following statements. Are they all true? (Select all that apply.)

People buy insurance to reduce the financial risk they face, but not every type of risk is insurable.

Both pure risks and speculative risks are insurable.

Most life insurance policies are valued contracts (hợp đồng định giá), which state at the time of policy issue the amount of the benefit payable when the insured person dies.

Most life insurance policies are issued at substandard premium rates (phí bảo hiểm dưới chuẩn).

Explanation:

People buy insurance to reduce the financial risk they face, but not every type of risk is insurable. (Đúng)
Giải thích:

Mọi người mua bảo hiểm để giảm bớt rủi ro tài chính.

Tuy nhiên không phải rủi ro nào cũng có thể bảo hiểm (ví dụ: rủi ro đầu cơ/speculative risk không được bảo hiểm).

Both pure risks and speculative risks are insurable. (Sai)

Giải thích:

Chỉ rủi ro thuần túy (Pure Risk - 純粋危険 - Thuần Túy Nguy Hiểm) mới có thể được bảo hiểm (ví dụ: tử vong, bệnh tật).

Rủi ro đầu cơ (Speculative Risk - 投機危険 - Đầu Cơ Nguy Hiểm) (như đầu tư cổ phiếu, cờ bạc) không được bảo hiểm.

Most life insurance policies are valued contracts, which state at the time of policy issue the amount of the benefit payable when the insured person dies. (Đúng)

Giải thích:

Hầu hết các hợp đồng bảo hiểm nhân thọ là Valued Contracts (Hợp đồng định giá).

Số tiền bồi thường (death benefit) được xác định rõ khi hợp đồng được phát hành, không phụ thuộc vào tổn thất thực tế.

Most life insurance policies are issued at substandard premium rates. (Sai)

Giải thích:

Hầu hết các hợp đồng bảo hiểm được phát hành với mức phí chuẩn (Standard Premium Rate), vì đa số khách hàng thuộc nhóm rủi ro bình thường.

Substandard Premium Rate chỉ áp dụng cho những người có rủi ro sức khỏe cao hoặc nghề nghiệp đặc biệt nguy hiểm.

Distinguish between speculative risk and pure risk.

### What is Life Insurance?
Life insurance reduces the economic impact that occurs when someone dies. How does it do this? To understand how first we need to discuss risk.

### What is Risk?
Risk is the possibility of an unexpected result. This could be a gain or a loss.

Usually, we worry about unexpected losses, but you’d probably like to see an unexpected gain, wouldn’t you?

### Speculative Risk vs. Pure Risk
Some risks are speculative risks that can result in a loss, gain, or no change....

…while other risks are pure risks that involve either a loss or no loss. There is no possibility of gain.

subsection: Example of Speculative and Pure Risk
text:
Example: Carol and John Wilson are both employed full time and their family needs both of their incomes to support their standard of living. Recently, they purchased $10,000 of stock in a growing software company. They believe that the software company will be successful and that their investment will soon be worth a lot more than $10,000.

Analysis: The Wilsons’ investment in a software company is an example of speculative risk because the value of their investment may increase, remain the same, or decrease.

The possibility that one or both of the Wilsons could die leaving their family with a loss of income is an example of a pure risk because there is no possibility of a gain in that situation.

Insurance companies won’t insure all types of risks. Which risks do you think are insurable?

Only pure risks

Only speculative risks

Both pure risks and speculative risks

Neither pure risks nor speculative risks

Explanation: Only pure risks

Insurance is designed to compensate for financial loss, not to provide an opportunity for financial gain. So, only pure risks can be insured. Because speculative risks involve the possibility of gain, they are not insurable.

Identify four risk management techniques.

Risks are a part of life. When you travel, start a business, make a purchase, or cross the street, you take a risk.

Because risks represent uncertainties, we try to manage them to eliminate or minimize our potential losses and, ideally, to maximize our gains.

Once risks have been identified and assessed, all techniques to manage the risk fall into one or more of these four major categories: avoid, control, transfer or accept.

How do these risk management techniques work? Select the risk management technique that matches the example.

Steve is nervous about losing money in the stock market, so he doesn’t invest in stocks.

Avoiding Risk

Controlling Risk

Accepting Risk

Transferring Risk

Explanation: Avoid Risk

By not investing in the stock market, Steve avoids the risk of losing money in the stock market. While effective, this technique isn’t always practical.

Mark uses a laptop computer in his business. If he loses the laptop or accidentally damages it, Mark will have to purchase another laptop or pay to have it repaired.

Avoiding Risk

Controlling Risk

Accepting Risk

Transferring Risk

Explanation: Accepting Risk

By assuming the financial responsibility for replacing a lost/damaged laptop, Mark accepts the risk. If you do not manage a risk by other means—avoiding, controlling, or transferring the risk—then you accept that risk.

To reduce the likelihood of fire destroying her convenience store, Rebecca installed smoke detectors and a sprinkler system.

Avoiding Risk

Controlling Risk

Accepting Risk

Transferring Risk

Explanation: Controlling Risk
Installing smoke detectors and sprinkler systems will help Rebecca control her risk by preventing or reducing losses from a fire.

Conrad purchased a disability income insurance policy to provide for his family in case he becomes sick or injured and can’t work.

Avoiding Risk

Controlling Risk

Accepting Risk

Transferring Risk

Explanation: Transferring Risk

By purchasing disability insurance coverage, Conrad transfers the financial responsibility for the risk to an insurance company.

Define basic insurance terminology.

### Insurance
Insurance is a method for transferring risk from an individual to an insurance company.

An insurer that accepts a risk makes a promise to pay an amount of money (the policy benefit) if a certain loss occurs.

In exchange for this promise, the person buying the insurance pays an amount of money, or premium, to the insurer.

So now you can probably guess what life insurance is:

A promise to pay money (a policy benefit) when the person named in the policy dies.

For example, Barbara Allen bought a $500,000 insurance policy on her life from Best Life Insurance Company. If Barbara dies while the policy is in force, Best will pay $500,000 to Barbara’s husband, Bob.

Life insurance protects against the financial consequences of dying, especially dying earlier than expected.

Financial consequences of death include the deceased person’s

Lost future income

Funeral expenses

Final medical expenses

Unpaid debts

Insurance is based on the concept of risk pooling (gộp rủi ro).

People who face the risk of a particular loss (tổn thất cụ thể)—like the financial loss associated with death—transfer this risk to an insurance company.

Insurers collect premiums from all insured people and spread the cost (phân tán chi phí) of the relatively few anticipated losses (số lượng tổn thất dự kiến tương đối) among all insureds.

Like dying too soon, living too long—or longer than expected—carries a financial risk.

Most of us don’t consider living a long life to be risky, but what would you do if you ran out of money while you were still alive?

This risk of outliving your assets is real. People can transfer this risk to an insurer by purchasing an annuity and receiving a promise of periodic payments for a set period or for life. Some annuities provide income for the rest of a person’s life, no matter how long that is.

For example, Wladimir Dobrzynski might purchase an annuity that will provide monthly payments of $1,000 to him for 20 years, with the understanding that if he lives another 30 years, he will have to survive 10 years without that source of income. Or he might purchase an annuity that provides monthly payments of $500 for the rest of his life, regardless of how long he lives.

Identify the type of insurance product that each person can use to address his needs.

Minh wants to provide her family with a source of income that they can use if she dies.

Annuity

Life Insurance

Answer: Life Insurance

Isaiah wants to make sure that he has a monthly income once he retires.

Annuity

Life Insurance

Answer: Annuity

Read the dialogue and recap Hector and Maria’s situation:

| Hector: So I’m the applicant, right? John: Yes, you’re the person applying for this policy. You actually serve a lot of roles. Since you’ll own the policy, you’ll be the policyowner. And, since we’re insuring your life, you are also the insured. Hector The money would go to my wife Maria. That makes her the beneficiary, right? John That’s right! John I’m really glad you decided to take out a policy on Maria, too. You’d need something to replace her income if something happened to her. Hector Who fills all of the roles for Maria’s policy? John You’re still the applicant, and you’ll be the policyowner. Since you’ll own the policy on Maria’s life, her policy is known as a third-party policy, and you’re the third party. Hector OK, I got it. John Maria will have to sign a statement saying that it’s okay with her that you purchase this policy. Now, you tell me—who is the insured and who’s the beneficiary? |
|---|

Insurance Policy on Hector's Life

Applicant: Hector

Policyowner: Hector

Insured: Hector

Beneficiary: Maria

Third-party policy? No

Insurance Policy on Maria’s Life

Applicant: Hector

Policyowner: Hector

Insured: Maria

Beneficiary: Hector

Third-party policy? Yes

The applicant is usually the policyowner. This is true for both policies.

The policyowner and the insured (policyowner-insured) are often the same person. This is true for the policy insuring Hector

When a covered loss occurs, the beneficiary files a claim with the insurance company, requesting payment under the terms of the policy

Identify the term that is being defined.

The person or entity that applies for an insurance policy.

Applicant

Beneficiary

Insured

Policyowner

Third-Party Policy

Answer: Applicant

The person or entity that owns the insurance policy.

Applicant

Beneficiary

Insured

Policyowner

Third-Party Policy

Answer: Policyowner

The person whose life, health, or property is insured under the policy.

Applicant

Beneficiary

Insured

Policyowner

Third-Party Policy

Answer: Insured

The person or party the policyowner names to receive the life insurance policy benefit.

Applicant

Beneficiary

Insured

Policyowner

Third-Party Policy

Answer: Beneficiary

A policy purchased by one person or entity on the life of another person.

Applicant

Beneficiary

Insured

Policyowner

Third-Party Policy

Answer: Third-Party Policy

Brad Lischka purchases a life insurance policy covering the life of his wife Cathy. He names their son, Nicholas, to receive the policy benefit if Cathy dies. Match the people with their roles.

Brad purchased the policy so he would be considered the ______________.

Policyowner

Beneficiary

Insured

Answer: Policyowner

Cathy would be the ______________.

Policyowner

Beneficiary

Insured

Answer: Insured

If Cathy dies the policy benefit would go to their son, Nicholas, the ______________.

Policyowner

Beneficiary

Insured

Answer: Beneficiary

Describe the characteristics of insurable risks.

### What do you think?
Monica Hardwick has an old car and wants a new one, so she plans to push her car down a hill into a lake. Is this an insurable loss?

Yes

No

Can’t tell. Need more information.

Answer: No

Explanation: To be insurable, a loss must occur by chance, which means it is either caused by an unexpected event or not intentionally caused by the insured person.

To be insurable, a loss must be:

Indefinite in terms of time  and amount

Definite in terms of time and amount

Answer: Definite in terms of time and amount

Explanation: An insurable loss must be definite in terms of time and amount. The insurer must be able to determine when to pay policy benefits and how much those benefits should be. The policy language controls how the insurer will determine the benefit amount, and whether the policy is considered a contract of indemnity (hợp đồng bồi thường) or a valued contract. (hợp đồng định giá).

subsection: Contract of Indemnity vs. Valued Contract
text:
Most insurance policies, other than life insurance policies, are contracts of indemnity which base benefits on the actual amount of the financial loss that results from a covered event when it occurs, subject to maximum limits.

Most life insurance policies are valued contracts, which state the benefit payable at the time of policy issue.

This benefit amount is the policy’s face amount (face value) because it is listed on the face, or first, page of a life insurance policy.

Karen Bell has individual medical expense insurance (bảo hiểm chi phí y tế). When she broke her leg, her insurance company paid a benefit for her medical expenses. Do you think the insurer based the amount of the benefit on an amount stated in her policy or on the actual cost of her medical care?

An exact amount stated in Karen’s policy

The actual cost of Karen’s medical expenses

Answer: The actual cost of Karen’s medical expenses

Explanation: Most health insurance policies are contracts of indemnity and base benefits on the actual amount of the financial loss for a covered event when it occurs, subject to maximum limits stated in the policies.

Charlie Crittenden is the policyowner-insured of a $100,000 life insurance policy. Charlie became sick and died from his illness while the policy was in force. The insurance company paid a death benefit to his wife. What did the insurance company use as the basis for the death benefit?

The policy’s $100,000 face amount (số tiền ghi trên hợp đồng)

The actual cost of Charlie’s final medical and funeral expenses

Answer: The policy’s $100,000 face amount

Explanation: Most life insurance policies are valued contracts, which state at the time of policy issue the amount of the benefit payable when the insured person dies.

Harold Monroe would like to buy insurance on his rake, in case he breaks it. Do you think his rake is insurable?

Yes

No

Can’t tell. No more information

Answer: No

Explanation: Insignificant losses (tổn thất nhỏ) are not normally insurable. The high expense of paying benefits for very small losses would make the cost of insurance too expensive.

On the other hand, a loss that is significant (tổn thất lớn) enough to cause financial hardship (khó khăn tài chính) usually is considered insurable.

Insurers look at the loss rate—the frequency of losses—when determining whether a risk is insurable. To be insurable, the loss rate must be…

Predictable

Unpredictable

Answer: Predictable

Explanation: An insurer must be able to predict the probable rate of loss—the loss rate—that a group of insureds will experience otherwise they wouldn’t know how much to charge each person for the coverage. Insurers predict the loss rate by predicting the number and timing of covered losses for that group.

How does a life insurer determine the loss rate?

Insurers look at past events to predict the likelihood of future losses for a given group. The larger the group, the more accurate the insurer’s loss rate predictions are likely to be. This is true because of a concept called the law of large numbers.

The law of large numbers states that, typically, the more times you observe a particular event, the more likely it is that the observed results will approximate the true probability that the event will occur.

To be insurable, a loss...

MUST be catastrophic to insurer

MUST NOT be catastrophic to the insurer

Answer: MUST NOT be catastrophic to the insurer

Explanation: If a single event would cause catastrophic financial damage to the insurer, the loss isn’t insurable.

To reduce the possibility of catastrophic loss, insurers transfer some risk to other insurers by purchasing insurance for themselves.

Reinsurance (tái bảo hiểm) is insurance for insurance companies.

Each insurer establishes a retention limit (giới hạn giữ lại)—the maximum amount of risk the insurer is willing to carry at its own risk. For example, a life insurance company may determine that it will only insure up to $500,000 on any one life.

Reinsurance allows the insurer—the direct writer (công ty bảo hiểm gốc) or ceding company (công ty chuyển nhượng)—to transfer (cede) risk above its retention limit to a reinsurer (công ty tái bảo hiểm) or assuming company (công ty nhận rủi ro). The reinsurer can accept all of the transferred risk, or it can cede some of the risk to a second reinsurer, known as a retrocessionaire (công ty tái bảo hiểm thứ hai)

Select the statements that would describe being insurable.(Select all that apply.)

The risk must be a pure risk

The risk must be a speculative risk

The loss must occur by chance

The loss must not occur by chance

The loss must be indefinite in time & amount

The loss must be definite in time & amount

The loss must be insignificant

The loss must be significant

The loss rate must be predictable

The loss rate must be unpredictable

The loss cannot be catastrophic to the insurer

The loss must be catastrophic to the insurer

Answer:

The risk must be a pure risk

The loss must occur by chance

The loss must be definite in time & amount

The loss must be significant

The loss rate must be predictable

The loss cannot be catastrophic to the insurer

Robert Smith owns a life insurance policy that will pay his named beneficiary $250,000 when Robert dies. Is his insurance policy an example of a valued contact or a contract of indemnity?

Valued contract

Contract of indemnity

Answer: Valued contract

Explanation: Robert’s contract is a valued contract because it states at the time of policy issue the amount of the benefit payable when the insured dies. By contrast, a contract of indemnity bases the insurance benefit on the actual amount of the financial loss for a covered event when it occurs.

The Colonnade Insurance Company issued a $1 million life insurance policy to Albert Goldin. Colonnade transferred $500,000 of the risk to the Fairness Insurance Company. Fairness accepted the $500,000 and then transferred $300,000 of the risk to the Journey Financial Services Company. Match each participant with the term that best describes it.

Colonade Insurance Company is the ____________.

Direct Writer

Reinsurer

Retrocessionaire

Answer: Direct Writer

Explanation: Colonnade is the direct writer; it issued the policy.

Fairness Insurance Company is the ____________.

Direct Writer

Reinsurer

Retrocessionaire

Answer: Reinsurer

Explanation: Fairness is a reinsurer; it accepted risk from a direct writer.

Journey Financial Services Company is the ____________.

Direct Writer

Reinsurer

Retrocessionaire

Answer: Retroccestionaire

Explanation: Journey is a retrocessionaire; it accepted risk from a reinsurer.

Explain the role of underwriters and how they assess specific risks.

After customer fills out the life insurance application , the application goes to Insurance Company’s underwriting department where its underwriters perform underwriting by reviewing the application and classifying the degree of risk the customer presents.

What do underwriters look for? Anything that may increase the amount of risk that the customer presents to the insurance company. Underwriters search for anything that makes them say "Uh-Oh."

Suppose YOU are the underwriter at the Insurance Company.

What would you think if you learned that customer’s family history (tiền sử gia đình) showed a high incidence (tỷ lệ cao) of heart disease (bệnh tim) that led to an early death (tử vong sớm)?

Uh-Oh

No problem

Answer: Uh-Oh

Explanation: A family history of heart disease is a medical risk factor. the customer would have a greater likelihood of dying sooner than would a person of the same age and sex who does not have a family history of heart disease. Other risk factors may involve a person’s unstable finances, hazardous job, or dangerous personal activities or hobbies.

What would you think if you learned that Hector was once convicted of a felony (kết án trọng tội)?

Uh-Oh

No problem

Answer: Uh-Oh

Explanation: Moral hazard (rủi ro đạo đức) is the possibility that a person may act dishonestly in an insurance transaction. If Hector has had a history of illegal (bất hợp pháp) or unethical (phi đạo đức) behavior, he could behave similarly when buying insurance.

Underwriters must investigate the information on insurance applications carefully because of the likelihood that people who need insurance more are more likely to apply for insurance, a tendency called antiselection (lựa chọn bất lợi).

Ideally, insurers want to accept applicants who are low or average risks.

Harold Swenson’s insurance application indicates that he works on a commercial fishing boat, which is generally regarded as a more dangerous profession than average. Do you think an underwriter would consider Harold’s occupation as a risk factor that is likely to increase his likelihood of loss?

Yes

No

Answer: Yes

Explanation: Harold’s job presents a risk factor because it might increase his likelihood of loss. Other risk factors include dangerous hobbies, alcohol or substance abuse (lạm dụng rượu hoặc chất kích thích), or an adverse medical history (tiền sử bệnh lý bất lợi).

So these “Uh-Ohs” would disqualify (loại) someone for life insurance, right?

Not necessarily, but Seashore’s underwriters will carefully consider Hector’s situation before assigning him to a risk class—a grouping of similar-risk insureds.

Insurers use risk classes to decide how much to charge each person for coverage. People in different risk classes are charged different premiums based on the degree of risk they represent to the insurer.

If insurers charged everyone the same amount, lower-risk people would pay too much for their coverage, and higher-risk people would pay too little.

By assigning proposed insureds to risk classes, the insurer charges a fair amount for each policy.

What is the purpose of assigning proposed insureds to risk classes?

To make sure everyone pays the same amount for life insurance

To make sure everyone pays a fair amount for their life insurance

To increase the effect of antiselection

Answer: To make sure everyone pays a fair amount for their life insurance

Explanation: By assigning each proposed insured to an appropriate risk class, the insurer charges a fair amount for each policy. Insurers try to avoid, not increase, the effect of antiselection, which is the tendency of people who are higher risks to seek insurance protection to a greater extent than those who are lower risks.

Identify four risk classes for proposed insureds.

Individual life insurers usually identify at least four risk classes for proposed insureds.

Standard (rủi ro tiêu chuẩn)

Insurers classify proposed insureds whose likelihood of loss is not significantly greater than average as standard risks. The insurance company charges its standard premium rates.

Most life insurance policies are issued at standard premium rates (mức phí bảo hiểm tiêu chuẩn).

Preferred (rủi ro ưu đãi)

Insurers classify proposed insureds whose likelihood of loss is significantly lower than average as preferred risks.

The insurance company charges these insureds preferred premium rates, which are lower than standard premium rates.

Substandard (rủi ro dưới chuẩn)

Insurers classify proposed insureds whose likelihood of loss is significantly greater than average as substandard risks.

The insurance company charges them substandard premium rates, which are higher than standard premium rates.

Declined (rủi ro bị từ chối)

Some proposed insureds present a risk that is too great for the insurer to cover. This might include people with a poor health history or those who engage in exceptionally risky activities, such as sky diving or mountain climbing.

These proposed insureds are known as declined risks.

The Hillside Insurance Company recently evaluated four applicants for insurance. Read each question and match the person with the term that best describes an appropriate underwriting decision.

Jason is in good health and is a test pilot for the military. Some of his maneuvers include flying inverted, rolling the aircraft, and putting the aircraft in a dive.

Standard risk class

Preferred risk class

Substandard risk class

Declined risk class

Answer: Declined risk class

Explanation: Some proposed insureds present a risk that is too great for the insurer to cover--known as declined risks. This might include people who engage in exceptionally risky activities, including being a test pilot. Jason is classified as a declined risk.

Melinda is in good health and is a recent college graduate. She works as a data analyst.

Standard risk class

Preferred risk class

Substandard risk class

Declined risk class

Answer: Standard risk class

Explanation: Insurers classify proposed insureds whose likelihood of loss is not significantly greater than average as standard risks. As a person in good health and a non-dangerous occupation, Melinda is classified as a standard risk.

Roberto is in excellent health. He maintains his health through diet and exercise.

Standard risk class

Preferred risk class

Substandard risk class

Declined risk class

Answer: Preferred risk class

Explanation: Insurers classify proposed insureds whose likelihood of loss is significantly lower than average as preferred risks. As someone who maintains his excellent health, Roberto is classified as a preferred risk.

Yolanda has had asthma for 10 years. Otherwise, she is in good health.

Standard risk class

Preferred risk class

Substandard risk class

Declined risk class

Answer: Substandard risk class

Explanation: Insurers classify proposed insureds whose likelihood of loss is significantly greater than average as substandard risks. Because of her asthma, Yolanda is classified as a substandard risk.

If a risk has all these characteristics AND if the underwriters approve the application, is the risk then insurable?

Not quite. Underwriters have to verify that you need the coverage and can afford to pay for it. They also need to verify that the policyowner will suffer a genuine loss (tổn thất tài chính thật sự) if the insured person dies. We call this having an insurable interest (quyền lợi được bảo hiểm) in the risk insured.

Laws in most jurisdictions (khu vực pháp lý) require insurable interest to be present at policy issue. This prevents people from buying insurance as a wager (cá cược), hoping to profit when they have no risk of financial loss.

subsection: So, is insurance really Risky Business?
text:
Not really. Insurance relieves the customer of risk.

Insurers expertly manage risks through the techniques we discussed:

Risk pooling

Careful underwriting

The ability to predict losses

Reinsurance

When evaluating an application, underwriters prefer the presence of insurable interest between the policyowner and insured, but they don’t require insurable interest to issue the policy.

True

False

Answer: False

Explanation: Laws in most states require insurable interest to be present at policy issue to prevent people from buying insurance to wager on other people’s lives.

After studying this lesson, you will be able to:

Identify types of financial institutions (tổ chức tài chính)

Describe the impact of convergence (sự hội tụ), consolidation (sự hợp nhất), and globalization (toàn cầu hóa) on insurance companies.

Explain why insurance companies must be organized as corporations (công ty cổ phần).

Distinguish among stock insurers (công ty bảo hiểm cổ phần), mutual insurers (công ty bảo hiểm tương hỗ), and fraternal benefit societies (hội tương trợ phúc lợi).

Identify the two primary types (hai hình thức chính) of insurance regulation (quy định bảo hiểm) in most countries.

Describe the roles of the U.S. state (chính quyền bang) and federal governments (chính quyền liên bang) with respect (quản lý) to insurance regulation (quy định bảo hiểm).
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

section: Financial Institutions and the Insurance Industry
text:
Identify types of financial institutions.

Describe the impact of convergence, consolidation, and globalization on insurance companies.

### What do you think?
The financial services industry is made up of different kinds of financial institutions. People often go to one type of financial institution to satisfy one financial need and to another for a different need.

See if you can match each financial need with the financial institution(s) that can help them.

Take out a car loan (Choose all that apply)

Bank

Insurance Company

Securities

Answer: Bank

Protect the value of their home from fire (Choose all that apply)

Bank

Insurance Company

Securities

Answer: Insurance Company

Save for college (Choose all that apply)

Bank

Insurance Company

Securities

Answer: All is correct

Invest money they've inherited (Choose all that apply)

Bank

Insurance Company

Securities

Answer: All is correct

Explanation: Until recently, a financial institution could only operate in one type of financial sector: banking, insurance, or investment securities. Today, a financial institution may operate in all three of these sectors. So, for example, a bank might sell insurance products or investment securities. People have lots of options for satisfying their financial needs. Let’s learn more.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

section: Financial Institutions and the Insurance Industry
text:
For regulatory purposes (vì mục đích quản lý), the financial services industry is divided into three sectors: insurance, banking, and securities.

Within these three sectors operate many different types of financial institutions.

Depository Institution (tổ chức nhận tiền gửi)

Commercial banks, savings and loan associations, and credit unions (liên hiệp tín dụng) are examples of depository institutions, which accept deposits and make loans.

Securities Firm (Công ty chứng khoán)

Securities firms specialize in buying and selling securities. A security is a certificate that represents either an ownership interest (quyền sở hữu) (a share of stock) or a debt owed (một khoản nợ) (a bond).

Mutual fund companies are a type of securities firm. Mutual fund shares represent a proportional ownership (quyền sở hữu tỷ lệ tương ứng) of the assets in a mutual fund investment portfolio.

Finance Companies

Finance companies specialize in making short- and medium-term loans.

Insurance Company

An insurance company provides insurance and annuities to help protect people against financial losses.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

section: Financial Institutions and the Insurance Industry
text:
Historically, regulatory barriers prevented banks, insurance companies, and securities firms in the U.S. from competing directly with each other.

In the 1990s, deregulation (việc nới lỏng quy định) allowed convergence (sự hội tụ) which resulted in consolidation (sự hợp nhất) in the financial services industry.

Consolidation has occurred primarily (chủ yếu) through mergers (hợp nhất) and acquisitions (mua lại).

Merger: Two companies are combined into one company. One company survives as a legal entity and the other company ceases (chấm dứt) to exist.

Acquisition: One company purchases a controlling interest (phần lớn quyền kiếm soát) in another company. After the acquisition, both companies continue to exist as legal entities (thực thể pháp lý or tư cách pháp lý).

Acquisitions can include the purchase of an entire company or just one line of business (một mảng kinh doanh cụ thể).

The result of consolidation is that companies that previously sold only one type of financial product now may offer multiple types of financial products.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

section: Financial Institutions and the Insurance Industry
text:
Another trend impacting financial institutions is globalization.

Financial services institutions, particularly those from Western Europe and North America, have been expanding their customer bases in nations outside their countries of origin.

Often companies have to purchase financial institutions in other countries to gain entry into foreign markets.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

section: Financial Institutions and the Insurance Industry
text:
People and businesses purchase insurance products to cover three types of risk:

Personal risk: Risks arising from the possibility of death (tử vong), illness (bệnh tật), injury (chấn thương), inability to work (mất khả năng lao động), or outliving one’s savings (sống quá lâu khiến cạn kiệt tiền tiết kiệm)

Property damage risk: Risks occurring from damage to (hư hỏng), destruction of (phá hủy), or the disappearance (mất mát) of real property such as a home, an automobile (ô tô), or personal belongings (đồ dùng cá nhân)

Liability risk: Risks occurring from acts (hành vi) or omissions (thiếu sót) that result in harm (gây thiệt hại) to others or their property

Life and health insurance companies insure personal risks. Property/Casualty insurance companies (công ty bảo hiểm tài sản và thiệt hại) insure property damage and liability risks.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

section: Financial Institutions and the Insurance Industry
text:
Do you know the difference between life and health products and property/casualty products?

Select the products that are in the life and health category.

Automobile insurance

Life insurance

Homeowners insurance

Annuities

Answer: Life insurance, Medical expense insurance, Disability insurance, Annuities

Life and Health Products

Life insurance

Medical expense insurance

Disability insurance

Annuities

Automobile insurance

source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

section: Financial Institutions and the Insurance Industry
text:
Which member of the financial services industry accepts deposits and makes loans?

Finance company

Insurance company

Depository institution

Answer: Depository

Explanation: Depository institutions accept deposits and make loans. Finance companies make short- and medium-term loans. Insurance companies issue products that cover personal risk, property damage risk, and liability risk.

Which member of the financial services industry pools money from many, often thousands, of investors to build diversified portfolios of investment securities?

Securities firm

Mutual fund company

Answer: Mutual fund company

Explanation: Securities firms buy and sell stocks, bonds, and other investments on behalf of investors. Mutual fund companies maintain a diversified portfolio by using investor’s funds to buy and sell a variety of securities.

Which member of the financial services industry primarily insures against financial losses resulting from death, disability, illness, accident, and outliving one’s savings?

Property/Casualty insurer

Life and heath insurer

Finance company

Answer: Life and heath insurer

Explanation : Life and health insurers protect against financial losses from death, disability, illness, accident, and outliving one’s savings. Property/casualty insurers insure against losses to your property and from your unintentionally harming others. Finance companies make short and medium-term loans.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
Explain why insurance companies must be organized as corporations.

Distinguish among stock insurers (công ty bảo hiểm cổ phần), mutual insurers (công ty bảo hiểm tương hỗ), and fraternal benefit societies (hội tương trợ phúc lợi).

In most countries, life and health insurance companies (các công ty bảo hiểm nhân thọ và sức khỏe) and other major businesses are organized as corporations (công ty cổ phần).

A corporation provides an element (yếu tổ) of stability (ổn định) and permanence (lâu dài) that makes it an ideal structure for insurers, whose contractual obligations (nghĩa vụ hợp đồng) extend many years into the future.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
Typically, insurance companies are organized as stock insurance companies, mutual insurance companies, or fraternal benefit societies.

Stock Insurer

Can issue shares of stock

Owned by stockholders (cổ đông), who have voting rights (quyền biểu quyết) in the company

Stockholders may receive shares of operating profits (lợi nhuận hoạt động) known as stock dividends (cổ tức bằng  cổ phiếu)

Mutual insurer

Owned by policyowners

Policyowners have membership rights (voting rights in the company)

Policyowners may periodically (định kỳ) receive an amount of money known as a policy dividend (cổ tức hợp đồng bảo hiểm)

Fraternal Benefit Society

Owned by members of a fraternal lodge system (hệ thống hội huynh đệ)

Provides social and insurance benefits only to fraternal members and their families

Legally required to have a representative form of government (hình thức quản trị đại diện. Ví dụ như bầu cử lãnh đạo, hội đồng đại diện)
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
The basic difference between stock life insurance companies and mutual life insurance companies is their ownership structure. A mutual life insurance company is owned by its policy holders. And I work for Northwestern Mutual and so we answer to our policy holders. They’re our primary focus. With a stock life insurance company, they are owned by their shareholders, the investors. One of the differences is that the stock company then has access to the equity markets to raise capital

From a regulatory perspective, whether you are a stock company or a mutual company, both will have to address regulatory issues and concerns within the states that they operate and those state insurance commissioners. A stock company will then have the added layer having to deal with the regulatory issues of being a stock company and being either private or on the equity market exchanges.

Mutual companies do have an annual policy holder meeting where the policy holders can come and gather and discuss issues and things that are going on currently with the company. They also have voting rights in terms of the board.

Sean McGinn

Assistant Director

Life Product

Northwestern Mutual
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:

Operates through a lodge system

Stock

Mutual

Fraternal

Answer: Fraternal

Can issue shares of stock

Stock

Mutual

Fraternal

Answer: Stock

Owned by the company’s policyowners

Stock

Mutual

Fraternal

Answer: Mutual

Stockholders have voting rights in the company

Stock

Mutual

Fraternal

Answer: Stock

Policyowners may periodically receive an amount of money if company experience is positive for the year

Stock

Mutual

Fraternal

Answer: Mutual
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
Identify the two primary types of insurance regulation (quy định bảo hiểm) in most countries.

Describe the roles of the U.S. state and federal governments with respect to insurance regulation.

### Regulation
The insurance industry plays a vital role in the stability of a nation’s economy by protecting people against economic loss and by providing them with mechanisms (cơ chế) for saving and investing.

Governments play a vital role in maintaining the public’s trust in the insurance industry by regulating (quy định) and monitoring (giám sát) the solvency (khả năng thanh toán) and ethical conduct of insurance companies.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
1. To ensure that every insurance company remains financially sound—is able to meet its debts and pay contractual obligations when they come due.

Solvency Regulation (Quy định về khả năng thanh toán)

Solvency regulations focus on making sure that insurance companies remain solvent. Solvency regulation is known as prudential regulation (quy định thận trọng) in many countries.

Most countries impose minimum financial requirements (áp đặt các yêu cầu tài chính) that an insurer must meet before it can conduct business.

Laws also regulate (điều chỉnh) the financial aspects of an insurance policy’s design and amounts of assets that insurers must maintain to support the liabilities of the company to its policyowners.

If an insurer were to have financial difficulties, laws are also in place in many countries and in all states in the United States to allow the government to act to protect the public interest. (lợi ích công cộng)

2. To ensure that every insurance company conducts its business fairly with honesty, integrity (đạo đức), and competence (năng lực).

Market Conduct Regulation (Quy định về hành vi thị trường)

Many countries have enacted market conduct laws (luật hành vi thị trường) that regulate (điều chỉnh) how insurance companies conduct business.

Market conduct laws help ensure that customers receive fair and accurate information before they buy insurance and equitable treatment (đối xử công bằng) during the buying process.

Such laws typically regulate the form and content of insurance advertisements to make sure they don’t mislead consumers as well as various aspects of the sales process.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
Countries also may differ in their systems for enacting and overseeing insurance companies.

Many countries centralize insurance regulation under the supervision of the national government. For example, in India, the sole insurance regulator is the national Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority (IRDA).

Some countries, including the United States and Canada, have federal systems of government in which a federal government shares power with lower-level governments, such as state governments (U.S.) or provincial governments (Canada).
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
Who primarily regulates insurance in the United States?

The federal government

Individual state governments

The federal government and state governments equally share regulation

Answer: Individual state governments

Explanation: Individual states primarily regulate insurance, although the federal government also plays a role.

The McCarran-Ferguson Act (1945) is a federal law (luật liên bang) that allows state governments (chính quyền tiểu bang) to regulate the business of insurance as long as Congress considers the state legislation (luật pháp tiểu bang) adequate (đầy đủ). Congress can intervene (can thiệp) if it considers state regulation to be inadequate (không đầy đủ).

The Dodd-Frank Act enacted in 2010 created a new federal agency, the Federal Insurance Office (FIO), with authority to monitor the insurance industry, identify areas with inadequate state regulation, and handle international insurance issues.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
State regulations have worked very well so far.

Each state has enacted its own laws that regulate the insurance companies operating in it. And, each has a state insurance department to make sure those insurers are following their insurance regulations.

An insurance commissioner (ủy ban bảo hiểm) (or superintendent (tổng thanh tra bảo hiểm) of insurance or director of insurance) directs the state insurance department.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
See as this industry expert explains how federal insurance regulation, through the Federal Insurance Office (FIO), will likely expand in the United States.

I do believe that the way the life insurance industry is regulated will change fairly dramatically going forward. Currently, we are in a patchwork system where each state has its own individual set of rules and regulations. There are model regulations to attempt to make these differences less across the states, but it is still quite a burden on life insurance companies that ultimately is reflected in a higher price to the consumer.

So yes, I do see the Federal Insurance Office playing a larger role as we move forward with the way the life insurance industry is regulated. Currently we have, what I would say is an untenable situation with the Federal Insurance Office, representing the United States of America on International boards and yet having no legislative authority back at home, that still resides with the states. I think you will see a little bit of a bumpy road to get to federal regulation of life insurance, but I believe it would be in the best interest of the consumer to ultimately get there.

Lincoln Demier

Product Design Manager

American Family Insurance
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
You might think things must get complicated for insurance companies operating in more than one state.

However, an organization called the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) (Hiệp hội các Ủy viên Bảo hiểm Quốc gia) helps standardize regulations among the states.

State insurance departments work with the NAIC to develop and promote model bills and regulations that serve as model laws.

The NAIC helps ensure that the laws among all the states are as uniform  as possible (càng đồng nhất càng tốt).

For starters, before an insurance company can conduct insurance business in a state, it must obtain a certificate of authority or license from the state insurance department
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
Recall that the two primary types of insurance regulations are solvency regulations and market conduct regulations and that a state’s Insurance Department is responsible for enforcement. Decide whether each Insurance Department action would be taken to enforce solvency or market conduct regulations.

Visit insurance companies to ensure that their nonfinancial operations are in compliance with regulations

Solvency Regulation

Market Conduct Regulation

Answer: Market Conduct Regulation

Demand regular financial reports from insurers

Solvency Regulation

Market Conduct Regulation

Answer: Solvency Regulation

Approve an insurer’s advertising materials before they start using them

Solvency Regulation

Market Conduct Regulation

Answer: Market Conduct Regulation

Review policy forms that insurers use to sell insurance products

Solvency Regulation

Market Conduct Regulation

Answer: Market Conduct Regulation

Conduct on-site financial condition examinations every three-to-five years

Solvency Regulation

Market Conduct Regulation

Answer: Solvency Regulation
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
To ensure compliance with market conduct laws, state regulators look at insurers’ nonfinancial operations. State regulators perform market conduct examinations of companies’ operations to ensure compliance with laws.

Advertising materials are reviewed by state regulators. They also regulate the policy forms that insurers may use to sell insurance products within the state. An insurer must file a copy of each policy form it plans to use with the state insurance department

To ensure compliance with solvency legislation, state regulators conduct formal investigations of an insurer’s financial condition at least every three to five years.

Regulators also require that insurers file reports each year illustrating that the insurer has met all financial requirements imposed by state laws.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
Let’s see how much you know about variable life insurance and variable annuities. Select each True statement about variable life insurance and variable annuities. (Choose all that apply.)

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulates variable life insurance and variable annuities.

The insurer, not the owner, assumes most, if not all, of the investment risk for a variable product.

In addition to a state license to sell insurance, individuals who sell variable insurance products must be licensed appropriately according to the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) guidelines.

Answer:

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) regulates variable life insurance and variable annuities.

In addition to a state license to sell insurance, individuals who sell variable insurance products must be licensed appropriately according to the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA) guidelines

Explanation:

Variable life insurance and variable annuities are considered to be investment products and so are regulated by the SEC. The owner, not the insurer, assumes most, if not all, of the investment risk for a variable product. Individuals selling variable insurance products must obtain both a state insurance license and a FINRA license to sell investment securities.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
Even federal laws that aren’t designed to regulate insurance directly affect the insurance industry. Here are some examples.

Consumer Protection Laws Consumer protection laws prohibit insurers from engaging in unfair competition or unfair or deceptive business practices, primarily related to interstate advertising.

Privacy Laws Privacy laws affect how insurers collect and use personal information about their customers.

Income Tax Laws Income tax laws encourage people to invest and employers to provide retirement savings plans and greatly impact the design of insurance products.

Antiterrorism Laws (Luật chống khủng bố) Antiterrorism laws require insurers to take steps to detect and prevent illegal activities used to finance terrorism

Corporate Governance Laws (Luật Quản trị Doanh nghiệp) Corporate governance laws impose certain requirements (áp đặt các yêu cầu nhất định) on corporations regarding organization, accountability, and disclosure. One such law, the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002, protects investors and other stakeholders by improving the accuracy and reliability of corporate disclosures made in accordance with securities laws.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
In each state in the United States, an organization that protects policyowners, insureds, beneficiaries, annuitants (người nhận niên kim), payees, and assignees (người được chuyển nhượng) against losses that might result from the impairment (sự suy giảm năng lực tài chính) or insolvency (mất thanh toán) of a life or health insurer that does business in the state. Insurers are required to participate in a state’s guaranty association in order to be licensed in that state.

A life and health insurance guaranty association is comprised (bảo gồm) of life and health insurers operating in that state. The association’s goal is to provide policyowners and beneficiaries with protection against the possibility of insurers becoming insolvent.

If an insurance company operating in a state becomes insolvent, that state’s guaranty association covers the company’s benefit payments up to state-mandated maximums. Most states guarantee payment of up to $300,000 in benefits.

Let’s review

In the United States, the solvency of insurance companies is primarily the responsibility of

State governments

The federal government

The NAIC

The SEC

Answer: The NAIC

Explanation: Each state enforces solvency laws and monitors the solvency of insurers operating there. The NAIC helps develop and promote insurance laws that are uniform from state to state. The SEC enforces laws on investments.

Because of the McCarran-Ferguson Act, the U.S. federal government plays no role in regulating insurance.

True

False

Answer: False

Explanation: Although McCarran-Ferguson allows states to be the primary regulators (cơ quan quản lý chính) of insurance, the federal government has the right to step in should it find that state legislation is inadequate. The newly created Federal Insurance Office is charged with monitoring the insurance industry to identify areas of inadequate state regulation. Federal securities laws also regulate variable insurance. And insurers are subject to federal unfair competition (cạnh tranh không lành mạnh), consumer privacy, (quyền riêng tư người tiêu dùng) antiterrorism (chống khủng bố), and other general business regulations.

The Wellborn Insurance Company is headquartered in State A and does business in States A, B, and C. Wellborn sells variable insurance and variable annuities.

State A’s insurance department issued Wellborn a certificate of authority to conduct business.

True

False

Answer: True

Explanation: Before conducting insurance business in a state, an insurance company must obtain a certificate of authority or license from the state insurance department.

State A’s insurance department regularly examines Wellborn’s financial statements.

True

False

Answer: True

Explanation: State A’s regulators visit Wellborn every three to five years to inspect its financial records. State A also conducts market conduct examinations at Wellborn.

Wellborn must only file a copy of its policy forms in State A.

True

False

Answer: False

Explanation: Insurers must file a copy of each policy form it plans to use with the state insurance department in each state in which it plans to operate

Wellborn is not subject to any federal regulation.

True

False

Answer: False

Explanation: The federal SEC oversees the sale of investment products, including variable insurance and annuities. Wellborn is also subject to numerous federal laws that affect all corporations.
source: LOMA281_M1L2_Knowledge File_Organization and Regulation of Insurance Companies.docx

text:
After studying this lesson, you will be able to:

Define a contract and identify the parties to an insurance contract.

Differentiate among types of contracts.

Differentiate between valid (hợp đồng hợp lệ), void (hợp đồng vô hiệu), and voidable (hợp đồng có thể bị vô hiệu) contracts.

Identify the general requirements to create a valid contract.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Define a contract and identify the parties to an insurance contract.

### What do you think?
Do you think Simone and Able Life negotiated the terms of the policy when she applied? Do you think that she can negotiate the terms now that the company has delivered the policy?

She could negotiate the contract terms at the time of application.

She can negotiate the terms at the time of delivery.

Neither

Both

Answer: Neither

Explanation: As Simone is learning, insurance policies are contracts, but they’re different from most contracts you’re used to. One example is the ability of only one party to set the terms of the agreement. You’ll learn more about this and other characteristics of insurance contracts in this lesson.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
An insurance policy is a legally enforceable contract (có hiệu lực pháp lý) between a policyowner who applies for and owns the policy—Simone, in our example—and the insurer that issues the policy—Able Life Insurance Company.

By accepting this insurance contract, Simone agrees to pay a premium for the coverage (để được bảo vệ). Able Life promises to provide the coverage (quyền lợi bảo hiểm) as long as Simone keeps paying her renewal premiums (khoản phí bảo hiểm gia hạn định kỳ).
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
What’s the worst thing that will happen if Simone doesn’t keep her agreement to pay the premiums on her life insurance policy?

Able Life can sue her in a court of law for recovery of the missed premium payments

Simone’s insurance coverage will cease

Answer: Simone’s insurance coverage (quyền lợi bảo hiểm) will cease (chấm dứt)

Explanation: Simone is not legally obligated to pay premiums so she can’t be sued. However, if she does not pay her premiums, her policy will lapse (The termination of an insurance policy for nonpayment of premium.)

Can Able Life choose to cancel Simone’s life insurance policy?

Yes

No

Answer: Yes

Explanation: For a life insurance policy, the insurer is legally obligated (nghĩa vụ pháp lý) to provide coverage according to the policy’s terms, as long as the policyowner pays the premiums. It can’t just decide to discontinue the coverage.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Differentiate among types of contracts

Contracts come in a variety of types. Every state has a large body of law (hệ thống pháp luật đồ sộ) that governs the operation and effect of contracts (điều chỉnh cách thức hoạt động và hiệu lực của hợp đồng).

The specific rules of contract law that apply to a given contract vary by the type of contract.

Let’s look at some of the most common types.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
(Hợp đồng đơn phương và hợp đồng song phương)

Able Life can’t make Simone pay her premiums. But as long as she does pay, Able Life’s promise to pay the policy’s benefit is legally enforceable (có hiệu lực pháp lý).

Because only one party to the contract makes a promise that can be enforced in a court of law (được thực thi trước pháp luật), the insurance contract is a unilateral contract (hợp đồng đơn phương).

In a bilateral contract (hợp đồng song phương), both parties make legally enforceable promises (cả hai bên đều đưa ra những cam kết có hiệu lực pháp lý).

### Like-for-Like Exchange
A transaction where both parties trade items or services of equal value and similar nature.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
In a commutative contract (hợp đồng đối ứng), the parties agree to exchange specified items or services that are equal in value.

In an aleatory contract, one party exchanges something of value for the other party’s conditional promise.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Under an aleatory contract (hợp đồng ngẫu hiên), if the specified event occurs, one party may then receive something of greater value than that party gave.

If a policy lapses soon after purchase ...

The policyowner gets little or nothing while the insurer keeps premiums paid.

Under an aleatory contract, if the specified event occurs, one party may then receive something of greater value than that party gave.

If the insured dies soon after purchase ...

The insurer receives few premiums while the beneficiary receives the full death benefit.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Is a life insurance contract a commutative or aleatory contract?

Commutative

Aleatory

Could be either one

Explanation: A life insurance policy is an aleatory contract. The policyowner exchanges something of value (the premium) for the insurer’s conditional promise to pay benefits if the insured dies while the policy is in force.

### Bargaining Contracts
In a bargaining contract, both parties set the terms and conditions.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Unlike a bargaining contract, a contract of adhesion (hợp đồng gia nhập) is one in which one party sets the contract terms that the other party must accept or reject outright.

An insurance contract is not a bargaining contract because the policyowner has no say about the terms of the contract. Able Life prepares the contract and Simone must accept it or reject it without any bargaining between them. This makes it a contract of adhesion.

With a contract of adhesion, the courts usually interpret any ambiguities (tòa án thường giải thích bất kỳ sự mơ hồ nào) in the contract against the party that drafted the contract.

For example, if a provision (điều khoản) in Simone’s life insurance policy is ambiguous, a court would likely interpret the ambiguous policy provision against Able Life and in favor of Simone, or her beneficiary.

### Policy Language in Contracts of Adhesion
Life insurance contracts have easily readable language and problems that can crop up by having easily readable language.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Informal contract: A contract that is enforceable because the parties to the contract met requirements concerning (đáp ứng) the substance (nội dung) of the agreement rather than requirements concerning the form of the agreement (hình thức của thỏa thuận)

Formal contract: A contract that is enforceable because the parties met certain formalities concerning the form of the agreement.

A formal contract requires the parties to meet certain formalities, such as creating the contract using legal counsel or having the contract validated by a notary public. A real estate sales contract is a formal contract.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Is an insurance contract an informal contract?

Yes

No

Answer: Yes

Explanation: Most contracts today, including insurance contracts, are informal contracts. They depend on substance rather than form and can exist without the aid or validation of a lawyer. Even though informal contracts can be oral or written, insurers always provide the policyowner with a written insurance policy. It’s a good business practice, and some states require it.

Select the characteristics of an insurance contract. (Choose all that apply.)

Commutative contract

Aleatory contract

Bargaining contract

Contract of adhesion

Informal contract

Formal contract

Unilateral contract

Bilateral contract

Answer: Aleatory contract, Contract of adhesion, Informal contract, Unilateral contract

Explanation:

Commutative contract: The parties to the contract exchange items or services that are relatively equal in value.

Aleatory contract: One party provides something of value to another in exchange for a conditional promise.

Bargaining contract: Both parties, as equals, set the terms and conditions of the contract.

Contract of adhesion: One party prepares the contract for the other party to accept or reject as a whole, without any bargaining.

Informal contract: Enforceable because the parties to the contract met the requirements concerning the substance of the agreement.

Formal contract: Enforceable because the parties to the contract met requirements concerning the form of the contract itself

Unilateral contract: Only one party’s promise is legally enforceable.

Bilateral contract: Both parties make legally enforceable promises.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Whether Simone’s rights under her life insurance policy and Able Life’s obligations under the policy are enforceable at law depend on whether the contract was issued according to certain principles of contract law and is a valid contract.

A contract is valid if it satisfies all legal requirements. A contract that does not meet one or more of the legal requirements to create a valid contract is void. A voidable contract is a contract under which one party has the right to avoid his obligations under the contract. The other party, however, is bound by the terms of the contract.

valid contract: A contract that is enforceable at law because it satisfies all legal requirements.

Void: Something that was never valid. A void contract is one that was never enforceable at law.

voidable contract: A contract in which a party has the right to avoid obligations under the contract without incurring legal liability.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Decide whether the sentences below describe a valid, void, or voidable contract.

A contract in which a party has the right to reject her obligations under the contract.

Valid

Void

Voidable

Answer: Voidable

A contract that satisfies all the legal requirements and is enforceable by law.

Valid

Void

Voidable

Answer: Valid

A contract that does not meet one or more of the legal requirements to create a legally enforceable contract.

Valid

Void

Voidable

Answer: Void
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Identify the general requirements to create a valid contract.

### Valid Contract Requirements
According to the laws in most of the United States and many other countries, contracts are valid and enforceable at law only if they meet certain requirements. Click or touch the arrows below to learn about these requirements.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
(Sự thỏa thuận lẫn nhau)

The parties must reach a “meeting of the minds” (thỏa thuận về ý chí) about the terms of the contract, which means that both, or all, of the parties to the contract have an understanding of the terms and agree to those terms.

The parties reach a meeting of the minds about the terms of a contract when

One party makes an offer and the other party accepts that offer

All parties express intent to be bound by the terms of the contract
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
(Giá trị trao đổi hợp pháp)

The parties to the contract must exchange consideration (trao đổi giá trị), which means that each party must give or promise something that is of value to the other party.

The rules of contract law don’t require the parties to exchange equal consideration. Whatever is of value to the parties generally is considered to be legally adequate consideration.

Applicant's Consideration

If the applicant had not paid the initial premium, she would not have provided consideration (giá trị trao đổi)  and no contract would exist.

Renewal premiums (phí bảo hiểm tái tục) payable to keep the policy in force are not consideration for the formation of the contract but a condition necessary for the continuation of the contract.

The applicant’s consideration is completing the insurance application and paying the initial premium while the insurer’s consideration is a promise to pay policy benefits if the conditions stated in the policy occur.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
A contract will NOT be valid if it was

Made for a purpose that is illegal

Against the public interest (đi ngược lại lợi ích cộng đồng)

An agreement between two parties to commit a criminal act (hành vi phạm tội), such as an agreement to kill an individual in exchange for money, is not legally enforceable. A contract can’t require a party to the contract to break the law.

Recall that life insurance policies might be considered unlawful wagering agreements (thỏa thuận đánh cược trái pháp luật) if not for the insurable interest(lợi ích được bảo hiểm) requirement.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Insurable interest (quyền lợi bảo hiểm) (mối quan hệ hợp pháp giữa người mua bảo hiểm hoặc gười thụ hường với người được bảo hiểm) is the interest that an insurance policyowner or beneficiary has in the risk that is insured. A policyowner or beneficiary has an insurable interest if he is likely to suffer a genuine loss (tổn thất thực sử) or detriment (thiệt hại)if the event insured against occurs.

Laws in most jurisdictions (khu vực pháp lý) require insurable interest to be present at policy issue to prevent people from buying insurance as a wager, hoping to profit when they have no risk of financial loss.

People generally have more to gain by living than by dying, so insurers presume (mặc định rằng) people to have an insurable interest in their own lives.

However...

A policyowner who purchases insurance on someone else's life must have an insurable interest in the insured’s life when the policy is issued ...

Insurable Interest - Beneficiary

And...

Most insurance companies require the beneficiary to have an insurable interest in the insured’s life when the policy is issued.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Laws in many jurisdictions require the applicant for a third-party life insurance policy to have an insurable interest in the proposed insured's life. An insurable interest is presumed to exist between certain family members.

Juan and his family are listed on the family tree. Choose each of Juan's relatives who would likely satisfy automatically an insurable interest requirement and be able to obtain an insurance policy on his life.

Juan's parents

Juan's spouse

Juan's children

Juan's uncles and aunts

Juan's cousin

Juan's brother and sister

Juan's sister-in-law

Juan's nieces and nephews

Juan's grandparents

Answer: Juan's parents, Juan's spouse, Juan's children, Juan's brother and sister, Juan's grandparents

Explanation: Spouses, children, parents, siblings, grandparents, and grandchildren automatically satisfy the requirement. Other relatives do not.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
The bonds of affection (mối quan hệ tình cảm) and financial dependence between certain family members are assumed by law to create an insurable interest between an insured and a policyowner or beneficiary.

Insurable interest is assumed for the insured’s spouse, child, parent, grandparent, grandchild, and sibling,

No insurable interest is assumed for insured’s aunt, uncle, cousin, niece, nephew, and more distant relatives.

Let’s look again at a family tree to see who has an insurable interest based only on family relationship.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
When a policyowner or beneficiary is a step relative (stepfather, stepdaughter, etc.), distant relative, business partner, or friend, the bonds of affection and financial dependence may not be so clear.

The insurer investigates these relationships on a case-by-case basis to determine insurable interest. If it exists, the insurer can issue the policy.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Suppose Simone’s business partner, Kathleen, applied for insurance coverage on Simone’s life, and named herself as the beneficiary. Do you think an insurable interest is present here?

Yes

No

Can’t tell. Need more information

Answer: Yes

Explanation: Because of their business relationship, Kathleen has a financial interest in Simone’s continued contribution to their business. Most likely, this business relationship satisfies the insurable interest requirement.

Suppose the delivery man applied for coverage on Simone’s life. Is an insurable interest present here?

Yes

No

Can’t tell. Need more information

Answer: Yes

Explanation: The insurer would probably decide that Simone’s delivery man does NOT have an insurable interest in Simone’s life, because there is no business relationship, financial dependence, or assumed bond of affection between them.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text: Insurable interest has to exist only when the policy is issued. In most cases, the ex-spouse beneficiary will be able to collect policy benefits upon the death of the former spouse. She will not have to demonstrate an insurable interest to collect those benefits.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Insurable interest is a requirement for forming a valid life insurance contract. For a third-party policy, most insurers apply the insurable interest requirements to

The policyowner only

The beneficiary only

Both the policyowner and the beneficiary

Neither the policyowner nor the beneficiary

Answer: Both the policyowner and the beneficiary

Explanation: Most insurance company underwriting guidelines require both the policyowner and the beneficiary of a third-party policy to have an insurable interest in the insured's life.

Andrew Kaczicki and Graham Young were business partners. Andrew applied for a life insurance policy on Graham’s life and named himself beneficiary. The insurance company determined that their business relationship satisfied the insurable interest requirement and issued Andrew the policy. Three years later, they dissolved the company and the partnership. Two years after that, Graham died while the policy was still in force. Andrew was still the named beneficiary.

In this situation, do you think Andrew was able to collect the life insurance policy benefit?

No, because Andrew no longer had an insurable interest in Graham’s life

Yes, because an insurable interest need only exist at policy issue

Answer: Yes, because an insurable interest need only exist at policy issue

Explanation: Insurable interest must exist only at the time of policy issue. When Graham died, whether Andrew still had an insurable interest was irrelevant..
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Remember that the requirements for a valid contract are:

Mutual assent

Legally adequate consideration

Lawful purpose

Contractual capacity (năng lực giao kết hợp đồng)

Now that we’ve described the first three requirements, let’s see how the parties to an insurance contract satisfy the contractual capacity requirement.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text: An insurance company acquires the contractual capacity to issue an insurance policy by being licensed or authorized to do business by the proper regulatory authority. A company that is not licensed or authorized does not have the legal capacity to make an insurance contract.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
The law generally presumes that individuals who apply for insurance have the contractual capacity to enter into a contract. However, a lack of full contractual capacity is assumed when those individuals

Are minors (chưa đủ tuổi vị thành niên)

Lack mental capacity (Thiếu khả năng nhận thức)
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Minors are people who have not reached the legal age of adulthood—18 in most jurisdictions. Minors typically do not have full contractual capacity.

If a minor is issued an insurance policy, the policy is voidable by the minor, which means that the minor can later decide to disaffirm (hủy bỏ) the policy, and the insurer will have to return the premiums the minor paid for the policy.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
People who lack mental capacity (for example, declared insane (mất trí) or mentally incompetent (không đủ năng lực hành vi dân sự) by a court of law) or whose mental capacity is impaired (for example, intoxicated (bị ảnh hưởng bởi chất kích thích như rượu hoặc ma túy) or mentally ill (bệnh tâm thần)) typically do not have full contractual capacity.

A person without mental capacity cannot enter into a valid insurance contract. Thus, any such contract would be void. A person with impaired mental capacity is like a minor in that an impaired person who later regains competency (khôi phục năng lự) may be able to avoid the insurance contract and receive a return of premiums.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
Based on the description, who has full capacity to enter into a valid insurance contract? (Choose all that apply.)

Raghib Idubu, a 40-year-old college-educated commercial pilot

Gladys Latfi, an 81-year-old retired schoolteacher in strong mental and physical health

Gabby Stephens, a 15-year-old high school sophomore

Enrique Vega, a 32-year-old welder with an 8th grade education

Lola Fugisi, a 50-year-old patient in a mental health institution who has been declared mentally incompetent

Answer:

Raghib Idubu, a 40-year-old college-educated commercial pilot

Gladys Latfi, an 81-year-old retired schoolteacher in strong mental and physical health

Enrique Vega, a 32-year-old welder with an 8th grade education

Explanation: Gabby Stephens (a minor) and Lola Fugisi (who has been declared mentally incompetent) are the only people above who do not have full contractual capacity.

Let’s say that your 12-year-old daughter signed a contract for a life insurance policy, and 12 is below the age at which the state allows people to purchase insurance on their own lives.

Do you think the contract would be valid, void, or voidable?

Valid

Void

Voidable

Answer: Voidable

Explanation: The insurer cannot avoid the contract because it sold your daughter the policy even though she was below the legal age. But your daughter can choose to avoid the contract, and the insurer will have to return any premiums she paid. This policy is voidable by her.

When Charles Antonelli purchased an insurance policy on his life, he named his wife Francesca as the beneficiary. Charles and Francesca divorced several years later, but Charles did not change the policy beneficiary designation (người thụ hưởng chỉ định). After Charles’ death, Francesca filed a claim for the policy proceeds.

In this situation, the insurance company most likely would find that the policy was

Valid

Void

Voidable

Answer: Valid
