Explanation: Because Francesca had an insurable interest in Charles’ life at the time of purchase, the policy is valid. Whether she still had an insurable interest when he died is irrelevant, and she could receive the policy benefit.
source: LOMA281_M1L3_Knowledge File_Life Insurance Policies as Contracts.docx

text:
After studying this lesson, you will be able to:

Explain the role of actuaries (chuyên gia định phí) in setting premium rates.

Define a premium rate, and calculate the annual premium amount for a given life insurance policy.

Explain how actuaries account for the cost of benefits (chi phí quyền lợi), investment earnings (thu nhập đầu tư) (, and expenses (chi phí hoạt động) in developing premium rates.

Explain how insurers use mortality tables (bảng tử vong) for pricing products, and describe the effect that mortality rates have on the cost of benefits and the premium rate for a block of policies (nhóm hợp đồng).

Describe the effect of compound interest on investment earnings, and calculate the amount of interest earned on a given sum of money.

Describe how pricing factors (yếu tố định giá) affect profit levels.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Explain the role of actuaries in setting premium rates.

Define a premium rate, and calculate the annual premium amount for a given life insurance policy.

### What do you think?
Simone’s $500,000 life insurance policy has an annual premium of $1,056. Although Simone is relieved to know her family would be financially okay if she should die prematurely, she has begun to question the cost of the policy. If she asks John how the insurer determined that $1,056 premium amount, which of the following do you think he might talk about with her? (Choose all that apply.)

Investment earnings

Cost of benefits

Profit

Operating expenses

Answer: All is correct

Explanation: All of these factors play a part in determining the premium for a life insurance policy, and we’ll look at each of these factors in this lesson.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Recall, that to have a valid contract, the parties must exchange consideration or something of value. In exchange for paying premiums, Simone transferred the risk of financial loss to Able Life and gained peace of mind that her family would be financially okay if she should die prematurely.

In exchange for accepting this risk from Simone, Able Life will receive premiums from Simone, which it needs to continue to operate as a business. That's the value exchange.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Actuaries specialize in calculating the financial impact of risk and uncertainty. They rely on the mathematics of insurance to determine how much insurers should charge customers so that the company can

Pay all future benefits

Cover operating expenses

Make a reasonable profit
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
See as this actuary explains how he decided to become an actuary and the various roles actuaries play in a life insurance company.

Anthony Ferraro

Vice President and Actuary

New York Life:

When I first went into the field of becoming an actuary, I wasn't exactly sure what an actuary did and a friend of mine, he was a math major and he went into actuarial science. I was actually working as an accountant initially and we had conversation. He said "Yeah, I'm an actuary" and I said "Well, what exactly do you do?" He explained to me that actuaries look at risks within the company. We look at contingent events and I thought, "Wow, I'm an accountant right now and I'm counting all the beans that I can see, it might be kind of fun to count these beans in the future that I'll never see, or may not ever see."

I decided to look into it. When you start researching a profession, you unveil what it actually is and what it's about and the mystery gets taken away from it. Actuaries really play in a lot of different roles in the company.

They run the gamut from being in the business and looking at strategy to being very technical and looking at valuations and exactly how much reserve should we hold, to doing a little bit of both, which is what the pricing actuary tends to do. Gets involved very much in the business strategy and what markets are we going after. What needs do I have to, how do I price this so that it meets the market need?

Then there's some very technical details in the background to actually coming up with the profitability and the assumption setting. It's a nice balance and I've enjoyed doing that, having that balance throughout my career.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Actuaries don’t set the exact premium amount that each policyowner pays. Instead, for each product, actuaries establish a premium rate per unit of insurance coverage. Actuaries express a premium rate as a "rate per thousand per year.

1 unit = $1,000 of life insurance coverage

We know that actuaries don’t just make up premium rates at random. But… premium rates have to be

Adequate: (đầy đủ) The company will have enough money to pay policy benefits.

Equitable: (công bằng)  Each policyowner pays a premium that reflects the degree of risk he presents to the insurer
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Explain how actuaries account for the cost of benefits, investment earnings, and expenses in developing premium rates.

Explain how insurers use mortality tables for pricing products, and describe the effect that mortality rates have on the cost of benefits and the premium rate for a block of policies.

### Pricing Factors
To develop adequate and equitable premium rates, actuaries take into account the following factors:
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
The main benefit provided by a life insurance policy is the death benefit that is paid when an insured dies. Some life insurance policies also include a surrender benefit. If a life insurance policy contains a savings element, the policyowner may receive the accumulated value—the cash value—of the policy as a surrender benefit.

In addition, a life insurance policy or an annuity contract may include several other types of benefits that we will describe later in this course. Actuaries must determine the cost for all of the benefits provided by a policy.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
The cost of benefits equals all of the insurer’s potential benefit payments multiplied by the expected probability that each benefit will be payable.

Cost of benefits = (potential benefit #1 × probability of payment) + (potential benefit #2 × probability of payment) + …

Some products have only one benefit; other products may have many different benefits. Regardless of the number of benefits in a product, dealing in probabilities is where this stuff can get tricky for an insurer.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
To determine the cost of benefits for life insurance products, actuaries must make statistical projections about (choose all that apply)

How many insured people will die each year

Exactly which insured people will die each year

How many policyowners will lapse or surrender their policies

Answer:

How many insured people will die each year

How many policyowners will lapse or surrender their policies

Explanation: Actuaries don’t try to predict who will die each year…but they are interested in how many insured people will die each year, as well as how many will surrender their policies and how many policies will lapse for nonpayment of premiums. We’ll discuss lapses and surrenders more later.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
To have the funds needed to pay life insurance claims as they come due, and to determine the cost of benefits for a life insurance product, actuaries must estimate the number of deaths that will occur each year.

3 of 15 insureds die. Mortality rate = 20 percent.

The mortality rate is the incidence of death, by age, among a given group of people. To price a life insurance product, an insurer must be able to predict the mortality rate for a given group of insureds.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Mortality tables (A chart that displays the number of people in a large group who are likely to die at certain ages.) display mortality rates for a given group of people at each age.

How Does it Work?

This mortality table shows that out of 96,535 of women alive at age 35, 97 will die before age 36.

That’s 0.001, or 1 per thousand.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

subsection: Excerpt of Female Non-Smoker Mortality Table
text:
So if 1,000 women like Simone are insured for $100,000 each, Able Life can expect to pay $100,000 in death claims during the year. Able’s cost of benefits for this life insurance product would be $100.

$100,000 benefits paid ÷ 1,000 women = $100 premium per woman

Remember that the cost of benefits doesn’t include other company expenses or a reasonable profit for the company.

### Mortality Tables
When not restricted by regulations, insurers can choose to price equivalent life insurance policies at a lower rate for women than for men of the same age.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
In general, the higher the mortality rate for a group of insureds of the same age and sex, the [ higher / lower ] the cost of benefits and, as a result, the [ higher / lower ] the premium rate.

Higher/higher

Higher/lower

Lower/higher

Lower/lower

Answer: higher/higher

Explanation: For a group of insureds with the same risk class and the same age, the higher the mortality rate, the higher the cost of benefits and the higher the premium rate. Conversely, the lower the mortality rate, the lower the cost of benefits and premium rate.

Mortality rate, cost of benefits, and premium rate all move in the same direction.

As the mortality rate rises, so do the cost of benefits and the premium rate.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Some policyowners decide they no longer want or need coverage.

If a policy doesn’t have a cash value, a policyowner may simply choose to stop paying premiums and allow the policy to lapse.

If a policy has accumulated a cash value, the policyowner will surrender the policy to the insurer in exchange for the policy’s cash surrender value.

To account for policy terminations and to calculate the cost of surrender benefits, insurers project lapse rates.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Actuaries project the mortality rate and lapse rate for a given group of people expected to be insured under a product. This block of policies (nhóm các hợp đồng bảo hiểm - A group of policies issued to insureds who are all the same age, the same sex, and in the same risk classification) contains insureds who are all the same age, sex, and risk class.

Insurance companies decide which policies are included in a block of policies. For example, an insurer may classify all term life insurance policies issued to females age 35, who are nonsmokers and have no significant medical history, into one block of policies.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
For its new life insurance product, the Sequoia Insurance Company set an annual premium rate of $2.50 per $1,000 of coverage for 30-year-old females in the preferred risk class. Amanda Bird, who falls into this class, applied for a policy with a face amount of $100,000.

What will Amanda’s premium amount be for the year (not including miscellaneous charges)?

$40

$100

$250

$400

Answer: $250

Explanation: With a $100,000 face amount, Amanda’s policy has 100 coverage units ($100,000 ÷ $1,000). Because Amanda will pay $2.50 for each coverage unit, her annual premium amount is $250 (100 units × $2.50).

Choose the term that you think provides the best answer.

Amanda can expect that the premium rate she pays for her coverage will be __________ the premium rate Sequoia charges other women who are her same age and in her same risk class.

Greater than

The same as

Less than

Answer: the same as

Explanation: Insurers apply the same premium rate to everyone in the same block of policies. This block of policies is made up of insureds who are all the same age, the same sex, and in the same risk class.

Choose the term that you think provides the best answer.

Sequoia’s actuaries projected that 10% of the people who are issued a policy will stop making premium payments before the end of Year 1, and before the insured person dies. This means that the_____________ rate for the first year is 10%.

Lapse

Mortality

Answer: Lapse

Explanation: The lapse rate represents the annual percentage of policies that don’t remain in force until the end of a given policy year because the policyowner stops paying premiums. The mortality rate reflects how many people in a group will die at each age.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Describe the effect of compound interest on investment earnings, and calculate the amount of interest earned on a given sum of money.

Describe how pricing factors affect profit levels.

### Investment Earnings
Most policies are in force for years or decades before claims are payable. Insurers invest a portion of these premiums to generate investment earnings.

Why? The earnings from these investments provide additional funds that allow insurers to charge lower premium rates than they could if they relied on premium amounts alone.

How important are earnings? In 2013, 24 percent of all income insurers earned came from investment earnings.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
When calculating premium rates, actuaries take into account the earnings the company’s investments will yield ( sinh lời. Consider the following situation:

Luau Insurance has two blocks of policies that are identical in every way except that the policies in Block A are likely to be in force for 10 years, whereas the Block B policies are likely to be in force for 25 years. All other factors being equal, how do you think projected investment earnings will affect premium rates for these two blocks?

The premium rate for Block A will be higher than the premium rate for Block B

The premium rate for Block B will be higher than the premium rate for Block A

Block A and Block B will have identical premium rates

Answer: The premium rate for Block A will be higher than the premium rate for Block B

Explanation: Typically, the longer a block of policies is in force, the more investment earnings will affect the premium rate calculations. Luau can potentially invest the premiums from policies in Block B for a longer time, earning more interest, and resulting in lower premium rates.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Many investments earn money in the form of interest payments. Interest is a payment for the use of money.

The rate of interest is expressed in terms of a percentage, such as 10 percent.

The principal (số tiền gốc) is the sum of money originally invested, loaned, or borrowed.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Simple interest is interest on the original principal only.

Consider an example of a simple-interest loan. In such a transaction, the lender is considered to be the investor.

Example

Luther Odom loaned Peggy Sims $1,000 for one year at an annual interest rate of 10 percent. A 10 percent interest rate on a loan indicates that the borrower must pay the lender the amount originally borrowed, plus an additional 10 percent of that amount.

Analysis

At the end of one year, Peggy owed Luther $1,100: the $1,000 Luther loaned Peggy originally plus $100 interest ($1,000 x 0.10 = $100).
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Until a loan is repaid, the interest on a compound-interest loan continues to accrue—or accumulate. In this situation, the investor usually earns interest on both the principal and the accrued interest.

Calculating an interest amount on both the principal and the accrued interest is called compounding.

Interest on both the principal and the accrued interest is called compound interest.

Example

Marcia Woodson loaned Bernard Carter $1,000 at an annual interest rate of 10 percent. Bernard did not repay any of the principal or interest on the loan for two years.

Analysis

At the end of the first year, Bernard owed Marcia $1,100, calculated as

$1,000 principal + ($1,000 principal x 0.10) = $1,100

At the end of the second year, Bernard owed Marcia $110 in interest, calculated as

$1,100 principal and accrued interest x 0.10 = $110

Thus, at the end of the second year, Bernard owed Marcia a total of $1,210, calculated as

$1,100 + $110 = $1,210

The interest in our compound-interest example was compounded annually. However, interest can be compounded with any frequency—quarterly, monthly, or daily, for example.

Although the additional $10 earned by compounding interest in the example may seem small, over a long period of time, compounding interest has a dramatic effect on the total amount of interest that is earned.

### Effect of Compounding
$1,000 held in an account for 25 years at 5% simple interest earns $1,250 simple interest.

$1,000 held in an account for 25 years at 5%, compounded annually, earns $2,386 compound interest.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
For the sake of simplicity, we illustrated investment earnings in terms of an interest rate earned on a loan and on a bank account. However, insurance companies can receive investment earnings from many other types of investments.

For example, insurance companies invest money by buying stock in other companies. While an insurance company owns the stock, the company may collect dividend payments on that stock.

In addition, the insurer might be able to sell the stock for more than it paid.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Insurers can’t just invest premiums to earn the highest possible return.

Simone depends on Able Life to remain in business and to pay promised benefits. She doesn’t want Able to take crazy risks with her money.

Regulators make insurers invest in safe investments that provide stable earnings.

State laws require insurers to establish policy reserves (Liabilities that represent the amount an insurer estimates it needs to pay future benefits. ) to pay projected future policy benefits when they come due. Reserve requirements affect how an insurer manages its investments.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
The premium amounts insurers charge must be adequate to pay for their expenses including:

Taxes, licenses, and fees

Salaries and commissions

Costs associated with developing new products

Costs associated with operating and maintaining the home office, regional offices, and/or sales offices

For some products, insurers account for operating expenses in the overall premium they charge for coverage.

Other products include separate charges to cover operating expenses.

### Life Insurance Company Expenses
This pie chart shows the percentage of insurer’s expenditures that went to pay benefits compared to operating and other expenses in 2017.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
So far we’ve covered actuaries’ first two pricing goals:

Pay future benefits to customers

Cover the cost of administering the products

The third goal is just as important if a company wishes to remain in business:

Make a reasonable profit
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Like all corporations, insurers attempt to generate a profit by producing goods and services and then selling them for more than what it costs to produce and deliver them.

Insurers have a responsibility to their owners and other stakeholders to operate profitably.

It’s imperative for actuaries to price products that make money because

Any business that doesn’t make a profit won’t be around for long.

The failure of an insurance company is terrible news for policyowners, beneficiaries, and the entire industry.

Consistent profit improves an insurer’s financial strength and stability.
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
What conditions can improve the financial results for a product?

Consider each factor below that can impact the profit on a block of life insurance policies. Choose the factors that illustrate conditions that would tend to increase profit from the level the actuaries assumed when they priced the block of policies. (Choose all that apply.)

Lower than anticipated mortality experience means fewer people die than are expected to, so the company pays less in death claims.

Higher than anticipated investment earnings means the company earns more than expected through investments, leading to additional profit for the company.

Lower than anticipated expenses means the company pays less to operate than it expected to.

Answer: All is correct

Explanatio:

Lower than anticipated mortality experience means fewer people die than are expected to, so the company pays less in death claims

Higher than anticipated investment earnings means the company earns more than expected through investments, leading to additional profit for the company

Lower than anticipated expenses means the company pays less to operate than it expected to
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
See as this company actuary explains the role of actuaries in setting premium rates, profit margins, and those key elements, mortality, expenses, investment income.

Anthony Ferraro

Vice President and Actuary

New York Life

There are many elements to pricing a product and setting premium rates. For example, one, if they're building a life insurance product, you must understand how the mortality might develop over time. You have to understand the premiums that are paid. How we might invest those premiums. And another aspect that the actuaries get involved with are the expenses. So we have to cover our overhead expenses and we really have to also understand the variable expenses involved in pricing a product. So the key areas that the actuaries always turn to is in setting the premium rates, the profit margins, the target and those key elements, mortality, expenses, investment income. And then... even just as importantly, there is policyholder behavior. And this is where judgment may come in. When you sell a product, you don't know how people are really going to react so you need to make... have some assumptions about that. How many people will surrender their policies. How many people might take loans against the policies. And is the product designed appropriately to handle those contingent events.

The greater the company’s financial strength, the more stable the company’s future. The more stable the company’s future, the greater the chances that the company will be able to make good on its promises to customers.

Greater strength = Stable financial future
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Premium rates vary according to factors such as the insured’s age, gender, smoking habits, and risk class. Underwriters evaluate applications and assign each proposed insured to a risk class.

Remember risk classes?

Standard—insureds who represent an average level of risk to the insurer

Preferred—insureds who represent a below-average level of risk

Substandard—insureds who represent an above-average level of risk

Declined—proposed insureds who represent a risk that is too great for the insurer to cover
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
Assume Able Life’s actuaries set the annual premium rate for a given life insurance product at $4.50 per $1,000 of coverage, for a 35-year-old non-smoker female in the preferred risk class. Also assume that Simone, who is a 35-year-old, non-smoker female in the preferred risk class, applied for a policy from this product line with a $200,000 face amount.

Determine the annual premium amount Simone will have to pay according to this rate schedule.

$450

$900

$1800

Answer: $900

Explanation:

Step 1: Calculate the number of coverage units for the policy.Simone’s policy has 200 coverage units ($200,000 face amount ÷ $1,000 per unit = 200 units).

Step 2: Multiply the price per coverage unit by the number of coverage units.Simone’s premium amount is $900 per year ($4.50 per unit × 200 units = $900).
source: LOMA281_M1L4_Knowledge File_The Value Exchange in the Insurance Transaction.docx

text:
After studying this lesson, you will be able to:

Describe term life insurance (bảo hiểm tử kỳ) coverage (phạm vi bảo hiểm) and distinguish between level term (tử kỳ cố định), decreasing term (tử kỳ giảm dần), and increasing term (tử kỳ tăng dần) life insurance plans.

Describe the operation of a return of premium (ROP) term policy (cách hoạt động của bảo hiểm tử kỳ hoàn phí)

Explain how the renewal (gia hạn) and conversion (chuyển đổi) features included in some term life policies increase their value to customers.

Describe term life insurance coverage and distinguish between level term, decreasing term, and increasing term life insurance plans.

### Financial Needs Met by Life Insurance
(Nhu cầu tài chính đáp ứng bởi công ty bảo hiểm)

Assume that Carter has life insurance on his own life. Select the box beside each financial need that you think life insurance can meet.

Paying household expenses (utility bills, food, clothing, etc.)

Covering outstanding debts (mortgage and car loans, etc.)

Paying future outstanding medical, hospital, and funeral expenses

Providing financial support for the family

Funding a child’s education

Answer: All is correct

Explanation: Proceeds can help cover household expenses and expenses related to Carter’s death that aren’t covered by other insurance. Carter’s wife Bridget won’t have to sell assets from Carter’s estate to pay off his debts; she can also use the proceeds to pay any private school and/or college. Finally, life insurance proceeds can support the family until Bridget can find new sources of income or can adjust to a lower income.

Carter needs financial protection for his wife and children in case he should die unexpectedly. His wife Bridget also needs financial protection for Carter and the children.

Term life insurance (Life insurance that provides a death benefit only if the insured dies during the period specified in the policy.), which provides coverage for a specified period of time, is one product that could help meet Carter’s needs.

No. For starters, the policy term varies from policy to policy. Three examples:

Flight insurance that insures a passenger's life during one airplane trip

A life insurance policy that insures a person for 20 years

A life insurance policy that insures a person until she reaches age 65

Few policy terms are shorter than one year.

Level Term Insurance

In level term life insurance (Term life insurance that provides a policy benefit that remains the same over the term of the policy.), the policy benefit—and usually the premium—remain the same over the policy term. Level term life is the simplest form of term life insurance. It provides temporary life insurance protection on a limited budget, which makes it a good choice for many insureds. They can purchase large amounts of level term life for a relatively small premium. And because the face amount and the premiums usually remain the same throughout the policy term, there’s no guesswork with level term.

In decreasing term life insurance (Term life insurance that provides a policy benefit that decreases in amount over the term of coverage.), the policy benefit decreases at stated intervals, but the premium amount usually remains the same. Decreasing term life is popular for people who have a financial need that decreases in amount over a defined time.

A common form of decreasing term is mortgage insurance (A plan of decreasing term insurance designed to provide a benefit amount that corresponds to the decreasing amount owed on a mortgage loan. )—the decreasing benefit corresponds to the decreasing balance on a mortgage loan.

When Michael bought a house, he obtained a mortgage loan from the Archway Bank. He also bought a mortgage insurance policy from Able Life.

Is Archway Bank a party to Michael’s mortgage insurance contract with Able Life?

Yes

No

Answer: No

Explanation: The mortgage loan and the insurance contract are separate transactions. The insurer is not a party to the mortgage loan and the bank is not a party to the mortgage insurance policy.

Who can Michael name as the beneficiary of his mortgage insurance policy?

His wife only

Archway Bank only

His wife, Archway Bank, or someone else

Answer: His wife, Archway Bank, or someone else

Explanation: Michael can name the bank as the beneficiary of his mortgage insurance policy, but he’s not required to. Usually, policyowners name their spouse or a family member as beneficiary.

If Michael names his wife as the policy beneficiary, does she have to use the policy proceeds to repay the mortgage loan?

Yes

No

Answer: No

Explanation: She can use the proceeds to repay the mortgage loan, but she isn’t required to.

Other types of decreasing term life:

Credit life insurance - Pays the balance on relatively short-term loans (such as automobile, education, home equity, and personal loans). The policy benefit decreases as the loan balance does. Unlike mortgage insurance, credit life insurance proceeds are always payable to the lender or creditor.

Family income coverage - Pays a stated monthly income benefit amount to the beneficiary if the insured dies during the term of coverage. Monthly benefits continue until the end of the term specified when the coverage was purchased. The longer the insured lives, the shorter the monthly benefit period and the smaller the benefit amount.

Example:
Elisabeth Romero bought 10-year family income coverage that provides a $1,000 monthly income benefit payable to her husband. Elisabeth died six years after buying the family income coverage.

Analysis:
Since Elisabeth died six years after buying the coverage, the insurer will pay the monthly income benefit for the remaining four years of the family income coverage.

In increasing term life insurance, the policy benefit (and the premium) start at one amount and increase at stated intervals because some financial needs increase with time.

For example, if Carter’s son attends private high school, tuition would likely rise each year, and college expenses might be even higher. Increasing term life could help pay for his son’s education if Carter dies before the child is out of college. This type of policy would make sense if the term ended when Carter’s child is expected to finish school.

Decide whether the statements below describe increasing term insurance, level term insurance, or decreasing term insurance.

A 5-year term life insurance policy that offers a death benefit of $50,000 for the first year of the policy term, $40,000 for the second year, and so on. The benefit for the fifth year is $10,000.

Increasing term insurance

Level term insurance

Decreasing term insurance

Answer: Decreasing term insurance

A 5-year term life insurance policy that provides a $100,000 death benefit if the insured dies at any time during the 5-year policy term.

Increasing term insurance

Level term insurance

Decreasing term insurance

Answer: Level term insurance

A 5-year term life insurance policy that pays a $100,000 benefit during the policy's first year, a $105,000 benefit during the second year, and so on. The benefit during the fifth year is $120,000.

Increasing term insurance

Level term insurance

Decreasing term insurance

Answer: Increasing term insurance

Describe the operation of a return of premium (ROP) term policy.

Explain how the renewal and conversion features included in some term life policies increase their value to customers.

### Return of Premium (ROP) Insurance
Return of premium (ROP) term insurance is a type of term coverage that

Pays a death benefit if the insured dies within the policy term

Returns all premiums paid on the policy if the insured is still alive at the end of the policy term

The premiums for ROP term life coverage are usually higher than the premiums for comparable traditional term insurance.

See as this actuary explains return of premium (ROP) term insurance from the company's point of view.

When you're looking at return of premium type products, essentially the company is taking premiums and investing them and if there's an event that occurs, obviously they pay the death benefit out. But if there is no event at the end of the term period, the company is hoping that they've earned enough investment income that by returning the premium they've made a profit margin.

It's very attractive to a client who feels like "Okay, I got the protection and I get all my money back after 20 years." In a very low-interest rate environment that we're in today, these products are very difficult to price effectively but the concept is very, very popular still with many different market segments. So some carriers still keep it out there.

Now there have been certain rules and regulations that eliminate the ability to just pay out one set of cash values at the end of 20 years. So there's non-forfeiture regulations that don't allow that type of, you get zero for 19 years and then your 20th year, boom you get this windfall. So there's been regulations developed, once again as an actuary, you have to understand the new regulations that come up, that now will grade into that amount over time.

It's not such a tontine, it's made the prices go up a little bit more on these products, but in theory, people like to know that if they didn't use something, they didn't lose everything. So you see a lot of product development happening in that arena.

Renewable term insurance lets you continue the coverage for another policy term without presenting evidence of insurability—proof that the insured person continues to be an insurable risk. There are some limitations (caveats), though.

What do you think?

Suppose Carter buys a renewable term insurance policy. Do you think he can renew the policy as many times as he wants?

Yes

No

Can't tell. Need more information.

Answer: No

Explanation: As long as the policyowner pays the premiums due, a renewable term policy will automatically renew for another term—usually the same length as the original term. The policyowner can also renew for a shorter term. However, a term policy can be renewed only a maximum number of times or until the insured reaches a maximum age limit.

One advantage of renewable term insurance is that the policy is still renewable even if the insured’s health has declined. The insured doesn’t have to show evidence of insurability—proof that he is still insurable—to renew the policy.

Assume Blythe Owens owns a $100,000 20-year renewable term policy that she bought when she was age 35.

At age 55, she can automatically renew the policy for another 20-year term, even if she has developed health problems that might otherwise prevent her from getting coverage.

Suppose Blythe renews her $100,000 20-year renewable policy at the end of the policy term. Do you think the amount of coverage is automatically cut in half to $50,000?

Yes

No

Can't tell. Need more information.

Answer: No

Explanation: The amount of coverage typically remains the same after policy renewal. So if the original 20-year term policy had a face amount of $100,000, the renewed 20-year term policy will also have a face amount of $100,000. Blythe can choose to renew the policy for a smaller amount, but not for a larger amount.

However, Blythe will have to pay a higher premium for the renewed policy than she was paying for the original policy. The renewal premium is based on the insured’s attained age at the time of renewal, rather than the age of the insured when the original policy was purchased.

Convertible term insurance (Term life insurance that gives the policyowner the right to convert the term policy to a cash value life insurance policy without providing evidence of insurability.

) lets the policyowner convert, or change, a term life policy to a different type of policy—a cash value policy that provides the insured with lifetime coverage.

As with renewable term policies, convertible term policies have some limitations...

A conversion provision allows a policyowner to convert a term policy to a permanent policy during a specified conversion period, which is shorter than the duration of the term insurance coverage.

Once the conversion period expires, the policy is no longer convertible.

See as this industry expert explains how convertible term insurance can be marketed and how company representatives encourage policyowners to convert their term coverage to permanent coverage.

So often when people are looking to purchase their first life insurance policy they're looking to get the most death benefit for the smallest amount of premium, and term really fits that bill. But one of the things that we encourage people to consider is the ability to convert that term to a permanent product in the future. Something to look at when you're purchasing a term product is does it have a conversion feature, which will allow you to convert to a whole life product with that company without any underwriting. That can really be a nice benefit for later when you have additional dollars that you can put towards your financial security, and start building that cash value, and being able to take advantage of the flexibility that permanent whole life product may offer.

Often what we see is reps, the first product they offer is a term product, and then they will say, but we'll be coming back at some point in the future to talk about converting it. They set that up to start with. Another thing to consider too when you're looking at a term conversion is what are the restrictions if any that that company has?

Generally, any product that we have available is available to have a term conversion too with a few exceptions. Some companies may restrict that more. So again, just something to look at as you are considering where to purchase that term product.

The conversion period usually are not unlimited. There is usually an end date for that conversion period. It also provides a great marketing opportunity for our financial reps. They can say "Hey, this is your last chance to convert your term product to a permanent. It's now or never." We do have quite a few that come in right at the deadline.

Assume Blythe Owens purchased a convertible term insurance policy instead of a renewable term policy. During the conversion period, Blythe’s health declined to the point where she would no longer be considered insurable. Can Blythe convert her term policy to a cash value policy?

Yes

No

Can't tell. Need more information.

Just like with renewable term life insurance, the insured doesn’t have to demonstrate evidence of insurability to convert the term policy to a cash value policy, and the insurer can't increase the premium rate for risk factors relating to the insured’s health. Premium increases can be based only on increased mortality risk associated with age.

Insurers limit the amount of converted cash value coverage to a percentage of the original term policy's face amount.

Typically, the policyowner can convert the full face amount of term coverage to cash value coverage within the first few years following policy issue. However, as the end of the conversion period gets closer, the convertible amount often decreases.

Unlike premiums for renewed term coverage, premiums for converted policies aren’t always based on the insured’s attained age.

Convertible term policies allow for either an attained age conversion (A conversion of a term life insurance policy to a cash value life insurance policy in which the premium rate for the cash value policy is based on the insured's age at the time the policy is converted. Contrast with original age conversion) or an original age conversion (A conversion of a term life insurance policy to a cash value life insurance policy in which the premium rate for the cash value policy is based on the insured's age when the original term life insurance policy was issued. Contrast with attained age conversion.), which bases new premium rates on the insured’s age at the time the original policy was issued.

This is attractive to customers because premiums are lower when the insured is younger. Original age conversions do have some strings attached, though....

subsection: Attained Age vs. Original Age Conversion
text:
Example: Marcia and Todd, each age 30, purchased 30-year convertible term life insurance policies. Both of them decided to convert their term policies to cash value policies at age 40.

Marcia decided to convert her term policy on an attained age basis.

### Attained Age Conversion
The effective date of cash value coverage is the date of conversion.

Todd decided to convert his term policy on an original age basis.

The effective date of cash value coverage is the term policy purchase date.

The renewal and conversion privileges can lead to some antiselection; insureds in poor health will be more likely to renew or convert their policies because they may not be able to obtain other life insurance coverage.

To account for the risk of antiselection, insurers charge higher premiums for renewable or convertible term policies than for comparable term policies that aren’t renewable or convertible.

Identify how renewal affects a term life insurance policy by selecting the correct answer. Note that some features have more than one correct answer.

When a term life insurance policy is RENEWED, the amount of coverage under the policy can ...

increase

decrease

remain the same

Answer: decrease, remain the same

Explanation: Renewal coverage can be for the same face amount as the original policy or smaller, but not for a larger face amount.

When a term life insurance policy is RENEWED, the coverage period can ...

increase

decrease

remain the same

Answer: decrease, remain the same

Explanation: Renewal coverage term can be same length as the original policy or shorter, but not longer.

When a term life insurance policy is RENEWED, the premium rate (for the same amount of coverage) can ...

increase

decrease

remain the same

Answer: increase

Explanation: Renewal premium rate is based on the insured's attained age at renewal and so is higher than original premium rate.

When a term life insurance policy is RENEWED, the type of coverage ...

changes

remains the same

Answer: remains the same

Explanation: The type of coverage before and after renewal remains the same.

When a term life insurance policy is CONVERTED, the amount of coverage can ...

increase

decrease

remain the same

Answer: decrease, remains the same

Explanation: The insurer may allow conversion for the full amount of, or a percentage of, the original policy.

When a term life insurance policy is CONVERTED,the coverage period can ...

increase

decrease

remain the same

Answer: increase

Explanation: The coverage period generally increases because coverage is provided for the insured's lifetime, not a specified term.

When a term life insurance policy is CONVERTED, the premium rate can ...

increase

decrease

remain the same

Answer: increase

Explanation: The premium rate for a cash value policy is higher than for the original term policy because of the higher cost of lifetime coverage.

When a term life insurance policy is CONVERTED, the type of coverage ....

changes

remains the same

Answer: changes

Explanation: Conversion involves changing the coverage from temporary coverage to lifetime coverage.

After studying this lesson, you will be able to:

Describe cash value life insurance coverage.

Distinguish between forms of traditional whole life insurance based on premium payment frequency.

Describe the benefits that modified whole life(Bảo hiểm nhân thọ trọn đời điều chỉnh), joint whole life (Bảo hiểm nhân thọ trọn đời chung), and last survivor insurance (Bảo hiểm nhân thọ trọn đời người cuối cùng qua đời) provide policyowners.

Describe the features and benefits of universal life (Bảo hiểm nhân thọ linh hoạt), indexed universal life (Bảo hiểm nhân thọ linh hoạt liên kết chỉ số), variable life (Bảo hiểm nhân thọ biến đổi), and variable universal life insurance (Bảo hiểm nhân thọ linh hoạt biến đổi) products.

subsection: What is Cash Value Life Insurance?
text:
Describe cash value life insurance coverage.

### What is Cash Value Life Insurance?
Like all forms of life insurance, cash value life insurance pays a death benefit if the insured person dies while the policy is in force.

But cash value life insurance (Life insurance that provides insurance coverage throughout the insured's lifetime and provides a savings element, known as the cash value. Sometimes known as permanent life insurance.) has two important features term life doesn’t have:

Lifetime coverage

A savings element

|  | Cash Value Life Insurance | Term Life Insurance |
|---|---|---|
| Length of coverage | Insured's Lifetime | Specified term only |
| Savings element? | Yes, through the cash value | No |

Cash value life insurance protects for the insured’s entire lifetime, as long as the premiums are paid. The insured can't outlive the insurance protection—as long as the policy is in force, the coverage never needs to be renewed.

In addition to a death benefit, cash value life insurance has a savings element—known as a cash value—that the policyowner can access while the insured is still alive.

Just how big a policy's cash value is at any given time depends on the

Policy face amount

Length of time the policy has been in force

Length of the policy's premium payment period

subsection: Cash Value Life and Term Life
text:
See as this industry expert explains "blended" products--products that have characteristics of both term life insurance and cash value life insurance.

Anthony Ferraro
Vice President and Actuary
New York Life

Term insurance is insurance that is set for a certain term. So you can think of it as not being permanent insurance. Permanent insurance is something that continues for the whole life of a person. So you'll have... typically term insurance doesn't come with cash values and permanent insurance likely will have cash values built up over time. And so oftentimes, people will associate permanent insurance with cash value life insurance and term insurance with having no cash value but lasting for a certain term.

In more recent years, there's been a blend of these types of products and so you have permanent products that have very little or no cash value associated with them. And you have term products today that actually will return premium after a certain period of time and one could think of that as a cash value as upon surrender, you'll get back the money that you put into the product. So there's been a bit of a blurring of the lines between term insurance and cash value life insurance but the key aspect to... the key difference will always be that a term insurance product will not last the whole life of a person.

There's a lot of permanent plans out there that are now built without cash value, which honestly many companies worry about a little bit because you're really... at that point in time there's no reason for somebody to lapse and if you're very aggressive on your premium setting, there may be... you may really be in the hole. The company could really be at risk. And so there's a lot of... there's a lot of talk in the industry about these types of products but they're... they've been around for ten years or so and they're very, very popular and they have a good place in the market.

### Policy Reserve and Cash Value
Both the policy reserve and the policy’s cash value increase throughout the life of the policy. Eventually, they both equal the policy’s face amount.

The owner of a cash value insurance policy can borrow against the cash value by taking out a policy loan directly from the insurance company.

Policy loans reduce policy values because a policy loan is actually an advance payment of part of the amount that the insurer eventually must payout under the policy.

With a policy surrender, you get the cash from the policy’s entire cash surrender value, which is the cash value amount less any amounts the policyowner owes, such as outstanding policy loans and charges.

After the surrender, the coverage terminates and no further benefits are payable.

In the United States, a cash value policy offers certain tax advantages to the policyowner, who pays

NO current income tax on interest or other earnings credited to the policy’s cash value

NO income tax on funds borrowed from the cash value through a policy loan

A cash value life insurance policy provides insurance protection for the insured’s entire lifetime.

True

False

Answer: True

Explanation: As long as premiums are paid and the policy remains in force, a cash value policy provides insurance protection for the insured’s entire lifetime.

A cash value policy with a policy loan outstanding can’t be surrendered.

True

False

Answer: True

Explanation: A policyowner can surrender the policy. The cash surrender value will be reduced by the amount of any outstanding policy loans.

Now let’s take a look at some of the different types of cash value coverage.

Traditional whole life insurance

Is the most basic type of cash value life insurance

Provides a constant amount of lifetime coverage

Has a level premium rate that doesn’t increase with insured’s age

Traditional whole life comes in three types based on the number of premium payments:

Single-premium
Only one premium payment
Limited-payment
Premium payments spread out over a stated period of time, such as a specific number of years or until the insured reaches a specific age
Continuous-payment
Premiums payable until the insured’s death

In each case, coverage continues for the insured’s entire lifetime as long as premiums are paid and the policy remains in force.

Customers for cash value life insurance come in all shapes and sizes, too. Review each client’s situation and decide which type of whole life insurance best meets the client’s needs.

Arabella:

My name is Arabella Simpson and I am 45. I want life insurance that will last as long as I do. I plan to retire by 65 and I don’t have a big chunk of money to spend on insurance right now. I’m also a little worried about being able to pay insurance premiums when I retire since my retirement income is probably going to be less than I earn now.

Which type of whole life insurance policy will best be able to give Arabella lifetime protection without straining her retirement income?

Single-premium whole life policy

Limited-payment whole life policy

Continuous-premium whole life policy

That's correct!

With a limited-payment whole life policy, Arabella can make premium payments until she retires. After that, her coverage will continue, but she won't need to pay any more premiums.

Ethan:

I'm Ethan Franco. I'm 52. And I've done pretty well with my investments. I've got some money in the bank. I could use some life insurance that would last my whole life but I don't want a lifetime of paying premiums every month. I want more control than that. I also want something that will provide me with money I can use if a good opportunity comes up.

Which type of whole life insurance policy will provide Ethan with a sizeable cash value immediately?

Single-premium whole life policy

Limited-payment whole life policy

Continuous-premium whole life policy

That's correct!

Under a single-premium policy, the entire premium needed to provide lifetime coverage is paid up front. This creates a sizeable cash value immediately. The cash values for other whole life insurance policies may take 3 or more years to begin to build.

Kimiko:

My name is Kimiko Chong. I'm a single mom with two kids. I make a good living and want to provide long term security for my family but I'm the sole breadwinner. My salary is increasing every year but I've also got a mortgage and a car payment. My current income won't stretch too much further.

Which type of whole life insurance policy will provide Kimiko with the most insurance coverage for the most affordable premium?

Single-premium whole life policy

Limited-payment whole life policy

Continuous-premium whole life policy

That's correct!

A continuous-premium whole life policy will let Kimiko spread the premium payments over her entire lifetime. Each individual premium will be lower than it would be under any other premium payment schedule. This will keep whole life insurance coverage within Kimiko’s budget.

Traditional whole life insurance policies are great for people who want coverage amounts and premium amounts that don’t change.

But needs can and do change over time. That's where modified whole life insurance policies come in with two types of modified policies:

Modified-premium whole life insurance

Modified coverage whole life insurance

Carter is considering purchasing either a traditional whole life insurance policy or a modified-premium whole life insurance policy. If the initial annual premium for both policies is $2,000, which policy do you think will provide Carter with the most life insurance coverage?

The traditional whole life policy

The modified-premium whole life policy

Both policies will provide the same amount of coverage

That's correct!

Because premiums under a modified-premium policy are lower during the early policy years and higher later on, Carter can afford to buy a larger face amount of modified-premium coverage than traditional whole life. But, his policy’s cash value will grow more slowly than it would under a traditional whole life policy.

Under a continuous-premium policy, Carter’s annual premium of $2,000 would never change. Under a modified-premium whole life policy, however, the annual premium does change.

Under a modified-premium policy, Carter’s annual premium of $2,000 would increase to $2,500 after an initial period—say 10 years—and remain at $2,500 for as long as the policy remains in force.

How do you think the annual premium for Carter’s modified coverage policy would compare to the annual premium for a $250,000 traditional whole life policy?

The premium would be less for the modified coverage policy

The premium would be more for the modified coverage policy

The premium would likely be the same for both policies

That's correct!

Carter’s annual premium would be lower for a modified coverage policy than for a traditional whole life policy. The modified coverage policy provides the most coverage when his mortality risk is low, and the least coverage when his mortality risk is high.

A modified coverage whole life policy could be structured to provide Carter with three levels of coverage:

Click or touch each tab below to see how Carter's coverage changes over time.

Level 1: $250,000 until he reaches age 50

While his kids are young and his wife isn’t working, he’s going to need the most coverage.

Level 2: $200,000 until he reaches age 65

By then he likely will have paid off his debts and his children will be out on their own, so he’ll need less coverage.

Level 3: $150,000 for the remainder of his life

This amount will cover his family’s needs if something happens to him.

The premium remains the same throughout the life of the policy.

subsection: Joint Whole Life & Last Survivor Life Insurance
text:
Another option is for Carter and his wife to buy one policy that insures both their lives. Two possibilities are

Joint whole life insurance )A plan of whole life insurance that has the same features and benefits as individual whole life insurance, except that it insures two people under the same policy; the death benefit is payable when the first insured dies.), which pays a benefit after the FIRST insured dies

Last survivor life insurance (A variation of joint whole life insurance under which the policy benefit is paid only after both people insured by the policy have died.), which pays a benefit after BOTH insureds die

Let’s look at both of these options.

Joint Whole Life & Last Survivor Life

|  | Joint Whole Life | Last Survivor Life |
|---|---|---|
| Who is insured? | Two people under a single policy | Two or more people under a single policy |
| What are premiums based on? | Average age of insureds | Average age of insureds |
| When does it pay a death benefit? | When the FIRST insured dies | When the LAST insured dies |
| When do coverage and premiums end? | Coverage ends after the death of the first insured; Surviving insured has a specified period in which to buy individual whole life coverage without providing evidence of insurability | Premiums stop after the first insured's death or can continue until last insured dies and benefit is payable |
| Other tidbits | If both insureds die simultaneously, the insurer pays a death benefit for each insured |  |

Choose JOINT whole life if…

…The surviving spouse needs a larger death benefit to cover continued living expenses, but won’t need a large life insurance policy when he or she dies later on.

Choose LAST survivor life if…

…You have other funds or a separate policy to pay expenses when the first insured dies, but you still want to provide a means for your heirs to pay final estate settlement costs after the second insured dies.

Either way, one joint whole life or last survivor life policy will probably be less expensive than two individual whole life policies.

Choose the correct life insurance product by the description.

Most basic type of cash value policy; level death benefit, level premium rate

Modified coverage whole life insurance

Last survivor life insurance

Traditional whole life insurance

Joint whole life insurance

Modified premium whole life insurance

That's correct!

Smaller premium for an initial period, followed by a larger premium for the remainder of the insured’s lifetime; death benefit remains the same

Modified coverage whole life insurance

Last survivor life insurance

Traditional whole life insurance

Joint whole life insurance

Modified premium whole life insurance

That's correct!

Insures two lives with one policy, for one premium; death benefit payable upon the death of the first insured

Modified coverage whole life insurance

Last survivor life insurance

Traditional whole life insurance

Joint whole life insurance

Modified premium whole life insurance

That's correct!

The face amount of coverage changes at stated intervals; usually highest face amount in early policy years, and lowest face amount in later policy years. The premium amount remains the same.

Modified coverage whole life insurance

Last survivor life insurance

Traditional whole life insurance

Joint whole life insurance

Modified premium whole life insurance

That's correct!

Insures two or more lives with one policy, for one premium. The death benefit is payable upon death of the last insured.

Modified coverage whole life insurance

Last survivor life insurance

Traditional whole life insurance

Joint whole life insurance

Modified premium whole life insurance

That's correct!

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
subsection: Describe the features and benefits of universal life, indexed universal life, variable life, and variable universal life insurance products.
text:
The cash value policies we’ve talked about so far earn an interest rate that is specified in the policy.

Some policyowners may want lifetime insurance coverage but also an opportunity to earn more than just a stated interest rate.

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
text:
Universal life (UL) insurance (A form of cash value life insurance that is characterized by its flexible premiums, its flexible face amount and death benefit amount, and its separation of the three primary policy elements.) has

Flexible premium amount

Flexible face amount and death benefit

Separate pricing factors

Let’s look at each of these features.

Premium flexibility is one of the primary advantages of universal life. After the first premium payment, Ethan can choose (within limits)

When to make additional premium payments, and

How large the payments will be.

This feature can be important for someone whose ability to pay may fluctuate. Larger premium payments may also increase the policy’s cash value.

A form o

What happens if Ethan fails to pay a renewal premium for his UL policy?

The policy could remain in force

The policy will lapse immediately for nonpayment of premium

That's correct!

As long as the policy's cash value is large enough to pay the periodic mortality and expense charges, the policy will stay in force even if Ethan doesn't pay renewal premiums. This gives Ethan more control over the timing of his premium payments.

But Ethan must be careful…if his premium payments are too small for too long, the policy could lapse, leaving him without insurance protection!

Some UL policies offer a no-lapse guarantee.

As long as the policyowner pays a minimum premium amount for a specified period of time, the policy will stay in force to age 100 or 120.

But, paying the minimum required premium usually isn’t enough to build up significant cash values.

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
subsection: Why wouldn’t it qualify as life insurance?
text:
Section 7702 corridor

In the United States, the difference between the policy's death benefit and its cash value is known as the Section 7702 corridor.

If the relationship of the policy’s cash value to its death benefit exceeds the regulatory limits, then the policy is treated for tax purposes as an investment product rather than an insurance policy.

That’s why most insurers will automatically increase the face amount of a UL policy if the cash value exceeds the allowed percentage of the death benefit. This helps the UL policy continue to meet the legislative requirement, and the policy remains an insurance policy rather than an investment.

Or, Ethan can decide that he wants his death benefit to increase based on increases in the policy’s cash value. If the cash value grows over time, so does the death benefit. However, if the cash values decline, then so would the death benefit.

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
text:
Term life insurance and traditional whole life insurance state the total premium amount the policyowner must pay to keep the policy in force.

A UL policy lists the pricing factors separately. This helps customers better understand UL policies and compare policies from different insurers.

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
text:
Mortality charges. The insurer periodically deducts from the cash value an amount to cover the mortality risk the insurer assumed by issuing the policy.

Interest rate. The insurer invests a portion of each premium and credits interest from these investments to the policy’s cash value. The insurer guarantees a certain minimum return and credits a higher interest rate if the investments perform well.

Expenses and fees. The insurer deducts stated expenses and fees from the cash value. Examples of deductions include:

A percentage of each premium (such as 4%) to cover expenses

Specific charges for policy withdrawals (deducted from the cash value)

Insurer receives premium.

Insurer deducts some expense charges…and credits remainder to cash value.

Insurer deducts monthly mortality charge from cash value.

Insurer credits cash value with interest.

Insurer may deduct additional expenses, such as other charges, loans or withdrawals, from cash value.

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
text:
See as this industry expert explains the difference between universal life insurance and term life insurance.

Lincoln Demier
Product Design Manager
American Family Insurance:

Universal life differs from term life insurance in some fairly fundamental ways. With term life insurance, you have a fixed amount of years and a fixed premium that the product is available. With universal life, you have tremendous flexibility. You have the ability to increase the death benefit. You have the ability to decrease the death benefit. You have the ability to increase premiums; you have the ability to decrease premiums.

You can have a level death benefit. You can have an increasing death benefit. You can do a 1035 exchange of funds from inside a universal life policy into an annuity contract and carry the cost basis of the universal life over to the annuity which can provide for a very tax-advantaged stream of income during retirement.

So, the universal life policy shifts the interest rate risk to the consumer and with that flexibility we believe comes a tremendous responsibility to do annual reviews of the universal life policy to make sure that the assumptions that were made when the product was purchased are holding true on an annual basis. So, universal life insurance, tremendous flexibility, terrific product, but that also means responsibility for the consumer, for the company issuing the policy, and for the agent servicing the policy.

Companies and agents are counseling the consumer that purchased universal life hopefully on an annual or a biannual basis. Many of the universal life policies sold in this country were sold in a much higher interest rate environment, which means customers would either need to lower their death benefit or increase the amount of premium paid into the policy in order for those policies to last until maturity.

So it's incumbent upon organizations and agents to reach out to customers who own universal life policies and bought them in a higher interest rate environment to let them know that action probably needs to be taken. The status quo is not the same as when the policy was bought. So lacking any kind of proactive action on the part of the policyowner, the universal life policy could be subject to early lapse.

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
text:
Indexed universal life (IUL) insurance (A type of universal life insurance that offers certain principal value and earnings guarantees, but also offers the possibility of additional earnings by linking the cash value to a published index. Also called equity indexed universal life (EIUL) insurance.), which is also known as equity indexed universal life (EIUL),

Offers the same features of traditional universal life insurance

Allows the policyowner to tie the cash value of the policy to the performance of a stock or other published equity index, such as the Standard & Poor's 500 Composite Stock Price Index (the S&P 500)

An IUL guarantees that the cash value will earn at least a minimum interest rate.

If the index-linked interest rate is higher than the guaranteed rate, the excess earnings are credited to the account up to a specified maximum rate. The IUL specifies the method used to calculate and credit excess interest.

If the index-linked interest rate is lower than the guaranteed rate, earnings are based on the guaranteed minimum interest rate.

The IUL also guarantees that the policyowner’s principal is protected by the minimum guaranteed interest rate.

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
text:
Some types of insurance products transfer investment risk from the insurance company to the policyowner.

For traditional insurance products, the insurer invests the premium payments in its general account that contains relatively secure investments, such as bonds or government securities. The insurer guarantees that a policy’s cash value will earn a minimum interest rate regardless of the insurer’s investment experience.

But variable insurance products, such as variable life and variable universal life allow the policyowner to choose how the premiums are invested.

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
text:
Variable insurance policyowners can choose from among alternative pools of investments, called subaccounts (or variable subaccounts). Each subaccount has a distinct risk/return profile and investment strategy.

These subaccounts are part of the insurer’s separate account (segregated account), which is an investment account the insurer uses to help manage the funds placed in its variable products.

Let's see how subaccounts work.

The policyowner pays the premium.

The insurer invest the premiums into subaccounts.

Subaccount values fluctuate according to investment performance…

…and the policy face amount and cash value also fluctuate.

If the variable policyowner’s subaccount investments perform well, the policy's death benefit and cash value [increase / decrease]. If the subaccount’s investments perform poorly, the death benefit and cash value [increase / decrease].

increase / increase

increase / decrease

decrease / increase

decrease / decrease

That's correct!

The policyowner assumes most or all of the investment risks for a variable policy. If investment funds perform well, the death benefit and cash value will increase; but if the funds perform poorly, the death benefit and cash value will decrease.

The policyowner can change his subaccount selections at least once a year if he’s not satisfied with the results.

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
text:
Because the owner of a variable policy assumes the risks associated with the underlying investments, variable products sold in the United States are classified as securities.

As securities, variable products must comply with federal securities laws, which are enforced by the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
text:
The most basic type of variable product is variable life (VL) insurance, which has

Fixed premiums

Death benefit and other policy values that vary according to the performance of subaccounts chosen by the policyowner

Most VL policies guarantee

A death benefit at least equal to the principal amount

Most VL policies DO NOT guarantee

Investment earnings for variable subaccounts

A minimum cash value

New policies of variable life insurance are rare.

section: Other Types of Life Insurance Policies
text:
By far the most commonly sold variable insurance product is variable universal life insurance (VUL).

A VUL policy really puts the policyowner in the driver’s seat.

VUL blends the investment flexibility of variable life insurance with the premium and death benefit flexibility of universal life insurance.

In short, VUL has

Flexible premiums

Choices regarding underlying investment accounts

Flexible death benefit

Let’s see how it works…

Suppose Ethan purchases a VUL policy.

At first, the VUL has a specified death benefit and a minimum scheduled premium payment designed to meet the first year’s mortality and expense charges.

After that, Ethan can pay into the plan whatever he wants, as long as the cash value continues to cover the mortality and expense charges.

section: VUL’s Choice of Underlying Investment Accounts
text:
VUL gives Ethan control of the cash value portion of his policy.

When he pays the initial premium, he can choose from among several—often many—subaccounts within the insurer’s separate account.

He can also choose to put some of his premium into a fixed fund in the insurer’s general account. He can redirect the investments at least once each year, without charge, to take advantage of his perception of the market.

The VUL death benefit may rise or fall, depending on the success of the underlying investments Ethan chooses. If he dies when values are down, his VUL policy will still pay a minimum death benefit to his beneficiary.

But the VUL offers Ethan the opportunity to build up a significant cash value if the stock market performs well over the period his policy is in force.

Select the type of policy based on the description. Choose all that apply.

Flexible premiums

UL

IUL

VL

VUL

That's correct!

All three types of universal life policies have flexible premiums.

Submit

Fixed Premiums

UL

IUL

VL

VUL

That's correct!

Premiums for VL policies are fixed for the life of the policy.

Submit

Separate pricing factors

UL

IUL

VL

VUL

That's correct!

All three types of universal life policies list investment earnings, charges, and cost of insurance separately in the policy.

Submit

Policyowner assumes investment risk

UL

IUL

VL

VUL

That's correct!

VL and VUL policies are considered securities in the U.S. because the policyowner chooses investment funds and assumes the investment risks.

Submit

Death benefit can remain level or vary with changes to cash value

UL

IUL

VL

VUL

That's correct!

All three types of universal life policies offer two options for the death benefit:

•  The death benefit can remain level—equal to the policy’s face amount

•  The death benefit can vary with changes in the policy’s cash value

Submit

Death benefit varies according to investment performance

UL

IUL

VL

VUL

That's correct!

For both VL and VUL, if separate account investment funds perform well, the death benefit increases; if the funds perform poorly, the death benefit decreases.

f cash value life insurance that is characterized by its flexible premiums, its flexible face amount and death benefit amount, and its separation of the three primary policy elements.

After studying this lesson, you will be able to:

Define an annuity and describe the key players involved in an annuity contract.

Define a single-premium immediate annuity (SPIA) and explain how it works.

Identify and distinguish among the types of payout options available under annuity contracts.

Define a deferred annuity and distinguish it from an immediate annuity.

Describe the ways that contract owners can access the funds in their deferred annuities.

Describe the features of different fixed annuity products, such as fixed-rate deferred annuities (FRDAs), fixed indexed annuities (FIAs), and others.

Explain how variable annuities differ from traditional FRDAs and describe the benefit riders associated with variable annuities.

Compare and contrast a registered index-linked annuity (RILA) with a traditional variable annuity and a FIA.

Define an annuity and describe the key players involved in an annuity contract.

Which of these statements best describes an annuity?

An interest-earning time deposit held in a depository institution for a specified time period.

A financial product that offers individuals a means of accumulating, protecting, and distributing assets.

An investment vehicle that pools money from many investors to purchase securities.

That's correct!

The first statement describes a certificate of deposit, and the third statement describes a mutual fund. Annuities are a financial product that allows for the accumulation, protection, and distribution of assets.

Recall from Module 1 that an annuity is a financial product in which an insurer promises to make a series of payments to a named person or entity in exchange for a premium or series of premiums. Annuities offer individuals a way to accumulate and protect assets, with the option of converting those accumulated assets into a sustainable stream of income. An annuity guarantees a stream of income through a series of periodic payments known as annuity payments in exchange for one or more premiums.

In this course, we will use the terms annuity contract and annuity interchangeably.

An annuity that a person buys is an individual annuity, as opposed to a group annuity. We'll discuss only individual annuities in this lesson.

Annuities may be purchased in connection with a retirement plan or individual retirement arrangement (IRA). An annuity purchased to fund or distribute funds from a retirement plan or IRA is known as a qualified annuity. Nonqualified annuities are purchased outside of a retirement plan or IRA. All the different types of annuities we discuss in this lesson can be either qualified or nonqualified annuities.

The parties to an annuity contract are the insurance company and the contract owner. A contract owner that is not a real person is often called a non-natural owner.

In the United States, only insurers can issue an annuity—which means assuming the risk associated with the annuity. However, insurers, banks, broker-dealers, and investment companies can all sell annuities. Although banks, broker-dealers, and investment companies can sell and distribute annuities, they are not parties to the contract.

Annuities come in many different varieties. Even the same type of annuity may differ from company to company, so understanding the terms of the annuity contract is very important.

Annuities have several key players. Let's take a look at them.

Who's the annuitant?

The annuitant is the person whose lifetime the insurance company uses to determine the amount and duration of annuity payments payable under an annuity contract. Typically, annuity contracts state that the insurer will make annuity payments to the annuitant.

What does the term payee mean?

The payee is a natural person or legal entity designated by a contract owner to receive annuity payments if the annuitant does not receive them.

What's a beneficiary?

The beneficiary is the person or legal entity who may receive the remaining value of the annuity contract upon the death of the contract owner or annuitant.

Who's a joint annuitant?

A joint annuitant is a person, in addition to the primary annuitant, whose lifetime the insurance company uses to determine the amount and duration of annuity payments payable under an annuity contract.

Note that annuitants and joint annuitants must be people—but payees and beneficiaries can be either people or legal entities, such as trusts or corporations.

For many annuity contracts,

subsection: Two Key Attributes of an Annuity
text:
Guarantees a lifetime stream of income.

Although some bank accounts are insured by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation (FDIC), only annuity contracts can guarantee annuity payments. The issuing insurance company guarantees these payments. In the unlikely event that the insurer becomes insolvent, the state guaranty associations offer some relief to consumers.

Favorable tax treatment promotes long-term savings.

To encourage people to save for retirement, the Internal Revenue Code (IRC) grants favorable tax treatment to people who purchase annuities.
