Taxes are not usually required on annuity earnings until they are withdrawn from the annuity. Note that some annuities lose the advantage of tax-deferred earnings if they are owned by certain non-natural persons, such as trusts or corporations.

In the United States, which financial institution can issue annuities?

Insurer

Bank

Broker-dealer

That's correct!

In the United States, only insurers can issue—that is, assume the risk associated with—an annuity. Insurers, banks, broker-dealers, and investment companies can all sell annuities.

Use the information below to answer the following questions:

Robert Connelly purchased an annuity from Silver Star Bank. Onyx Life Insurance Company will issue the annuity. Robert wants to start receiving annuity payments when he retires. Robert and his wife, Madge, will receive payments until the last one dies. Their daughter, Karen, will receive any value that remains in the annuity contract after both Robert and Madge die.

The parties to this annuity contract are

Robert and Silver Star Bank

Robert and Onyx Life

Robert, Silver Star Bank, and Onyx Life

That's correct!

The parties to an annuity contract are the insurance company (Onyx Life) and the contract owner (Robert). Silver Star Bank sells the annuity but is not a party to the contract.

Madge is a (primary annuitant / joint annuitant), and (Karen / Madge) is a beneficiary.

primary annuitant / Karen

primary annuitant / Madge

joint annuitant / Karen

joint annuitant / Madge

That's correct!

Madge is a joint annuitant because the insurer uses her lifetime, in addition to Robert's lifetime, to determine the amount and duration of annuity payments.

Karen is the beneficiary because she will receive the remaining value of the annuity contract after Robert and Madge die.

Define a single-premium immediate annuity (SPIA) and explain how it works.

Identify and distinguish among the types of payout options available under annuity contracts.

### Learning Objectives
An immediate annuity begins making annuity payments within one year after purchase. These annuities are also known as single-premium immediate annuities (SPIAs) because they require only one lump-sum premium payment.

SPIAs allow contract owners to

Use a lump sum from another source, such as a retirement plan account balance, as the premium payment. This feature makes SPIAs a popular retirement product, especially for individuals who aren't covered by a pension plan.

Begin receiving annuity payments immediately, but not more than one year later.

### Learning Objectives
How often the insurer makes annuity payments depends on the annuity period selected by the contract owner.

Annuity periods can be

Annuity periods of one month and one year are the most common.

Insurers offer different payout options, which let contract owners choose how they will receive annuity payments.

Payout options affect

The amount of each annuity payment

How long annuity payments continue

Taxes

As a result, choosing the correct payout option is an important decision.

The life annuity (also known as the life only annuity or straight life annuity) makes payments for the lifetime of the annuitant. When the annuitant dies, the payments end.

A contract owner may receive more in payments than was paid into the annuity if the owner lives longer than the insurer expected when it issued the contract. However, the contract owner will receive less than was paid in if the owner dies sooner than expected.

A life income with period certain annuity guarantees that the insurer will make payments for the annuitant's lifetime and for at least a specified period of time, even if the annuitant dies before the end of that time period. The guaranteed period is known as the period certain.

The contract owner specifies the length of the period certain—which is usually 5, 10, 15, or 20 years—when the contract is purchased. The owner also designates a beneficiary to receive annuity payments if the annuitant dies before the end of the period certain.

A life income with refund annuity makes payments throughout the lifetime of the annuitant and guarantees that at least the purchase price of the annuity will be paid out.

If the annuitant dies before the total purchase price of the annuity has been paid out, the insurer will pay the beneficiary the difference between the annuity payments made and the purchase price of the contract. Some annuity contracts give the contract owner a choice of a lump-sum refund or an installment payment refund.

An expert explains that many people will accept lower annuity payments in exchange for guarantees.

A joint and survivor annuity makes payments based on the life expectancies of two annuitants, and payments continue until the last annuitant dies.

The annuity contract specifies whether payment amounts

Remain the same after the first annuitant dies or

Decrease by a stated amount, such as 50%, should one annuitant survive the other

All life contingent options—life annuity, life income with period certain annuity, and life income with refund annuity—can be based on a single life or joint lives. The following illustration is of a joint and survivor life annuity.

Some people want annuities that guarantee payments for a certain period of time or for a specific amount, without being based on the annuitant's life expectancy.

Let's see what kinds of options they have.

The fixed period annuity guarantees annuity payments for a specified time period. If the annuitant dies, the insurer pays the remaining annuity payments to a beneficiary until the end of the specified period.

Example

Joe Bingham purchased a 20-year fixed period annuity. He named his wife, Trish, as the beneficiary.

Analysis

Joe's annuity will pay $540 per month for 20 years. If Joe lives for 27 years after his payments begin, he will receive payments for only 20 of those years. If, however, Joe dies 8 years after payments begin, the insurer will continue to make payments to Trish for 12 years.

A fixed period annuity can create a temporary income stream to delay taking Social Security benefits or to supplement other income sources for a specified period of time.

As its name suggests, a fixed amount annuity provides annuity payments of a specified amount. With a fixed amount annuity, the contract owner informs the insurer of the premium she will pay and the amount of each annuity payment. The insurer then calculates how long the premium plus interest can provide annuity payments of the specified amount.

If the annuitant dies before the annuity payments equal the premium paid, the insurer will pay the remaining annuity payments to the beneficiary.

Example

Max Crider bought a fixed amount annuity paying $1,000 each month and designated his son, Michael, as his beneficiary. His premium is $250,000.

Analysis

The insurer will inform Max of how long he will receive monthly annuity payments of $1,000, as well as the amount of the last payment, which will be less than $1,000. If Max dies before the annuity payments have equaled the premium paid, Michael will receive the remaining annuity payments.

The longer the insurer is likely to make annuity payments—as a result of a guaranteed period certain, a refund, or joint life expectancies—the lower the annuity payments will be.

Insurers frequently offer a cost of living adjustment (COLA) option with SPIAs. A COLA helps the amount of the annuity payments keep up with inflation. Typically, a COLA option increases the annuity payments by a set percentage each year.

Some COLAs tie the annuity payments to the Consumer Price Index. In that situation, annuity payments may increase each year, but they are lower to begin with than they would have been without the COLA.

Some, though not all, SPIAs allow the contract owner to exercise what's known as a commutation right, where the owner receives a lump-sum payment from the contract's principal—even while the owner is receiving annuity payments under the contract. Under a typical commutation right, the owner is permitted to "commute" a portion of the future payments, such as 40%. If the owner chooses commutation, the remaining annuity payments will be lower.

The following situations describe different payout options for annuities. Select the answer choice that illustrates the payout option known as a life income with period certain annuity.

Drew and Beth Dawson own an annuity under which payments will continue until both of them have died.

Mable Cowan owns an annuity that makes payments for as long as she lives. When she dies, the payments end.

Kyle Bastrop's annuity will make annuity payments for at least 10 years. If he dies before the end of that period, the insurer will continue payments to his beneficiary. If he lives beyond 10 years, the insurer will make payments until he dies.

With the payout option known as a life income with period certain annuity, the annuitant knows that payments will be made for at least the specified period (in this case, 10 years); if he lives beyond that period, he will continue to receive payments until his death.

Paul Landry's annuity guarantees that annuity payments will continue throughout his lifetime, and that at least the purchase price of the annuity will be paid out.

That's correct!

Define a deferred annuity and distinguish it from an immediate annuity.

Describe the ways that contract owners can access the funds in their deferred annuities.

Angela Townsend is 58 years old and plans to retire in ten years. Her aunt recently died and left her an inheritance. Angela wants to use this money to purchase an annuity; however, she is still working full time, so she doesn't currently need an income stream. Angela might find a deferred annuity to be more appropriate for her needs than an immediate annuity.

Some people want to accumulate savings and allow those savings to increase in value over time. For these people, a deferred annuity may be a good option. A deferred annuity has an accumulation period, which extends from the time the contract owner purchases the contract until the owner either begins receiving annuity payments or surrenders the contract.

A deferred annuity can be classified as a single-premium deferred annuity (SPDA) or a flexible-premium deferred annuity (FPDA). However, most deferred annuities are SPDAs. The figure below illustrates a typical SPDA.

The contract owner's premiums generate earnings during the accumulation period. The total of the premiums and earnings, less any withdrawals and charges assessed by the insurer, is called the accumulated value.

The contract owner can withdraw some or all of his money during the accumulation period.

If a contract owner wants to access funds from the annuity's accumulated value, the following options are usually available:

Taking withdrawals

Surrendering the contract

Annuitizing the contract

These choices are not mutually exclusive.

Chang Lee's deferred annuity allows for withdrawals of 10% of the accumulated value each year. What do you think the insurer will do if Chang requests a withdrawal of 15% in the second year of the contract?

Cancel the annuity contract

Pay 10% of the accumulated value only

Pay 15% of the accumulated value but impose a penalty charge

That's correct!

The insurer can't cancel the contract or limit the amount of Chang's withdrawal. However, if Chang's contract limits the amount of penalty-free annual withdrawals to a certain percentage, the insurer is likely to assess a penalty charge on any amount taken in excess of that percentage.

Deferred annuities typically allow the contract owner to take withdrawals from the accumulated value during the accumulation period. A typical contract allows the owner to withdraw 10% of the accumulated value each year.

Withdrawals, however, reduce the annuity's accumulated value and may result in a surrender charge from the insurer, as well as federal taxes. Surrender charges help insurance companies recoup the acquisition costs incurred in issuing and selling contracts.

An insurer imposes a surrender charge when the contract owner either (1) takes withdrawals in excess of those allowed by the contract or (2) fully surrenders the contract before the expiration of the surrender charge period. A full surrender terminates the contract, and the contract owner receives the contract's surrender value.

Surrender charges typically apply during the early years of the contract and decrease each year until the end of the surrender charge period. The reason for this decrease—and the eventual elimination of the charge—is that, the longer the contract is in force, the more of the acquisition costs the insurer is able to recover through invested premiums and/or charges on the products.

The following figure illustrates surrender charges for an annuity.

Annuitization is the optional process of converting the accumulated value of a deferred annuity into annuity payments; if the contract owner chooses to annuitize, the contract enters the payout period. In this case, the owner must select a payout option that determines how the insurer will distribute the payments.

Upon annuitization,

The deferred annuity basically becomes an immediate annuity.

The insurer begins making annuity payments on the annuity start date.

Most contract owners do not annuitize their contracts. They usually choose to take systematic withdrawals from the accumulated value instead.

The following table summarizes the differences between immediate and deferred annuities.

|  | Immediate Annuity | Deferred Annuity |
|---|---|---|
| Purchased with a single premium or multiple premiums? | Always a single premium | Either, but single premium is the most common |
| Payout period? | Yes | No, unless the annuity is annuitized |
| Accumulation period? | No | Yes |
| Owner loses ownership and control of the funds? | Yes, unless the contract contains a commutation right | No |
| Can owner withdraw funds from the annuity? | No, unless the contract contains a commutation right | Yes, although withdrawing funds during the accumulation period may result in charges or taxes |

Mindy Duggan purchased a deferred annuity from Good Day Life Insurance Company. Eight years later, Mindy is considering surrendering her deferred annuity. Will Mindy receive annuity payments if she surrenders her annuity?

Yes

No

That's correct!

If Mindy surrenders her annuity, she will receive the annuity's accumulated value minus any surrender charges imposed by the insurer.

Mindy's annuity probably contained (higher / lower) surrender charges during the contract's early years, and these charges have likely (increased / decreased) over time.

higher / increased

higher / decreased

lower / increased

lower / decreased

That's correct!

Annuity contracts typically contain higher surrender charges during the early years of a contract, and these charges usually decrease each year until the end of the surrender charge period.

Describe the features of different fixed annuity products, such as fixed-rate deferred annuities (FRDAs), fixed indexed annuities (FIAs), and others.

Close Transcript

John:

People buy annuities with different goals in mind. Some like the security of earning a fixed amount on their money. Others like the potential for earning more than a guaranteed amount. As a result, insurers offer two basic types of annuities: fixed and variable.

Annuities can be classified as fixed or variable annuities. Who do you think bears the investment risk for a fixed annuity?

The insurer

The contract owner

That's correct!

For fixed annuities, insurers—not contract owners—bear the investment risk, making these products an attractive option for risk-averse individuals. Let's learn more about fixed annuities.

An insurer bears the investment risk for all its fixed annuities—whether they are immediate or deferred—so the insurer deposits the premiums for these annuities in its general account. Recall that the general account is an account that an insurer uses to support the company's contractual obligations to pay benefits for guaranteed insurance products, which include fixed annuity products.

We discussed single premium immediate annuities (SPIAs) earlier. Although variable SPIAs exist, they are relatively rare. So, SPIAs are usually fixed annuities. Now let's look at a more popular product—fixed deferred annuities—starting with fixed-rate deferred annuities, or FRDAs.

Fixed-rate deferred annuities, or FRDAs, combine the principal and interest-rate guarantees of a fixed annuity with the asset accumulation properties of a deferred annuity. Clients often purchase FRDAs to protect and grow their principal and avoid market risks.

With an FRDA, the interest-rate guarantee ensures that the accumulated value will increase over time and the contract owner will never lose any of the premiums—or principal—paid into the annuity (unless possibly when the contract owner surrenders the contract during the surrender charge period).

During the accumulation period, the accumulated value of an FRDA earns interest in one of two ways:

Guaranteed minimum interest-crediting rate

The insurer states a guaranteed minimum interest-crediting rate in the contract. For example, an insurer might guarantee a minimum interest-crediting rate of 0.50%. In this case, the contract's accumulated value will never earn less than 0.50%.

Current interest-crediting rate

An insurer declares and pays this rate on the contract's accumulated value for a specified period. Insurers typically offer different current interest-crediting rates, depending on the term chosen by the contract owner—for example, a three-year, four-year, or five-year term.

After the initial period, the FRDA is subject to a new interest rate for the next period. The new current interest-crediting rate might be higher or lower than the previous rate, but it can't drop below the guaranteed minimum interest-crediting rate.

Example

The Tandem Insurance Company offers a guaranteed minimum interest-crediting rate of 0.50% on its FRDAs. Tandem provides different current interest-crediting rates, depending on whether the contract owner chooses a three-year, four-year, or five-year term:

| 3 Years | 4 Years | 5 Years |
|---|---|---|
| 1.35% | 1.40% | 1.50% |

Contract owners who decide to annuitize their FRDAs can select from any of the payout options previously discussed. FRDAs contain payout schedules, which guarantee a minimum monthly annuity payment per $1,000 of accumulated value. (The actual payout factors used at annuitization may be more than these minimums.)

Most FRDAs specify that, when the insurer begins making annuity payments, the amount of each payment won't change for the time period specified. Having a dependable income allows for better budgeting of expenses. However, clients living on a fixed income may experience a loss of purchasing power during periods of inflation. Some FRDAs offer a cost-of-living adjustment (COLA) rider or have a built-in auto increase feature to help protect against the effects of inflation.

Fixed-rate deferred annuities provide principal guarantees. Another type of annuity is a fixed indexed annuity, or FIA. Do you think an FIA provides a principal guarantee?

Yes

No

Maybe

That's correct!

Because it's a type of fixed deferred annuity, an FIA provides a principal guarantee, but it also offers the potential for more earnings, as you will see.

Fixed indexed annuities (FIAs) offer principal guarantees and a guaranteed minimum interest-crediting rate during the accumulation period. In addition, FIAs offer the possibility of additional earnings based on changes in an index. A commonly used index in FIAs is the Standard & Poor's (S&P) 500 composite stock price index. The index specified in an FIA is known as the reference index.

Depending on the performance of the reference index, an FIA may earn interest credits. Most FIAs credit interest on an annual basis. As a general rule,

If the reference index gains in value over the year, an annuity will earn interest credits

If the reference index shows no growth or loses value over the year, an annuity will not earn interest credits

To compensate the insurer for these guarantees and for the costs of administering the product, the FIA contract typically limits the amount of interest credits that may be earned.

Despite its name, the deferred income annuity (DIA) is not a deferred annuity—and neither is a qualified longevity annuity contract (QLAC), which is a variation of a DIA. DIAs and QLACs possess features of immediate annuities and FRDAs:

Like immediate annuities, DIAs and QLACs have no accumulation period and therefore no accumulated value. At the time of purchase, the buyer locks in a guaranteed payment amount at a future date.

Like FRDAs, the date when payments will begin is at some point in the future—from 12 months to 40 years or more after purchase. Unlike FRDAs, though, DIAs and QLACs specify this date.

Let's see what an expert says about who could benefit from a deferred income annuity.

A DIA is generally used in retirement to provide part of a retiree's income. Many DIAs give the owner flexibility after purchase to change the date that annuity payments begin. Some contracts allow cost-of-living adjustments, although they come with smaller initial annuity payments.

Many newer DIAs offer death benefits, but they come with the cost of noticeably lower annuity payments.

Example

Brian Adolphi is 60 and just bought a DIA. Annuity payments are scheduled to begin in 7 years when he retires at 67 and will continue for the rest of his life. Brian's DIA has no death benefit.

Analysis

If Brian dies at 65, he'll never receive any annuity payments. If he dies at 68, he'll receive only a year of payments, and no one else will receive any money after that. On the other hand, if Brian lives to be 90, he'll have received payments for 23 years, and these payments will have been larger than those for a traditional FRDA.

QLAC annuity payments do not begin until an advanced age, such as 80. As a result, the annuity payments the person receives from a QLAC will be quite large compared to FRDA payments bought for the same premium amount. As with DIAs, contract owners of QLACs can choose a death benefit feature, but the income payments will be lower.

Note that, unlike the other types of annuities we discuss in this lesson, a QLAC can only be purchased with qualified retirement plan funds.

Example

At age 65, Manny Iruku has substantial funds in a brokerage account. He's concerned about outliving his assets, though, so he decides to buy a QLAC that will begin making payments when he turns 80. Manny takes $75,000 from his 401(k) balance to pay for the QLAC.

Analysis

Manny will find it easier to plan for his income needs from age 65 to 80 because he knows that he has another source of income that will start at age 80.

The figure below summarizes the different types of fixed annuities. (Click or touch image to enlarge.)

Naomi Brandt bought an FRDA from the Tandem Insurance Company. Select the true statements about her annuity.

Naomi chose an FRDA to grow her principal.

Naomi's FRDA helps her to avoid market risk.

Tandem guarantees a minimum interest-crediting rate on Naomi's annuity.

That's correct!

All of these statements are true. By purchasing an FRDA from Tandem, Naomi will protect and grow her principal, as well as avoid market risk.

Submit

Tandem's new current interest-crediting rate on its FRDAs will always be more than or equal to its guaranteed minimum interest-crediting rate.

True

False

That's correct!

The current interest-crediting rate can be higher or lower than the previous rate, but it can't drop below the guaranteed minimum interest-crediting rate stated in the contract.

Assume that the reference index for a given fixed indexed annuity (FIA) lost value over the interest-crediting term. In this situation, the contract owner would lose some of the premiums invested in the contract.

True

False

That's correct!

In this situation, the FIA would not earn any interest credits over the term. As a fixed annuity, however, the FIA would guarantee no loss of principal.

Select the true statements about deferred income annuities (DIAs) and qualified longevity annuity contracts (QLACs).

For DIAs and QLACs, the buyer locks in a guaranteed source of retirement income that will begin at a future date.

Unlike fixed-rate deferred annuities, DIAs and QLACs specify the date when payments will begin.

Like deferred annuities, DIAs and QLACs have an accumulation period and an accumulated value.

Both DIAs and QLACs are categorized as fixed annuities but not deferred annuities.

That's correct!

All of these statements are true except for the third statement. Like immediate annuities, DIAs and QLACs have neither an accumulation period nor an accumulated value.

Explain how variable annuities differ from traditional FRDAs and describe the benefit riders and charges associated with variable annuities.

What kind of customer do you think might be interested in a variable annuity? (Choose all that apply.)

An individual who is comfortable accepting some investment risk.

An individual who wants a guarantee of no loss of principal.

An individual who wants to choose how premiums are invested.

That's correct!

A variable annuity offers fewer guarantees than a fixed annuity. In the absence of a living benefit rider, a variable annuity offers no principal guarantee. (We discuss living benefit riders later in this lesson). On the other hand, a contract owner who is willing to take some risk in how premiums are invested may be able to grow the principal.

Submit

Traditional variable annuities provide contract owners with investment choice—but along with that investment choice comes the possibility of investment losses. Because of the risk of investment losses, variable annuities must be registered as securities with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

In recent years, a newcomer has emerged in the variable annuity space: the registered index-linked annuity, or RILA. RILAs operate a bit differently from traditional variable annuities. We discuss RILAs later in this lesson. Until then, when we say variable annuity, we are referring to a variable deferred annuity other than a RILA.

The chart below summarizes the types of variable annuities. Variable immediate annuities are rare, so our discussion will focus on the more common product—the variable deferred annuity.

With a variable annuity, the following amounts may vary according to market performance:

The accumulated value

The annuity payments for contract owners choosing to receive variable annuity payments

Any withdrawal amounts or annuity payments made under the terms of a living benefit rider

Within limits, a variable annuity contract owner is free to choose the accounts into which premiums are invested. Click or touch each type of account below to learn more.

Fixed Accounts

Subaccounts

Subaccounts, you may recall, are alternative pools of investments, each having a distinct risk/return profile and investment strategy. For example, a subaccount seeking significant growth in the principal usually involves a higher level of risk. These subaccounts are part of the insurer's separate account. Recall that insurers use separate accounts to help manage the funds placed in variable products, including variable annuities.

When subaccount values increase, the annuity contract owner—and not the insurer—benefits. The contract owner also bears the investment risk when these values decrease. If subaccount values decrease significantly, contract owners even run the risk of losing part of their principal.

Within stated limitations, contract owners have flexibility in their investment choices. A contract owner can

Change the percentage of money allocated to fixed accounts and subaccounts

Transfer funds between fixed accounts and subaccounts

An attractive feature associated with variable annuities is that, during the accumulation period, any transfer of funds among fixed accounts and subaccounts will not trigger a tax liability for the contract owner.

With variable annuities, contract owners can use their premiums to purchase accumulation units, which represent ownership shares in the selected subaccounts. The insurer normally subtracts certain expense charges from these premiums.

The number of accumulation units that a given amount will purchase depends on the accumulation unit value of the subaccount at that time.

Confused?

Here are the formulas insurers use to calculate the accumulated value of a variable annuity:

| Current Value of an Accumulation Unit | x | # of Accumulation Units in the Subaccount | = | Contract Owner's Subaccount Value |  |  |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|

| Total of All Subaccount Values | + | Fixed Account Values | = | Contract's Total Accumulated Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|

The actual calculations for determining a variable annuity's accumulated value are beyond the scope of this course.

A contract owner who chooses to annuitize a variable annuity has the same payout options as a fixed annuity contract owner. In addition to selecting a payout option, variable annuity contract owners can choose fixed or variable payments. Do you think they typically choose fixed annuity payments or variable annuity payments?

Fixed

Variable

They're equally popular

Sorry, that's incorrect.

Fixed payments are more popular because contract owners typically prefer a predictable income stream.

Although contract owners who annuitize are usually looking for predictable income, variable payments may be appropriate for someone who can tolerate risk and market volatility. One advantage of variable payments is that they may offer some protection against inflation risk.

Variable annuity contract owners can also select a combination of fixed and variable annuity payments—for example, half of each payment is fixed, while the other half fluctuates.

Remember, though—few deferred annuity owners choose annuitization.

Close Transcript

Carla:

A variable annuity sounds great when the market goes up, but not so great when the market goes down.

John:

That's right! That's why insurers offer guaranteed benefits that can help VA contract owners protect their investments.

Carla:

Are there any guarantees about the size of the death benefit?

John:

Annuity contracts specify how the death benefit will be determined. But, most insurers offer enhanced benefits, including a guaranteed minimum death benefit.

Insurers use policy riders to amend variable annuity contracts to provide supplemental benefits. The two main types of supplemental benefits are death benefits and living benefits.

A guaranteed minimum death benefit (GMDB) guarantees that the insurer will pay the beneficiary at least a specified minimum amount, regardless of the contract's accumulated value at the time. Insurers offer many variations on the GMDB, but the typical one is a return of premiums less any withdrawals.

Example

Elena Takayashi paid a single premium of $300,000 for a variable annuity with a GMDB. This GMDB guaranteed that the beneficiary—her husband, Martin—would receive at least a return of premiums less any withdrawals. When Elena died, she had taken no withdrawals. The current accumulated value of her contract had fallen to $250,000 due to market performance.

Analysis

The insurer paid Martin a death benefit of $300,000, which was $50,000 more than he would have received if Elena had not purchased the GMDB.

Contract owners of variable annuities may have the option of purchasing living benefit riders, which offer protection from market downturns by guaranteeing certain income, withdrawal, or accumulation amounts. Owners of other types of deferred annuities—such as FIAs and FRDAs—may also be able to purchase a living benefit rider.

The guaranteed income or withdrawal amounts depend on the contract's benefit base, which is a value that the insurer calculates separately from the contract's accumulated value. This benefit base is typically protected from any decreases in the financial markets. For a product like a variable annuity, where the accumulated value fluctuates based on the underlying investments, having a protected value—i.e., the benefit base—can give the contract owner peace of mind.

Click or touch each type of major living benefit rider below to learn more.

Guaranteed minimum income benefit (GMIB)

Guaranteed lifetime withdrawal benefit (GLWB)

Guaranteed minimum withdrawal benefit (GMWB)

Guaranteed minimum accumulation benefit (GMAB)

A guaranteed minimum accumulation benefit (GMAB) guarantees a minimum protected amount if the annuity stays in force for a specified period of time.

An important aspect of the GMAB is that it operates automatically. While the owner of a contract with a GMIB, GLWB, or GMWB must choose to utilize the withdrawal or income benefit, the insurer provides the GMAB to the owner if the protected amount exceeds the accumulated value at the end of the specified period.

Tim Gannon
Vice President
Product Management
Fidelity Investments Life

If you're looking at buying a variable annuity with a benefits rider there are many options that you can choose from. One is a GMIB or guaranteed minimum income benefit which promises if you put X amount of dollars into an account, five or ten years down the road it will create a lifetime income stream to you. That will usually be of a fixed nature, similar to an income annuity.

You sometimes have the choice of a withdrawal benefit or WB rider. A withdrawal benefit rider provides a minimum amount of income in retirement but with upside potential based on market participation in the underlying funds.

Another option for a person who is not necessarily looking to create income but wants some level of protection in market participation is an AB or an accumulation benefit product. Sometimes referred to as a GMAB and what these do is, they're a very small piece of the industry, but as the market volatility continues they may become more popular.

They say put $100,000 in, we guarantee you'll get $100,000 out but if the markets participate and do well you'll capture that upside potential. So when you're buying a variable annuity you have to ask yourself the question, what am I looking to accomplish? Am I looking to create lifetime income? Do I want income that has growth potential? Do I just want something that's fixed and set for as long as I live? Or am I looking for a variable annuity that also just provides just principal protection whether or not I want to turn that income on in the future?

Recall that all deferred annuities—including variable annuities—are subject to surrender charges. For variable annuities, the surrender charge is usually called a contingent deferred sales charge, or CDSC.

Variable annuities also include explicit expense charges to cover services such as contract maintenance and fund management. These explicit expense charges are established at contract issue and do not change while the contract is in force.

Andrew Yates purchased a variable annuity with a guaranteed minimum death benefit (GMDB) from Jardim Insurance and named his wife, Flora, as the beneficiary.

Which statements are true about this situation? (Choose all that apply.)

A GMDB guarantees that Jardim will pay Flora at least a specified minimum amount, regardless of the contract's accumulated value at the time of Andrew's death.

Jardim pays the GMDB only if Andrew dies after annuitizing the contract.

Insurers like Jardim offer many variations on the GMDB, including all premiums paid, less withdrawals.

Sorry, that's incorrect.

A GMDB does guarantee that Jardim will pay Flora at least a specified minimum amount, and insurers like Jardim offer many variations on the GMDB. However, Jardim pays the GMDB only if Andrew dies during the annuity's accumulation period.

Of the following living benefit riders, which one bases the annuity payment amounts on annuitization of the benefit base?

Guaranteed minimum withdrawal benefit (GMWB)

Guaranteed minimum accumulation benefit (GMAB)

Guaranteed lifetime withdrawal benefit (GLWB)

Guaranteed minimum income benefit (GMIB)

Sorry. That's incorrect.

Of these four living benefit riders, only the GMIB bases the annuity payment amounts on annuitization of the benefit base. Typically, the contract owner must keep the annuity in force for a certain number of years during the accumulation period, which is known as the "waiting period," before having the option to annuitize.

Which of the following types of annuities typically come with surrender charges? (Choose all that apply.)

Fixed indexed annuities (FIAs)

Fixed-rate deferred annuities (FRDAs)

Variable deferred annuities

That's correct!

All of these annuities are types of deferred annuities. Deferred annuities, whether they're fixed or variable, typically come with surrender charges.

Compare and contrast a registered index-linked annuity (RILA) with a traditional variable annuity and a fixed indexed annuity (FIA).

Insurers have created a variable product called a registered index-linked annuity (RILA). Knowing that it's a variable product, how do you think a RILA works? (Choose all that apply.)

A RILA is a security.

A RILA owner can lose money if the subaccounts perform poorly.

Submit

In recent years, registered index-linked annuities (RILAs) have become popular with customers seeking equity market exposure combined with some protection against investment losses. As the word "registered" indicates, a RILA must be registered as a security, just like a traditional variable annuity.

RILAs resemble FIAs in the following ways:

The insurer invests RILA premiums in its general account.

RILAs offer the potential for principal growth based on the performance of a stock market index, such as the S&P 500, over a specified term, such as one year or more. Also like FIAs, this growth is subject to specified limits—sometimes called caps or participation rates.

Because RILAs are variable annuities, though, contract owners can lose money on them. Recall that a variable annuity contract owner can lose money if the underlying investments—or subaccounts—perform poorly.

Although RILAs don't have subaccounts, they do have an investment component—because principal growth is affected by the performance of an associated stock market index. With a RILA, the contract owner can lose money based on decreases in the value of the stock market index.

However, unlike traditional variable annuities, RILAs offer contract owners some protection against excessive investment loss by using buffers or floors. Please click or touch each box below to learn more.

With a buffer, the accumulated value is protected until index losses exceed a specified limit, such as 10%, 15%, or 20%. For example, with a 10% buffer, if the value of the index decreases by 15%, you would lose 5%.

With a floor, the accumulated value is protected from index losses in excess of a specified percentage, such as 10%. For example, with a 10% floor, if the value of the index decreases by 15%, you would lose 10%.

For simplicity, we are assuming that the contract owner selects just one buffer or floor. In reality, with most RILAs, owners can divide their premium(s) between different buffer and floor options.

Because RILAs are deferred annuities, they come with surrender charges.

Valerie Jordan purchased a RILA from the Tandem Insurance Company. At the end of the term, the index dropped 15% in value, and the accumulated value of Valerie's RILA decreased by 5%. This information indicates that Valerie's RILA has a

Floor of 10%

Buffer of 10%

After studying this lesson, you will be able to:

Describe the benefits provided under major medical and supplemental medical expense insurance policies.

Explain common cost participation methods.

Explain how managed care plans attempt to manage members’ access to medical care and the costs of that care.

Describe the basic characteristics of consumer-driven health plans.

Describe the benefit triggers for long-term care insurance policies.

Distinguish among the various definitions of total disability and explain the purpose of elimination periods in disability income policies.

Describe the government-sponsored disability income plans available in the United States.

Describe the benefits provided under major medical and supplemental medical expense insurance policies.

Explain common cost participation methods.

Like life insurance and annuities, most health insurance products are available on a group or individual basis.
A group health insurance policy would insure the health of a specific group of people, such as a group of employees.

An individual would purchase an individual health insurance policy that would insure the health of a named individual. Some individual policies also insure the named person’s immediate family or a second insured person.

Health insurance protects against the risk of financial loss from illness or injury and comes in three forms:

Medical expense coverage provides benefits to pay for treatment of illnesses and injuries

Long-term care (LTC) coverage provides benefits for medical and other services to insureds who need constant care at home or in a qualified facility

Disability income coverage provides income replacement benefits to an insured who is unable to work because of illness or injury

We'll look at medical expense coverage first.

Many people obtain medical expense coverage through their employer, if offered, under a group insurance policy. For the self-employed and others who cannot get coverage through work, many types of individual coverages are available.

The most common type of medical expense insurance—major medical coverage—generally pays benefits for

Basic hospital, surgical, and physicians' expenses

Additional services related to illness or injury

Prescription drugs

Some preventive care

People insured under major medical policies typically receive comprehensive health care in exchange for a fixed, periodic—usually monthly—premium. This premium covers the cost of most health care services.

What isn’t covered?

Cosmetic surgery (other than required corrective surgery)

Illness or injury during military service or acts of war

Intentionally self-inflicted injuries

Routine dental treatments, eye exams, and corrective lenses

Medical expense policies typically include features that require the insured to share in the costs of some types of medical care.

Forms of cost sharing:

Deductible—a flat dollar amount of eligible expenses that the insured pays before the insurance pays benefits.

Deductible example

Most major medical policies include a calendar-year deductible. For example, a policy with a $500 calendar-year deductible requires that the insured incur $500 of covered medical expenses in one calendar year before the insurance policy will begin payments.

Coinsurance—a percentage of the allowable expenses remaining after the insured has paid the deductible that must be paid by the insured.

Coinsurance example

Major medical policies often include a 20 to 30 percent coinsurance requirement. For example, a policy with a $500 annual deductible and a 20% coinsurance requirement would require that the insured pay 20% of all charges above $500.

Most major medical policies limit the total amount of money the insured must pay by including a maximum out-of-pocket provision, also known as a stop-loss provision. For example, a policy with a $5,000 out-of-pocket maximum will pay 100% of allowable medical expenses after the insured has paid $5,000 to satisfy deductible and coinsurance requirements.

Features of health insurance plans under the Affordable Care Act (ACA)

Offered by private insurance companies

Guaranteed issue - Health insurance issuers must enroll any applicant, regardless of age, gender, or health status.

Guaranteed renewal - Health insurance issuers must offer to continue coverage at the option of the policyholder, as long as premiums are paid.

Health insurance premium subsidies - Government subsidies are available to people who qualify based on their income levels.

Coverage must be provided for children until age 26

Coverage must be provided for preventive care with no copayment or other out-of-pocket expense.

Coverage must be provided for at least the following ten essential services:

Ambulatory patient services, also known as outpatient care

Emergency services

Hospitalization

Maternity and newborn care

Mental health and substance use disorder services, including behavioral health treatment

Prescription drugs

Rehabilitative and habilitative services and devices—habilitative services are health care services that help you keep, learn, or improve skills and functioning for daily living

Laboratory services

Preventive and wellness services and chronic disease management

Pediatric services, including oral and vision care

Marissa Barbosa bought an individual medical expense policy in January. The policy includes a $500 calendar-year deductible and a 20% coinsurance requirement. Marissa incurred $800 in allowable medical expenses in May. How much of this amount will she have to pay?

$500

$560

$800

Now suppose Marissa incurs an additional $400 in allowable medical expenses in June. How much of this amount will Marissa have to pay?

$80

$100

$400

For an additional fee, insurers offer coverage for many excluded services through supplemental medical expense policies. Click or touch the tabs below to learn more about these supplemental policies.

Dental Expense Coverage

Vision Care Coverage

Dread Disease (DD) Coverage

What about supplemental coverages in other countries? In countries in which medical care is provided through government programs, insurers sell supplemental coverages for services not covered by government medical insurance or for care in private hospitals.

Select the medical expenses that are typically excluded under a major medical insurance policy. (Choose all that apply.)

Intentionally self-inflicted injuries

Basic hospital expenses

Basic surgical expenses

Elective cosmetic surgery

Services related to illness or injury

Physicians’ expenses

Some preventive care

Routine eye exams and corrective lenses

Illness or injury incurred during military service or acts of war

Routine dental treatments

Submit

Select the major medical policy feature based on its correct description.

A flat dollar amount of eligible expenses that the insured pays before the insurance begins paying benefits

Coinsurance

Deductible

Maximum out-of-pocket provision

A percentage of the allowable expenses in excess of the amount already paid by the insured

Coinsurance

Deductible

Maximum out-of-pocket provision

A limit on the amount the insured must pay out of pocket per year

Coinsurance

Deductible

Maximum out-of-pocket provision

The type of supplemental coverage that pays for medical expenses incurred by the insured who has contracted a specified disease is dread disease (DD) coverage.

True

False

Explain how managed care plans attempt to manage members’ access to medical care and the costs of that care.

Insurers emphasize prevention, early intervention, and cost-savings by structuring medical insurance plans so that physicians and insureds are in partnership with the insurer in coordinating the management and delivery of medical care. Managed care features include

Contracting with provider networks of doctors and health care facilities to deliver medical services for negotiated fees

Requiring or financially motivating insureds to obtain care from network providers

Using primary care providers (PCPs) to coordinate insured’s' medical care and access to specialists

Medical insurance managed care plans can be classified according to how medical care is managed within a plan. Typically, the more freedom insureds are allowed in choosing providers, the higher the cost of the plan. Three primary types of managed care plans are:

Health maintenance organizations (HMOs)

Preferred provider organizations (PPOs)

Point of service (POS) plans

Let’s compare these types of plans

|  | HMO | PPO | POS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary care provider (PCP) | Required | Not required | Usually required |
| Referrals to other providers | Required | Not required | Usually required |
| Use of in-network providers | Required | Higher benefit levels for in-network providers | Higher benefit levels for in-network providers |
| Use of out-of-network providers | No benefits are paid | Lower benefit levels for going outside the network | Lower benefit levels for going outside the network |

Insureds under managed care plans generally must pay a network provider a specified, fixed amount, known as a copayment, each time the plan member receives services from the provider. For example, insureds may be required to pay $20 for each doctor visit and for each prescription.

HMO members typically are only responsible for copayments. Other managed care plans typically have a copayment for use of in-network providers, and deductible and coinsurance requirements for out-of-network providers.

Managed care plans place a greater emphasis on preventive care than do traditional medical expense plans. Managed care plans usually cover prenatal and well-baby care, routine physical exams, screening tests, and childhood immunizations.

In addition, managed care plans cover many services that traditional medical expense plans may not, such as:

Wellness programs (smoking cessation or weight or stress reduction)

Patient education programs that help plan members make informed decisions about their health care

In managed care plans, physicians and insureds are in partnership with the insurer in coordinating the management and delivery of medical care.

True

False

Typically, the more freedom insureds in a managed care plan are allowed in choosing providers, the higher the cost of the plan.

True

False

Describe the basic characteristics of consumer-driven health plans.

Another innovation in providing medical insurance is the consumer-driven health plan.

A consumer-driven health plan (CDHP) uses a health savings account (HSA) for the payment of routine health care expenses, while a high-deductible health plan (HDHP) protects the insured from catastrophic medical expenses.

Some CDHPs use a health reimbursement arrangement (HRA), usually with an HDHP.

Paul Long is a member of a consumer-driven health plan (CDHP) funded by his employer, Capital Manufacturing. (Click or touch the arrows to the right.)

Recall that Paul Long is a member of a consumer-driven health plan (CDHP) funded by a health savings account (HSA) established by his employer, Capital Manufacturing. Once the funds in Paul’s HSA are gone, Paul cannot receive any additional medical care.

True

False

Describe the benefit triggers for long-term care insurance policies.

Medical expense coverage is only part of the health insurance picture…another part is long-term care (LTC) coverage, which provides benefits for medical and other services to insureds who need care for an extended period in their own homes or in a qualified facility.

Because the costs of long-term care can deplete savings quickly, many people turn to long-term care insurance to provide that care.

A long-term care insurance policy requirement specifying the conditions that establish an insured’s eligibility to receive long-term care benefits.

Two common benefit triggers are:

An inability to complete activities of daily living (ADLs)

Cognitive impairment

The activities of daily living (ADLs) are the activities of eating, bathing, dressing, continence, toileting, and transferring into and out of a bed, chair, or wheelchair.

A licensed health care practitioner must certify that the insured requires assistance with at least the specified number of ADLs.

Cognitive impairment is a reduction in a person’s ability to think, reason, or remember. Alzheimer’s disease and dementia are common types of cognitive impairment.

Cognitive impairment severe enough to require regular supervision or assistance to protect the insured and others around the insured triggers LTC benefits. LTC policies generally treat severe cognitive impairments as a benefit trigger even if the insured can perform all of the ADLs without assistance.

See as this industry expert explains how the benefit triggers for long-term care coverage have been changing to include more than just the activities of daily living.

Long-term care is provided in an insured’s home or in a qualified facility. Qualified facilities may include

Nursing homes

Rehabilitation facilities

Inpatient behavioral health facilities—a behavioral health facility is involved in the care and treatment of persons with mental disorders and addiction disorders

Hospices—a hospice focuses on making life comfortable for those confronting a terminal illness by providing medical care, managing pain, and provided emotional and spiritual support

Assisted living facilities

Adult daycare centers

Care paid for by LTC coverage may include

Diagnostic services

Preventive services

Therapeutic services

Rehabilitative services

Maintenance services

Personal care services

Long-term care insurance policies generally state a daily benefit amount, which is the maximum amount of benefits payable for each day of an insured’s long-term care at a care facility or in the patient’s home.

Daily benefit amounts typically range from $50 to $500. Many LTC policies provide a lower daily benefit amount for certain types of facilities.

### Example
For example, an LTC policy may provide a daily benefit amount of $200 a day for care in a nursing home, $150 a day for care in an assisted living facility, and $100 a day for home care.

Some newer long-term care policies provide a monthly benefit amount instead of a daily benefit amount. Such policies allow the insured more flexibility in paying expenses. Let’s look at a comparison of amounts paid under daily and monthly benefits amount policies.

An insured incurs eligible expenses of $300 a day for five days, and expenses of $100 a day for the 25 remaining days in the month.

$150 Daily Benefit

For five days, $150 maximum paid = $750 benefits
For 25 days, $100 maximum paid = $2,500 benefits
Total benefits paid = $3,250

$5,000 Monthly Benefit

For five days, $300 maximum paid = $1,500 benefits
For 25 days, $100 maximum paid, = $2,500 paid
Total benefits paid = $4,000

Distinguish among the various definitions of total disability and explain the purpose of elimination periods in disability income policies.

Describe the government-sponsored disability income plans available in the United States.

Disability income insurance is available to individuals and groups and provides short-term or long-term coverage, depending on the length of the benefit period—the time during which benefits are payable. The benefit periods for individual and group coverage differ. Click or touch the right arrow below to see how.

Disability income policies define total disability to determine whether an insured is eligible to receive disability income benefits.

Most policies use one of three definitions: (click or touch each one below)

"Current Usual" Definition—Used in Most Policies

"Own Previous Occupation" Definition

"Income Loss" Definition

Some disability income policies classify certain conditions as presumptive disabilities that, if present, automatically cause the insured to be considered totally disabled. An insured with a presumptive disability receives the full income benefit amount provided under the policy, even if he resumes full-time employment in a former occupation.

Presumptive disabilities include

Total and permanent blindness

Loss of the use of any two limbs

Loss of speech or hearing

Marissa suffered a disability that meets her policy’s definition of total disability. Will she start receiving disability income benefits on the first day of her disability?

Yes

No

Insurers specify an elimination period (waiting period) which is how long an insured must be disabled before disability income benefit payments begin. An elimination period reduces an insurer’s cost to process and pay claims for conditions that last for a very short time.

After the elimination period passes, Marissa begins receiving disability income benefit payments. How will the amount of these payments compare to her pre-disability income?

Disability income benefits will be higher than her pre-disability earnings

Disability income benefits will be lower than her pre-disability earnings

Disability income benefits will be the same as her pre-disability earnings

If disability payments weren’t lower than pre-disability income, a disabled person could earn as much while disabled as she could while working and would have no financial incentive to return to work.

So, insureds usually receive a percentage of their regular earnings, such as 60 or 70 percent, as disability income.

A variety of supplemental benefits are available in connection with disability income policies:

A partial disability benefit pays when an insured can't perform some regular job duties or can't work full time.

A cost-of-living adjustment (COLA) benefit periodically increases benefit amounts to correspond with increases in the cost of living.

A U.S. federal program that provides disability income benefit payments to qualified individuals.

Supplemental Security Income (SSI): For people with limited incomes who are disabled, blind, or age 65 or older. The benefit is based on financial need.

Social Security Disability Income (SSDI): For workers under age 65 who have paid Social Security tax. The benefit is based on Social Security taxes paid.

SSDI defines total disability as "The inability to work because of sickness or injury that has lasted, or is expected to last, for at least one year, or to lead to death."

What about government-sponsored disability programs in other countries?

Most countries offer a government disability (invalidity) program similar to the Social Security Disability Income (SSDI) in the United States. There may be variations in the definition of disability, the way the benefit is funded, and in how the amount of the benefit is determined. Some countries offer a supplemental benefit to disabled persons who require daily help.

U.S. workers who are disabled as a result of work-related accidents or illnesses are covered under state-mandated workers' compensation programs.

Programs provide

Medical expense benefits

Wage replacement benefits

Mandatory employer contributions fund workers’ compensation. Benefit amounts vary by state.

What about workers’ compensation programs in other countries?

Most countries offer a workers’ compensation (work injury) program that is similar to the program in the United States. Some countries have separate work injury programs; other countries’ work injury programs are included as part of the general social security program.

David, an architect, purchased disability income insurance through his employer. Six months later, he lost the use of his right arm in an accident while on vacation. For two years, he was unable to work while he underwent physical therapy and learned to use a prosthesis. He then found a job teaching at a local college. His salary, which was $150,000 as an architect, dropped to $50,000 as a teacher.

If David's disability income policy has a two-year "current usual" definition of total disability, how long is he entitled to receive disability income benefits?

For two years

Until he is able to work as an architect again

Now suppose that David's policy includes an "own previous occupation" definition of total disability. How long is he entitled to receive disability income benefits?

For the first two years only

Until he is able to work as an architect again

Now suppose that David's policy includes an "income loss" definition of total disability. How long is he entitled to receive disability income benefits?

For the first two years only

Until he is able to work as an architect again

Until he is earning as much in his new career as he earned as an architect

Congratulations.

You have reached the end of our look at the various products that life and health insurance companies offer. Let’s review how some of these products helped people you’ve met along the way manage the personal risks they face.

Carter McIntosh is married with children and has an established career. He needed peace of mind that his wife Bridget and their children would be financially OK if something happened to Carter before Bridget goes back to work. They purchased an inexpensive term life to get the protection they needed.

Ethan Franco wants lifetime insurance coverage and access to the cash value. He also wants more control over his insurance policy’s earnings. Ethan purchased variable universal life. That gives him both death benefit and premium flexibility, and the family will be protected as long as the policy stays in force. He also adds to his family’s savings through deciding how the policy’s cash value is invested.

Carla Abernathy is closer to the end of her career than the beginning and wants to make sure she’ll be comfortable in retirement. She likes to assume investment risk but wants to defer paying taxes on her earnings until she actually needs the money. Her income has gone up substantially, and she’s maxed out her retirement options at work. She purchased a variable annuity, which will give her the investment flexibility and the tax deferral she desires.

Marissa Barbosa is young, on her own, and self-sufficient. Both of her parents are gone, so she’s got no one to fall back on if something happens to her and she can’t work. A serious injury or illness could ruin her financially, through high medical bills, lost income, or both. Her two biggest needs were medical insurance and disability insurance. With these two products, her finances are protected if something unfortunate happens.
Identify three types of supplemental disability benefits that can be added to life insurance policies.

Life insurance policies protect millions of lives and help meet a variety of financial needs.

But sometimes the basic insurance policy is not a complete solution.

Insurance companies sell supplemental benefits that expand basic life insurance coverage to help customers meet more of their financial needs.

Policyowners pay an additional premium for each supplemental benefit they select.

The additional charge ends when the supplemental benefit expires or is canceled.

Carter has questions—lots of them--that his new insurance agent, John Smith, can answer.

Who do YOUR customers call for answers to questions about supplemental benefits?

What if they called YOU?

Even if they don’t call you, knowing answers to common questions helps you better serve customers, understand how your company’s products meet customer needs, and perform your job.

Insurance companies offer three types of supplemental benefits to protect a life insurance policyowner or insured who becomes disabled.

Close Transcript

Gloria:

Policy services, this is Gloria, how can I help you?

Paul:

This is Paul Long. I have a life insurance policy with you. When my friend became disabled, his premium was paid by his insurance company. Can I get something like that?

Gloria:

Certainly. Pending underwriter approval, we can add a waiver of premium for disability benefit where we pay the premium if you are totally disabled.

Paul:

I only have a term life policy....

Gloria:

We can add the waiver to almost any type of policy. Then, if you become disabled, you just submit proof of your total disability.

subsection: Waiver of Premium for Disability Benefit
text:
One of the most common supplemental benefits that may be added to almost all individual life insurance policies is the waiver of premium for disability (WP) benefit.

Under a WP benefit, the insurance company pays the renewal premiums if the insured becomes totally disabled and satisfies a three- to six-month waiting period.

In other words, the insured must be totally disabled for three to six months and continue to make the premium payments that come due before the insurer will begin waiving premium payments. Some policies will refund the premiums paid during the waiting period if the insured is still disabled when the waiting period ends.

The WP benefit typically defines total disability for the insured as being unable to perform the duties of

Any occupation

The insured’s own previous occupation

Any occupation for which the insured is reasonably suited by education, training, or experience

Most WP benefits define total disability as the insured’s inability to perform the duties of any occupation for which the insured is reasonably suited by education, training, or experience. The insurer usually requires periodic proof that the insured continues to be totally disabled.

Close Transcript

Paul:

That sounds great. I also have a life insurance policy on my wife. Can I add the waiver to that policy?

Gloria:

If you own the policy and pay the premiums, then you can add a waiver of premium for payor benefit, pending underwriter approval. We’ll waive the premiums for that policy if you die or become totally disabled.

section: Waiver of Premium for Payor Benefit
text:
Most individual life insurance policies are issued to a policyowner who is also the insured. However, sometimes the policyowner is not the insured but a third-party. Under a waiver of premium for payor benefit, the insurer waives renewal premiums if the policyowner, rather than the insured, dies or becomes totally disabled.

For example, the benefit often is attached to a juvenile insurance policy covering the life of a child. The policy is owned and paid for by an adult, usually the child’s parent or legal guardian.

For juvenile policies, the insurer usually waives the premiums until the insured child reaches a specified age, such as 18 or 21, when the ownership of the policy and the responsibility for paying the premiums passes to the insured.

To add a waiver of premium for payor benefit to a life insurance policy, who do you think must be insurable?

The insured

The policyowner

Both the insured and the policyowner

Neither the insured or the policyowner

Answer: Both the insured and the policyowner

Explanation: The policyowner generally must provide satisfactory evidence of insurability—in addition to providing evidence of the insurability of the insured in order to add a waiver of premium for payor benefit to a life insurance policy.

What is the difference between the waiver of premium for disability benefit and the waiver of premium for payor benefit?

| Waiver of Premium for Disability Benefit | Waiver of Premium for Payor Benefit |
|---|---|
| Insurer waives policy premiums if the insured becomes totally disabled. | Insurer waives policy premiums if the policyowner dies or becomes totally disabled. |
| Designed for policies in which the policyowner is also the policy’s insured | Designed for third-party policies in which the policyowner is not the policy’s insured |
| Total disability defined as    •  The insured’s inability to perform the essential duties of her own occupation OR any other occupation for which she is reasonably suited by education, training, or experience. | Total disability defined as    •  The policyowner’s inability to perform the essential duties of her own occupation during the first two years of disability    •  The policyowner’s inability to perform the essential duties of any occupation for which she is reasonably entitled by education, training, or experience after the first two years of disability |

One other disability benefit offered with some life insurance policies is a disability income benefit.

If the insured becomes totally disabled, after a waiting period of three to six months, the insurance company pays a monthly disability income. This benefit typically contains a definition of total disability that is the same as the definition in a waiver of premium (WP) for disability benefit.

The amount of the monthly income benefit is a stated percentage, often one percent, of the policy’s face amount. Some DI benefits state either a maximum monthly benefit amount payable or a maximum benefit period or both.

Some disability income benefit riders also provide a benefit if the insured becomes partially disabled.

Identify the supplemental disability benefit rider with its example.

The UL insurance policy Drew Stephens owns on his life includes a rider that will pay him a monthly benefit if he becomes totally disabled.

Disability income benefit rider

Waiver of premium for payor rider

Waiver of premium for disability rider

Answer: Disability income benefit rider

A rider on Mary Margaret Satterfield’s insurance policy specifies that her insurer will pay her renewal premiums if she becomes disabled.

Disability income benefit rider

Waiver of premium for payor rider

Waiver of premium for disability rider

Answer: Waiver of premium for disability rider

3. A rider on Benjamin Mumford’s policy covering the life of his daughter provides that the insurer will waive the policy’s renewal premiums if Mr. Mumford dies or becomes totally disabled.

Disability income benefit rider

Waiver of premium for payor rider

Waiver of premium for disability rider

Answer: Waiver of premium for payor rider

Describe the characteristics of an accidental death benefit rider.

People who believe they may have a greater likelihood of a fatal accident than most people can purchase an accidental death benefit that provides an additional death benefit if an accident causes the insured’s death.

Why is an accidental death benefit sometimes called a double indemnity benefit?

It frequently doubles the policy’s face amount

It requires two forms of proof that the death was accidental

It doubles the premium for the coverage

Answer: It frequently doubles the policy’s face amount

Explanation: Frequently, the amount of an accidental death benefit is equal to the face amount of the life insurance policy, which doubles the total face amount payable when the insured dies in an accident. However, the accidental death benefit may be some other multiple of the policy’s face amount—such as three times the face amount—or it may be unrelated to the face amount.

Sometimes it’s hard to determine if an accident was actually the cause of death.

Accidental death benefit riders typically include certain exclusions. In other words, the insurer will not pay accidental death benefits if the insured’s death results from certain stated causes. Which of the following causes of death do you think are typically excluded? (Choose all that apply.)

Death resulting from suicide

Death as a result of war-related accidents

Aviation-related accidents other than as a passenger on an airplane

Death occurring during the commission of a crime

Anwser: All is correct

Explanation: All of these causes are typically excluded. Note that the exclusions relate only to the accidental death benefit. Often times, the policy’s basic death benefit is still payable.

Submit

Some life insurance policies include an accidental death and dismemberment benefit. Do you think that such policies pay two benefits: one for death caused by an accident and an additional benefit if the deceased is dismembered in the accident?

Yes

No

Sometimes

Anwser: No

Explanation: Only one benefit or the other applies, not both. Let’s see how it works.

The accidental death and dismemberment (AD&D) benefit pays an accidental death benefit if an insured dies by accidental means or it pays a dismemberment benefit to an insured if an accident causes the insured to lose any two limbs or the sight in both eyes.

The dismemberment benefit amount is same as accidental death benefit

A smaller benefit may be payable for loss of one limb or one eye

Kevin Walter was the policyowner-insured of a $75,000 life insurance policy that included a typical $75,000 accidental death and dismemberment (AD&D) benefit rider. While his policy was in force, Mr. Walter, who had been diagnosed with terminal stomach cancer, died in a traffic accident.

If Mr. Walter’s insurance company determines that the accident was the cause of his death, the total death benefit payable to the policy’s beneficiary would be

0

$75,000

$150,000

Answer: $150,000

Explanation: Because the accident caused Mr. Walter’s death, his beneficiary is entitled to the accidental death benefit as well as the basic death benefit.

If Mr. Walter from the previous question had survived the accident but had lost one arm and one leg, the benefit rider would have provided him with a dismemberment benefit of

0

$75,000

$150,000

Answer: $75,000

Explanation: The AD&D rider provides for a stated benefit amount (in this case $75,000) if an accident causes the insured to lose any two limbs or the sight in both eyes.

Identify three types of accelerated death benefit riders and recognize the differences among those riders.

An accelerated death benefit (living benefit) allows a policyowner access to some or all of a life insurance policy’s death benefit in certain circumstances while the insured is alive.

An accelerated death benefit can be added to just about any life insurance policy.

However, to keep administrative costs down, some insurers limit the benefit to policies with a large face amount, typically over $100,000:

Payment reduces death benefit to the beneficiary

Usually, paid in a lump sum that is 25% to 75% of the policy face amount

Let’s see how accelerated death benefits work.

Close Transcript

Gloria:

Policy services, this is Gloria, how may I help you?

Ellen:

Hi Gloria, my name is Ellen Hall. My mother Carmen Stevens has a life insurance policy with you. She’s very sick, breast cancer. Her doctor doesn’t think she has very much longer.

Gloria:

I’m very sorry to hear about your mother. I can imagine how difficult this time has been for you.

Ellen:

Thank you, it’s been very rough. We’ve been trying to stay positive but sometimes, well it’s hard.

Gloria:

I am sure.

Ellen:

She has a lot of bills that have piled up. I mean more than we can handle. A lot of medical stuff. There have been so many procedures, poor thing, and well not every dime has been covered. Some bill collectors have started calling and they can be so awful.

Gloria:

I understand.

Ellen:

When mom’s old bridge partner got really sick she was able to get some money from her life insurance before she died. Is that possible with my mom’s policy? Anything that she could get now would really take the pressure off.

Gloria:

Let’s see if we can help you out. Your mom’s friend was referring to an accelerated death benefit rider which would be attached to the main policy. Maybe you have heard the term “living benefit”?

Ellen:

I think that sounds familiar.

Gloria:

That’s another term for accelerated death benefit because it pays a policy benefit to the insured person while they are still living. There’s several different kinds of accelerated death benefits but the most common is a terminal illness benefit.

Ellen:

So do you have to be terminally ill to collect?

Gloria:

That’s right. The TI rider pays for part of the death benefit if the insured has less than 12 months to live. Is that the case for your mother?

Ellen:

Uh, yes, her oncologist is telling us three to six months.

Gloria:

Ok, I’m checking your mother’s policy now and it does have a terminal illness benefit rider attached.

Ellen:

That is great news.

Gloria:

Normally to add a terminal illness benefit the policy’s face value must be 100,000 or above. Your mother’s policy is 250,000.

Ellen:

Can she get all 250,000 now?

Gloria:

I’m afraid not. Usually the policy owner can get up to 75 percent of the face amount, that’s 187,500 in your mom’s case.

Ellen:

That still would be a great help.

Gloria:

If you would like to proceed I can send you a TI benefit election form. We’ll also need your mom’s policy and a statement from her doctor certifying her life expectancy. Normally we pay the TI benefit in a lump sum.

Ellen:

Yes, please start the process. And thank you so much for your help. This is the first good news we’ve had in a while.

Gloria:

My pleasure.

The most common type of accelerated death benefit is the terminal illness (TI) benefit, which pays a portion of the policy’s death benefit if the insured has a terminal illness with a physician-certified life expectancy of less than a specified period, usually 12 or 24 months.

Although TI benefits are commonly used to pay for medical care, living expenses, or outstanding debts, they can be used for any purpose, such as travel expenses.

Two other forms of accelerated death benefits:

Dread disease benefit

Long-term care insurance benefit

A dread disease (DD) benefit (critical illness benefit) pays a portion of a policy’s face amount if the insured suffers from one of several diseases listed in the benefit’s contract wording.

The benefit can be paid in a lump sum or in installments over 6 to 12 months.

An insured becomes eligible for DD benefits when he or she has a disease or undergoes a medical procedure identified as a DD in the supplementary benefit. Which of the following diseases or procedures do you think typically qualifies an insured for DD benefits? (Choose all that apply.)

Life-threatening cancer

Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome (AIDS)

Diabetes

End-stage kidney failure

Heart attack

Stroke

Multiple sclerosis

Coronary bypass surgery

Answer: All, except diabetes and multiple sclerosis

Explanation: Of these diseases and medical procedures, only diabetes and multiple sclerosis are not typically considered dread diseases. In most cases; the remaining diseases and medical conditions would qualify for the DD benefit under most policies.

Submit

The long-term care (LTC) insurance benefit is an accelerated benefit payable if the insured requires extended LTC that meets policy provisions.

Premiums on basic life insurance policy and the LTC benefit rider—waived while insured receives LTC

Waiting period—typically 90 days

Monthly LTC benefit—stated as a percentage of policy face amount such as 2 percent

Payments stop when specified percentage (50 to 100 percent) of policy benefit has been paid out

Let's review long term care.

Long-term care is a range of services that meet a person's medical and personal needs in a variety of settings for an extended time. Most long-term care supports activities of daily living, such as dressing, bathing, and using the bathroom.

Long-term care benefits cover nursing home care, assisted living care, home health care, and adult daycare.

Accelerated death benefits are payable only after the death of the policyowner-insured.

True

False

Answer: False

Explanation: Accelerated death benefit riders provide that, under certain conditions, a policyowner can receive all or part of the policy’s death benefit before the insured’s death.

Identify the type of accelerated death benefit to its example.

Kyle Zabriski’s accelerated death benefit rider will pay him because he is suffering from kidney failure, which is an affliction specified in the rider.

Dread disease benefit rider

Long-term care benefit rider

Terminal illness benefit rider

Answer: Dread disease benefit rider
