Describe the purpose and operation of benefit schedules in group life insurance policies.

A benefit schedule shows how much group life insurance coverage each group insured receives.

The benefit schedule states either the dollar amount of coverage or the method the insurer will use to determine that dollar amount.

section: Three Common Types of Benefit Schedules for Employee Groups
subsection: Benefit formula based on annual salary
text:
| Annual Salary | Amount of life insurance |
|---|---|
| Less than $60,000 | 1 x salary |
| $60,000 to $100,000 | 2 x salary |
| Over $100,000 | 3 x salary |

### Benefit is a flat amount for every employee
| Annual Salary | Amount of life insurance |
|---|---|
| Any | $50,000 |

### Benefit amount based on job classification
| Annual Salary | Amount of life insurance |
|---|---|
| Nonmanagement | $30,000 |
| Supervisors | $50,000 |
| Managers | $100,000 |
| Officers | $200,000 |

Dependent coverage amounts are listed in a separate benefit schedule.

Examples:

A flat amount of coverage for each covered dependent.

A flat amount of coverage for a group member’s spouse and a lower amount for each dependent child.

Some insurance laws—and insurer underwriting guidelines—require that dependent coverage be less than the covered group member’s coverage.

The Daisy Company offers group life insurance to its 100 employees as part of its employee benefit plan. The table below presents information about two employee who work at Daisy:

| Group Life Insurance |  |  |  |  |  |  |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Employee | Job Title | Age | Salary | Employed | Amount of Life Insurance | Dependents |
| John Warren | Manager | 60 | $80,000 | 8 years | $160,000 | Spouse |
| Todd Addison | Welder | 24 | $25,000 | 3 years | $25,000 | None |

The benefit schedule in Daisy's group life insurance policy is based on

A standard flat amount

An employee's annual salary

Can’t tell; need more information

If Daisy's policy is typical, then the amount of coverage provided for John's wife is

Greater than the amount of coverage provided for John

Less than the amount of coverage provided for John

Usually, insurers pay group life insurance policy death benefits in a lump sum to the named beneficiary. But some group policies allow a beneficiary to choose a settlement option.

The settlement options are the same as those in individual policies (click or touch each tab to review that settlement option):

Lump-sum option: The insurer pays the proceeds in a single payment.

Interest option: The insurer invests the policy proceeds and periodically pays interest on the proceeds.

Fixed-period option: The insurer pays the policy proceeds in equal installments for a specified period of time.

Fixed-amount option: The insurer pays equal installments of a stated amount until the policy proceeds, plus the interest earned, are exhausted.

Life income option

Identify the features of group term life insurance plans, group accidental death and dismemberment plans, and group cash value plans.

Describe how group creditor life insurance policies differ from policies issued to other types of groups.

Like most group life policies, The Stetson Company’s policy is yearly renewable term (YRT) insurance. The coverage lasts for one year.

Group YRT policies do not build cash values.

The insurer can change the premium rate at the end of each policy year.

Typically, an employee can receive up to $50,000 of noncontributory group term insurance coverage tax free. An employee must pay federal income taxes on the employer's cost of providing coverage over $50,000.

A group policy may provide accidental death and dismemberment (AD&D) benefits. Or a group policyholder may have separate group life and group AD&D policies.

Jerome’s group life coverage includes a $50,000 accidental death benefit. If he dies as the result of an accident, the named beneficiary will receive

$50,000 YRT + $50,000 AD&D = $100,000

Some group life policies provide cash value insurance rather than YRT coverage.

Usually, cash value life insurance is supplemental coverage. Eligible group members have the option to buy cash value insurance in addition to their group YRT coverage.

Three common types of group cash value (permanent) life insurance plans:

Group Paid-Up

Group Level-Premium Whole Life

Group Universal Life

Group paid-up plans combine whole life insurance with decreasing amounts of term life insurance.

### Group Paid Up Plan Example
Ben Song has $100,000 of total life insurance coverage through a contributory group paid-up plan.

Ben’s premiums purchase paid-up whole life insurance. His employer’s premiums purchase YRT life insurance. Last year, Ben’s premiums purchased $10,000 of paid-up whole life. Ben’s employer paid for $90,000 of YRT.

Over time, as his paid-up whole life insurance coverage increases, the amount of his YRT coverage decreases. Together, Ben will always have $100,000 of coverage. Ben’s paid-up whole life coverage remains in force even if he leaves the company.

section: Group Level Premium Whole Life Plans
text:
Group level premium whole life plans provide level premium whole life insurance.

The plan can be contributory or noncontributory. If the employee contributes, she has a vested right in the amount of the cash value provided by her premium contributions.

section: Group Level Premium Whole Life Plans
subsection: Group Level Premium Whole Life Example
text:
Violet Young is insured by a noncontributory group level premium whole life insurance policy. Her whole life coverage will be paid-up at age 65, her normal retirement age.

At retirement, her employer will use the cash value of her paid-up coverage to provide her a retirement income.

If Violet leaves the group before her retirement, her group life insurance coverage will terminate and any accumulated cash value belongs to her employer.

If the policy had been contributory, and Violet had left the group before retirement, she would be entitled to a portion of the accumulated value. For example, if she had paid 25 percent of the premiums for the coverage, she would be entitled to 25 percent of the accumulated value.

Group universal life plans provide universal life insurance and covered group members usually pay the entire premium.

### Group Universal Life Example
Walt Hart’s employer provides a group universal life insurance plan that operates much like an individual universal life insurance policy. Walt chooses the premium amount he wants to pay, which affects the amount of the policy’s cash value.

Walt’s coverage is portable; if he leaves his job, he can continue his coverage under the group plan.

Jerome has a credit card issued by the Swift Bank. Jerome is insured under Swift’s group creditor life insurance policy.

At any time, the amount of insurance Jerome has on his life equals the amount he owes on the credit card account.

Does Jerome get to name the beneficiary of his group creditor life insurance coverage through Swift Bank?

Yes

No

Pat McLain is covered under a noncontributory group life insurance plan offered by his employer, The Chestnut Company. Chestnut's policy provides Pat with $100,000 of group term life insurance. Does Pat have to pay federal income taxes on any of Chestnut’s cost for providing his coverage?

No, group life insurance benefits are never taxable.

Yes, Pat must pay federal income taxes on Chestnut’s cost of providing $50,000 of coverage.

Yes, Pat must pay federal income taxes on Chestnut’s cost of providing $100,000 of coverage.

Sean Cranston is covered under a typical group paid-up life insurance plan through his employer, The Neptune Company.

Neptune’s group plan combines paid-up whole life insurance with

Decreasing amounts of term life insurance

Increasing amounts of term life insurance

If Sean retires or leaves the group, his paid-up whole life coverage will

Terminate

Remain in force

Emily Thompson signed up for the group universal life insurance plan offered by her employer, the Dayshore Company.

If this plan is typical, who pays the premiums for Emily’s coverage?

Emily

Dayshore

Is Emily’s UL coverage portable?

Yes

No

Learning Objectives

After studying this lesson, you will be able to:

Describe the basic elements of employer-sponsored retirement plans: who is involved, why the plans exist, and why insurers are ideally suited to meet this market need.

Identify the basic requirements of qualified employer-sponsored retirement plans in the United States.

Distinguish between defined benefit and defined contribution retirement plans.

Identify and describe different types of defined contribution plans.

List and describe retirement plans available to small employers in the United States.

Describe the basic elements of employer-sponsored retirement plans: who is involved, why the plans exist, and why insurers are ideally suited to meet this market need.

Retirement security is an important financial need. Recall individual annuities and individual retirement arrangements (IRAs)? People can purchase those products to provide them with an income after they retire.

In addition, people often receive retirement income from private retirement plans sponsored by employers or labor unions as well as from government programs like the Social Security program in the United States.

What about outside the United States? Countries outside the United States offer combinations of government-sponsored, employer-sponsored, and personal retirement plans.

Can you think of any reasons why insurance companies are well-suited to assisting employers or labor unions with their pension plans?

Insurers have expertise in…

Long-term investing

Managing group benefit plans

Managing the risk associated with lifetime payments

The Stetson Company offers a retirement plan to its employees.

In this arrangement, Stetson is the plan sponsor. The entity that establishes and maintains a retirement plan is usually

An employer

A union

Plan participants can be employees or union members.

Carla says, "We’re plan participants—we’ve chosen to participate in Stetson’s retirement plan."

Insurers seek to satisfy the retirement needs of two different groups—the organizational customer or plan sponsor, and the individual customers or plan participants.

Why do employers offer retirement plans?

To attract valuable employees

For tax advantages for themselves and participating employees

To assist employees in saving for retirement

All of these reasons

Carla says,"I’m the plan administrator of our retirement plan. Stetson appointed me to handle the operation of the plan.

"I maintain records, prepare required reports for governmental agencies, and give plan participants information about the plan."

The plan sponsor decides what type of retirement plan to establish and what the terms of the plan will be. The terms of the plan are set out in a legal agreement—the plan document.

Plan participants receive a summary plan description that informs them of their rights under the plan.

Retirement plans can have the following sources of funds:

Contributions made by the plan sponsor

Contributions made by the plan participants

Investment/interest earnings on all contributions

Identify all the parties that can serve as the plan sponsor of a group retirement plan. (Choose all that apply.)

Employers

Employees

Unions

Submit

Identify the role(s) of each entity in Stetson’s retirement plan. (Choose all that apply.)

HR Manager Carla Abernathy

Plan Sponsor

Plan Administrator

Plan Participant

Submit

Jerome Tate

Plan Sponsor

Plan Administrator

Plan Participant

The Stetson Company

Plan Sponsor

Plan Administrator

Plan Participant

Identify the basic requirements of qualified employer-sponsored retirement plans in the United States.

The U.S. government offers tax advantages to plan participants and to employers that set up retirement plans according to certain guidelines.

To become a qualified plan and receive favorable tax treatment, a plan must meet requirements set out in federal tax laws and the Employee Retirement Income Security Act (ERISA).

What are ERISA requirements? Let’s look at a few of them.

A qualified retirement plan can’t favor highly compensated employees

Plan assets must be held in a trust account at a financial institution for the exclusive benefit of employees or their beneficiaries

The plan sponsor must provide certain reports to plan participants and governmental agencies

The plan must meet certain minimum vesting requirements regarding a participant’s right to receive plan benefits

Employees are immediately vested in 100 percent of their own retirement plan contributions.

Employer contributions vest according to one of two types of vesting schedules:

Graded vesting (or step vesting)

Cliff vesting

Graded Vesting

Stetson Co. employees are vested in 20% of Stetson's contributions after one year. Each year they vest in an additional 20% until they are fully vested in Year 5.

Cliff Vesting

Dosun Engineering employees have no vested interest in the employer's contributions until the end of Year 3, when they become 100 percent vested.

To be considered a qualified retirement plan under ERISA, a retirement plan must (choose all that apply)

Favor highly compensated employees

Provide certain reports to governmental agencies

Allow plan participants to be immediately vested in employer contributions

Submit

The following companies have qualified retirement plans for their employees. Select the correct type of vesting schedule for each example.

At Teldar Company, employees become 25% vested in Teldar’s contributions at the end of Year 1, 50% vested at the end of Year 2, 75% vested at the end of Year 3, and 100% vested at the end of Year 4.

Cliff vesting schedule

Graded vesting schedule

At the Portrait Company, employees become completely vested in retirement benefits funded by Portrait’s contributions at the end of year 3. Before that time, they have no vested interest in Portrait’s contributions.

Cliff vesting schedule

Graded vesting schedule

Distinguish between defined benefit and defined contribution retirement plans.

Qualified employer-sponsored retirement plans are generally divided into two types:

A defined benefit (DB) plan, often referred to as a pension plan, states the amount of the periodic benefit each plan participant will receive at retirement. A formula in the plan typically provides either a dollar amount or a percentage amount according to years of service or other objective factors.

A defined contribution (DC) plan specifies the contributions that the plan sponsor agrees to make to the plan. The benefit that a participant will receive is not determined in advance of the participant’s retirement but depends on the investment performance of the funds in the plan.

Carla says, "One of the great things about The Stetson Company is that they still offer a pension plan. That’s a defined benefit plan that will provide me a monthly income starting at my retirement and continuing for the rest of my life—in other words, a pension."

Key features:

The plan sponsor must set aside enough money to fund the promised benefits—the formula does not tell the sponsor how much that will cost.

The plan sponsor is obligated to fund, in advance, at least a portion of the plan's specified benefits each year.

The plan sponsor may deduct the amount it contributes on behalf of plan participants from its current taxable income as a business expense in the year it makes the contributions.

The plan sponsor assumes all investment risk.

The plan participant does not make contributions.

The plan sponsor must choose a funding vehicle for investing the retirement plan’s assets. Insurers offer a variety of funding vehicles, including group annuities.

Carla says, "There are several different defined benefit formulas—and you tend to need a math degree to understand them. In Stetson’s pension plan, the pension benefit is based on the employee’s salary in the years just prior to retirement and how long the employee worked at the company."

The longer an employee works for a company and the higher his salary, the higher the monthly income payment.

That means the pension benefit is based on the employee’s highest-earning years.

A DB plan is a terrific benefit for employees! Here’s why:

Plan participants do not have to contribute their own money to the plan; only plan sponsors make contributions

A plan participant typically gets a monthly retirement benefit—usually provided by an annuity

The retirement benefit payments are guaranteed to continue for the participant’s lifetime

Investment professionals manage and allocate pension fund investments

A participant’s benefit is guaranteed up to certain limits in the United States by the Pension Benefit Guaranty Corporation (PBGC), a unit of the federal government that insures pension plans

But many companies have abandoned or frozen their existing DB plans, and hardly any employers are establishing new DB plans. Why?

DB plans are typically expensive to set up and administer—even for the largest companies.

Plan costs are determined by actuarial assumptions relating to employer contributions and investment earnings that are difficult to predict from year to year. In years in which plan investments don’t perform as well as expected, the employer has to contribute more to the plan than anticipated, which can hurt the company’s financial results.

DB benefits aren’t portable; however, an employee will receive vested benefit amounts from the employer upon retirement.

DB benefit calculations are difficult for employees to understand.

section: Shift From Defined Benefit Plans to Defined Contribution Plans
text: See as this industry expert explains why most companies have replaced their defined benefit pension plans with defined contribution plans and how the insurance industry has responded to the shift.

Because companies want to offer a retirement benefit to their employees, but may not want to set up a DB plan, many have turned to defined contribution (DC) plans.

Remember, a DC plan specifies only what goes into the plan, not what will be paid out. The exact retirement benefit isn’t known until the employee retires and begins withdrawing funds.

The plan sponsor establishes an account for each employee.

Employer contributions on behalf of an employee are allocated to that employee’s account.

Investment earnings as well as expenses, are also allocated to a participant’s account.

The amount of a participant’s retirement benefit depends on the participant’s account balance and so cannot be accurately predicted very far in advance of the retirement date.

section: Tax Advantages of Defined Contribution Plans
text:
There are limits, of course, but generally:

A plan sponsor may deduct the amount it contributes on behalf of plan participants from its current taxable income as a business expense

Plan participants’ contributions are typically made on a tax-deferred basis—which means they don’t pay taxes on contributions or earnings until they are withdrawn from the plan

A plan sponsor’s contributions on behalf of a participant are not considered current taxable income to the participant until the participant withdraws those contributions from the plan.

Some DC plans include a Roth contribution feature that allows employees to make after-tax contributions in place of all or part of pre-tax contributions. We’ll explain Roth contributions later in the lesson.

section: Tax Advantages of Defined Contribution Plans
subsection: Do other countries provide tax advantages for private pension plans?
text:
Countries use different methods of providing tax advantages.

For each of the methods below, an “E” stands for “exempt” and a “T” stands for “taxed”. The first letter indicates the tax status of “contributions”, the second letter indicates the tax status of “investment income and capital gains”, and the third letter indicates the tax status of “benefits”.

The three primary methods of providing tax advantages are:

|  |  |
|---|---|
| Exempt | Contributions |
| Exempt | Investment income and capital gains |
| Taxed | Benefits |

|  |  |
|---|---|
| Taxed | Contributions |
| Exempt | Investment income and capital gains |
| Taxed | Benefits |

|  |  |
|---|---|
| Taxed | Contributions |
| Exempt | Investment income and capital gains |
| Exempt | Benefits |

The majority of countries use some variation of the EET system. For example, the tax treatment of contributions may vary depending on the source of the contribution—whether made by employee or employer. Most countries exempt from taxation investment income and capital gains. Source

section: Defined Contribution Plans vs. Defined Benefit Plans
text:
Here are ways that DC plans differ from DB plans.

DC participants typically control the investment of their retirement funds but also assume the investment risk

DC participants have the potential of a higher investment return than is generally earned by a DB plan

DC participants can often contribute, within limits, extra amounts of money to boost the balance of their accounts

DC participants may have emergency access to the funds in their accounts before retirement

The Stetson Company has a qualified defined benefit pension plan. Select all true statements about a defined benefit pension plan.

Stetson sets up an individual account for each employee.

If investments perform poorly, Stetson must set aside additional amounts to ensure that the plan is funded.

The amount of each plan participant’s retirement benefit is determined in advance.

Plan participants can make contributions to the plan.

Plan participants direct the investment choices.

Submit

Indicate whether a company looking for the following advantages would be better off selecting a defined benefit or a defined contribution retirement plan.

More predictable costs for the employer

DC

DB

Government guarantees for retirement benefits in the event the retirement plan terminates without sufficient money to pay retirement benefits

DC

DB

Guaranteed retirement income for life

DC

DB

section: Defined Contribution Plans vs. Defined Benefit Plans
text:
Identify and describe different types of defined contribution plans.

If you live in the United States, chances are you’ve heard of a 401(k). A 401(k) is an example of a

Defined contribution plan

Defined benefit plan

There are several types of defined contribution plans that differ in pretty significant ways. Stetson’s plan is a common type of defined contribution plan known as a 401(k) plan. Other types of defined contribution plans include profit sharing plans and stock bonus plans. Let’s take a closer look at the traditional 401(k) plan first.

A 401(k) plan allows employees to make pre-tax contributions to the plan. Employees enter into a salary reduction arrangement so that the employer can deduct contributions from their salaries.

(Below, click or touch the arrow to the right.)

Jerome says, "Salary reduction? That sounds a little scary. We need every bit of my salary to make ends meet. I might have to pass on this particular benefit."

First, employee contributions to a traditional 401(k) are pre-tax contributions. That means the contributions come out of the employee’s salary before income taxes are calculated, so they’re not taxable in the year they are made.

Pre-tax contributions reduce the amount of the employee’s taxable income and therefore reduce the employee’s current tax bill.

Second, by contributing to the plan, an employee may receive employer matching contributions. For example, Stetson will contribute to Jerome’s 401(k) account, but only if he contributes.

The employer contribution can equal the employee's contribution, or be a percentage of that amount, subject to a specified maximum.

Employer contributions are not considered current taxable income to the participant until the participant withdraws them from the plan.

The Stetson Company matches 50% of employee contributions up to 6 percent of the employee’s salary. So if Jerome contributes 4 percent of his salary, Stetson contributes 50% of that, or 2 %.

(Click or touch the arrow to the right.)

Jerome says, "So, if I don’t contribute, I’m actually giving up money that Stetson is willing to pay me."

Participants in a traditional 401(k) plan choose how to invest the amounts in their retirement accounts. Do you think participants pay taxes on their investment earnings?

Yes, taxes are paid on investment earnings in the year in which they occur

Yes, taxes are paid on investment earnings when they are withdrawn from the plan

No, participants never pay taxes on qualified retirement plan investment earnings

Tax deferral is a good thing. Earnings grow faster than if they were taxed each year.

And, if the participant is in a lower tax bracket after retirement (which is common), the earnings will be taxed at a lower rate.

At retirement, the employee pays income taxes on pre-tax contributions and investment earnings when he withdraws them from the plan.

Early withdrawals—before age 59½ —are possible, but the participant usually incurs an additional penalty tax.

So I can't access my money without penalty before retirement?

Start

401(k) plans typically allow a participant to take loans from his vested account balance, subject to regulatory and plan limits. Typically the loan interest rate is low, for example, prime rate + 1%.

However, the participant must repay the loan with after-tax dollars through payroll deductions.

In a 401(k) plan, an employee can contribute to the plan only if the employer contributes to the employee’s account.

True

False

Carla Abernathy elected to defer 8% of her wages each pay period to Stetson’s qualified defined contribution retirement plan. Stetson contributes 4% to the retirement plan on behalf of Carla. By deferring a part of her compensation, Carla’s current tax liability will

Increase

Decrease

Carla must pay taxes each year on the amount that Stetson contributes on her behalf to her qualified retirement plan.

True

False

Carla will have to pay taxes on her investment earnings

In the year the earnings occur

When she withdraws them from the plan

Never

Jerome says, "What was that about a “traditional” 401(k) plan? Is there another type?"

Starting in 2006, U.S. employers were permitted to add Roth 401(k) features to their 401(k) plans, in which participants can elect to have all or a portion of their contributions deducted from their gross income after taxes. With this feature, employees can receive tax-free distributions of contributions and earnings at retirement.

Traditional 401(k) employee contributions are pre-tax—that is, contributions are deducted from an employee’s earnings before income taxes are calculated. However, amounts are taxed upon withdrawal.

Roth 401(k) employee contributions are deducted from an employee’s earnings after taxes—that is, taxes are paid before the contribution is calculated. But, amounts generally aren’t taxable when withdrawn.

So, with either type, you’re going to pay taxes on your contributions, it’s just a matter of when.

Right.

Roth 401(k) plan contributions are taxed sooner.

Traditional 401(k) contributions are taxed later.

|  | Traditional 401(k) | Roth 401(k) |
|---|---|---|
| Employee Pre-Tax Contributions? | Yes, subject to limits | No |
| Employee Post-Tax Contributions? | May be allowed, in addition to pre-tax | All contributions are post-tax, subject to limits |
| Income tax paid on contributions when they are withdrawn? | Yes, on pre-tax contributions | No |
| Income tax paid on investment earnings when they are withdrawn? | Yes | No, provided certain conditions are met |

A 401(k) plan with a Roth feature may offer an advantage that a traditional 401(k) plan doesn’t offer. What do you think it is?

A plan with a Roth feature offers tax-free accumulation of investment earnings

A plan with a Roth feature offers a tax-deferred accumulation of investment earnings

A plan with a Roth feature guarantees no loss of principal in a participant’s account balance

Roth 401(k) investment earnings can be withdrawn tax-free and penalty-free IF

• The account has been open for at least five years, AND

• The account owner

Is at least age 59 ½ OR

Has died OR

Has become disabled OR

Is a first-time homebuyer.

### Tax-Free Withdrawals
Two types of plans similar to the 401(k):

403(b) plan—primarily for school and college employees and many non-profit organizations

457 plan—primarily for government employees

Now let’s talk about those other two types of defined contribution plans.

A profit sharing plan is a DC plan in which employer contributions to employees’ retirement accounts are based on the company’s profits.

The amount of contributions varies from year to year.

Contributions are at the employer’s discretion; some years, there may be no contributions.

To be a qualified plan, a profit sharing plan must have contributions that are substantial and recurring.

A stock bonus plan is a DC plan in which the plan sponsor makes contributions on behalf of participants in the form of the corporation's stock.

Contributions are not tied to corporate profits

The value of the stock fluctuates

Identify each type of plan by the description.

Acme Corporation’s plan allows full-time employees to make pre-tax contributions from their salary. Acme matches employee contributions up to a maximum of 3% of each employee’s salary.

Profit sharing plan

Stock bonus plan

Pension plan

401(k) plan

Beacon Corporation contributes to its plan in the form of shares of the corporation's stock.

Profit sharing plan

Stock bonus plan

Pension plan

401(k) plan

Compton Company’s plan provides retired employees with a lifetime monthly income benefit.

Profit sharing plan

Stock bonus plan

Pension plan

401(k) plan

Denton Corporation’s plan contributions come from corporate profits, and the company can decide to make no contributions when profits are weak.

Profit sharing plan

Stock bonus plan

Pension plan

401(k) plan

List and describe retirement plans available to small employers in the United States.

Many small business owners and self-employed people are discouraged by the costs and complexity of setting up and managing a qualified retirement plan.

For many employers, the ERISA requirements they must meet to get the tax benefits are a big deal.

Burdensome. Expensive. Complicated.

So, can only big companies have retirement plans?

NO!

Small businesses and the self-employed have several retirement plan options that do not have to comply fully with ERISA, including

SEP

SIMPLE IRA

SIMPLE 401(k)

Keogh Plan

A SEP (simplified employee pension) is a plan that allows a business of any size (including the self-employed) to make contributions up to a stated limit to an IRA for each participating employee.

SEP = Simplified Employee Pension and IRA = Individual Retirement Arrangement

Key points:

A SEP is funded by employer contributions only

An employee is always 100% vested in his SEP IRA

A SIMPLE IRA is a tax-advantaged retirement plan for employers with 100 or fewer employees (including the self-employed). SIMPLE IRAs include employee and employer contributions, and they use IRAs to fund the benefits for plan participants.

Key points:

SIMPLE IRA is funded by both employer and employee contributions

An employee is always 100% vested in all SIMPLE IRA contributions

Employer contributions are excluded from the employee’s current taxable income

A SIMPLE 401(k) is a simplified 401(k) plan for employers with 100 or fewer employees.

Key points:

Plan functions much like a traditional 401(k) plan with employer and employee contributions up to a stated maximum

Employer contributions are excluded from the employee’s current taxable income.

A Keogh plan is a qualified retirement plan intended primarily for self-employed people. A Keogh plan can be set up as either a DC plan or a DB plan, although most are DC plans.

Key points:

The business or individual makes annual tax-deductible contributions, subject to certain limits and conditions

Investment earnings are tax-deferred

Contribution limits are higher than for SEP and SIMPLE plans, but Keoghs are more complex to set up and administer

Keogh plan

SIMPLE 401(k)

SEP

Section 457 plan

SEP

An insurer’s overall direction is determined by its executives, with guidance from the board of directors. In this module, we explore how an insurer is organized and managed to balance the needs of a diverse set of stakeholder groups, and how an insurer manages the risk it must take in order to earn a return.

In this module, you’ll learn about

How an insurer’s board and executives manage the company ethically to meet the needs of various stakeholders

The ways an insurer can be organized for success

How an insurer manages the various risks it faces to earn an appropriate return

An insurer is governed by a board of directors, which gives broad guidance to management. But how do the board and management know if the company is succeeding? Learn how an insurer balances its obligations to multiple stakeholders, while adhering to the appropriate ethical standards.

Describe various stakeholder groups connected with an insurance company.

Explain the governance role of a company's board of directors, and describe the impact of governance on conflicting priorities.

Define ethics, and identify mechanisms companies employ to foster a culture of ethical behavior.

Learning Objective

Describe various stakeholder groups connected with an insurance company.

Welcome to LOMA 291: Improving the Bottom Line—Insurance Company Operations. My name is Grace Avon and I am CEO of Forthright Financial, a life insurance company. Like anybody, I want to be successful in my job and make sure that my company is successful as well. But how do I know if I am being successful? How do you measure a company's success?

Should I aim to maximize revenues, or profit, or stock price? Should I be most worried about our ratings from the ratings agencies, about satisfying government regulators, or about paying a dividend—to shareholders or to policyowners? Should I be aiming to create an amazing workplace full of happy employees, or a company that other companies and individuals love to do business with?

The answer to all these questions is "Yes!" But which factors should be the top priorities? That's a difficult question, and different people would probably give a different answer. As you'll see, Forthright must weigh the interests of several different groups. In this course, we'll start by examining how an insurance company decides what goals it will pursue. Then we'll discuss the roles of all the different areas of the company in achieving these goals.

Insurance Company Stakeholders

Lots of different groups have a stake in how Forthright Financial operates. We sometimes refer to these groups as stakeholders (A party that has an interest in how a company conducts its business. Also known as a constituent. )or constituents. Stakeholders are parties who have a stake in how Forthright conducts its business.

Let's meet some of these stakeholders.

Frank Pulaski—Forthright Stockholder

We stockholders expect the company to grow steadily. We expect to earn a reasonable return, sometimes in the form of dividends. Do you know what a dividend is? Sometimes the board of directors votes to make a payment to the stockholders. The payment is called a dividend (Compensation that a company may pay to owners upon a vote by the company's board of directors.)

A: What about mutual and fraternal companies

B: They don't issue corporate stock, so they don't have stockholders. They're owned by their policyowners.

A: So they don't issue dividends?

B: They don't issue stockholder dividends, but sometimes they issue policyowner dividends by action of the board of directors.

Start

Dale Jensen—Forthright Policyowner

Here are some factors I considered when I chose to buy a policy from Forthright:

Good company reputation

Policy features

Reasonable charges

Financial strength

Customer service

Forthright provides my personal safety net because of their good reputation and policy features. They charge me for reasonable expenses and they have the financial strength to be around for the long haul to pay the policy benefits they promise me and my family. So far, I'm pleased with my policy and the company's customer service.

"Mac" MacDonald—Financial Professional

As a financial professional (In insurance sales, any individual—including agents, bank-affiliated sales personnel, and brokers—who is licensed to sell insurance products, solicit sales, or negotiate insurance contracts.), I sell Forthright's products to the public. Some people call me an insurance agent because I have an agency relationship with Forthright. I'm a Forthright customer, too. Why do I place business with Forthright? Here are some reasons:

Forthright's products meet my clients' needs.

Forthright offers competitive compensation on the business I sell.

Its staff is top notch and provides fast and accurate support.

The company has good ratings, so I trust its ability to meet policy obligations in the future.

Khalid Husseini—Forthright Employee

I've been in customer service at Forthright for almost 15 years. I manage the customer service staff, and we're always busy. I like working with good people and in a reputable company. I appreciate Forthright's employee benefits and competitive compensation.

I value the long-term employment.

I enjoy my coworkers.

I'm proud to work for a reputable company.

I appreciate the salary and benefits.

Pol Rand—Reinsurer

I'm with Gargantuan Reinsurance. Reinsurance (Insurance that one insurance company, known as the direct writer, purchases from another insurance company, known as the reinsurer, to transfer all or part of the risk on insurance policies that the direct writer issued. )—that's insurance for insurance companies.

Gargantuan Re extends reinsurance to Forthright when Forthright wants our backup on claims. Because of reinsurance, Forthright can issue more insurance than it would without us. In some ways, we're like business partners.

Other Stakeholders

Forthright has other stakeholders.

Consultants/Vendors

I'm a public accountant. I provide external audit services to Forthright. Many vendors provide goods and services to Forthright.

Borrowers

I'm a real estate developer. To fund construction projects, I borrow from Forthright Financial. Forthright lends money as one of its investments. They expect to earn a return, so they keep a close eye on me.

Regulators

I conduct in-person examinations of insurance companies that do business in my state. I make sure they comply with regulations and maintain fair business practices.

Rating Agencies

I assign a grade to Forthright Financial and other companies. The grade, called a rating, indicates my company's opinion of Forthright's overall health and stability.

Associations

As a Forthright actuary, I belong to several organizations that set professional standards. Forthright employs many professionals—actuaries, accountants, auditors, attorneys, underwriters, medical directors, and others. All of these groups have professional associations.

Select each group which counts among an insurance company's stakeholders.

In each of the following questions, select the person who would most likely make each statement about his or her interests as a stakeholder.

Financial professionals

Reinsurance companies

Employees

Trade associations

Owners of company stock

Policyowners

All is correct

All of these groups and many more can be counted among the stakeholders of an insurance company!

Learning Objective

Explain the governance role of a company's board of directors, and describe the impact of governance on conflicting priorities.

Each stakeholder group has an interest in how the insurer operates—the decisions it makes and the actions it takes.

Sometimes stakeholder groups share the same company goals. But other times these groups have different interests and, as a result, different goals. For example, suppose Forthright has some capital—money, that is—and it needs to decide how to spend it. Some options could be

Declare a stockholder dividend

Declare a policyowner dividend

Purchase another company

Expand into a new product line

Which would you want the company to do?

Your answer depends on whether you're a stockholder, policyowner, employee, or financial professional. That's why the company needs to

Balance the interests of the different stakeholder groups and

Prioritize each group's interests, if necessary

Board of Directors

The owners elect a board of directors (A corporation's governing body, elected by the owners, that makes broad policy decisions to ensure that stakeholder interests are safeguarded appropriately. )—a "government" for the company. The directors are responsible for corporate governance (A corporate board's accountability, responsibility, and authority to direct an organization to properly fulfill its missions on behalf of legitimate stakeholders.) Governance refers to a board's responsibility and authority, as well as to management's accountability, to direct an organization to properly fulfill its missions on behalf of its owners and other legitimate stakeholders.

How do they safeguard stakeholder interests? Use the right arrows below to see some tools they can use.

Company Policy

Codes of Conduct

Organizational Structure

Compensation Arrangements and Bonus Pay

Organizational Control Tools

Board Transactions

A board's decisions—called policy decisions—give broad guidance to management. Typical policy-making responsibilities of Forthright's board of directors include

Authorizing a major business transaction, such as a merger

Declaring a dividend payable to owners

Setting a major company policy

Operating permanent committees of the board

Evaluating the company's operating results

Appointing executives

Setting compensation, including incentives, for executive officers

Board Committees

A company's board of directors meets regularly, but, outside of these meetings, permanent committees of the board do important work.

These committees set general guidelines and make decisions about how to direct Forthright's resources toward achieving corporate objectives.

Inside and Outside Directors

Forthright's board contains inside directors (A member of the board of directors who also holds a position within the company.) and outside directors (A member of the board of directors who does not hold a position within the company. Also known as an independent director.). Several of Forthright's senior executive officers are inside directors. Forthright's outside directors are business people, academic or medical professionals, and community leaders.

Why have both inside and outside directors?

Inside directors are knowledgeable about a company's operations.

Because of their experiences outside of the company, outside directors can provide a broader, less biased perspective than inside directors.

Both perspectives are important.

For the agenda for Forthright Financial's board meeting, select the items that are a board responsibility. Choose all that apply.

Place an advertising campaign in appropriate media

Authorize a major transaction, such as a merger

Declare a cash dividend payable to owners

Set a major policy for the company

Evaluate the company's operating results

Assemble financial reports requested as part of an audit

Set compensation, including incentives, for executives

Recruit financial professionals

Authorize a major transaction, such as a merger

Declare a cash dividend payable to owners

Set a major policy for the company

Evaluate the company's operating results

Set compensation, including incentives, for executives

The board of directors is responsible for making broad policy decisions that provide broad guidance to a company's management.

Can you identify the inside and outside directors on Forthright's board?

2.1. Hi. We've met before. I'm Chief Executive Officer of Forthright Financial. I chair the human resources committee and sit on the risk committee.

Inside Director

Outside Director

Inside Director

Grace is an inside director because she holds a position within Forthright Financial.

2.2. My name's Forrest. I'm retired from a career in securities law, so now my board positions for several companies are my career. I also chair the tax committee of Forthright's board.

Inside Director

Outside Director

Inside Director

Forrest is an outside director because he does not hold any other position with Forthright Financial.

Learning Objective

Define ethics, and identify mechanisms companies employ to foster a culture of ethical behavior.

Boards of directors fulfill their governance function, in part, by establishing a culture of ethical conduct. It's important for a company to show a true commitment to high ethical standards and for all employees—especially those at the top—to embrace these standards. If employees see their leaders display ethical behavior, they're more likely to trust the company and follow suit.

And companies with strong ethical cultures usually have more motivated and committed employees.

Corporate scandals can damage a company's reputation and cost millions in settlement payments. So when it comes to ethics, the old saying about "an ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure" couldn't be more true.

Ethics and Ethical Conduct

Ethics (A system of accepted standards of conduct and moral judgment that combines the elements of honesty, integrity, and fair treatment.) is a system of accepted standards of conduct and moral judgment. It includes honesty, integrity, and fair treatment.

For most companies, the basic ethical rules start with respecting stakeholders (including employees), competing fairly and within the law, and being a responsible corporate citizen

Here are some basic components of ethical corporate behavior:

Follow accepted legal and moral principles of right and wrong

Uphold promises to customers

Perform high-quality work in a timely fashion

Keep private information confidential

Support for Ethical Behavior

Use the right arrows below to look at some of the tools that companies like Forthright can employ to encourage integrity and principled business conduct.

An effective organizational structure

Systems of internal and external controls

A formal compensation system
Forthright's system for awarding bonus pay encourages employees to act ethically and also discourages them from acting contrary to Forthright's interests.

Learning and development opportunities for employees
Forthright promotes employee learning and development opportunities that emphasize compliance in the heavily regulated insurance industry.

Participation in industry associations and professional organizations
Forthright actively participates in industry associations that promote professionalism and good business conduct, including a self-regulatory market conduct program.

A code of conduct

Forthright's business must unfailingly meet the highest ethical and moral standards. Company success depends on principled business conduct and public confidence in our integrity.

Annually, Forthright's employees read and sign the code of conduct (A formal statement of a company's values and its expectations for the behavior of its employees. Also known as a code of business conduct or a code of ethics.), which is a statement of the principles employees should follow when representing the company. Every employee receives a wallet reminder card on this occasion.

Ethical Guidance

Many companies offer employees ready access to guidance in ethical matters. Click or touch each heading below to learn more.

Ethics Office

An ethics office offers qualified professionals who consult with employees about handling ethical issues.

Online Ethics Help

Provides company policies on:

Conduct

Inside information

Equal employment opportunity (EEO)

Sexual harassment

Privacy and data protection

Ombudsman

An ombudsman—an independent, impartial, and confidential professional with expertise in handling ethics problems.

Inside Information

Employees, in the course of performing a job, have access to inside information.

"Hey Todd, I've got a great stock tip for you."

Ethically and legally, employees may not unfairly profit by the misuse of inside information. Here are some examples:

Conducting a transaction in Forthright securities during a blackout period

Advising a third party about transactions in Forthright securities based on non-public information

Disclosing any non-public information to third parties

Employees need to keep inside information private, and companies need to provide employees with the resources they need to recognize inside information and understand the importance of keeping it private.

1. Which of the following would Forthright consider to be inside information? Choose all that apply.

Information published by a rating agency

Marketing strategies

Product design assumptions

Professional standards set by a trade association

Marketing strategies

Product design assumptions

Marketing strategies and product assumptions are examples of inside information. The other types of information are readily available to the public and are therefore not inside information.

Which of the following would Forthright consider to be inside information? Choose all that apply.

On your company's website, find the board of directors (usually under Investor Relations or Governance) and read about standing committees of the board.

Find your company's code of ethics (also known as a code of conduct or code of business conduct) and identify support tools to help employees interpret questions of proper conduct.

Identify elements your company uses to help define an ethical culture.
