An insurer’s overall direction is determined by its executives, with guidance from the board of directors. In this module, we explore how an insurer is organized and managed to balance the needs of a diverse set of stakeholder groups, and how an insurer manages the risk it must take in order to earn a return.

In this module, you’ll learn about

How an insurer’s board and executives manage the company ethically to meet the needs of various stakeholders

The ways an insurer can be organized for success

How an insurer manages the various risks it faces to earn an appropriate return

What is management? It is generally described as consisting of four functions. Companies differ in how they approach the four functions of management. Learn about the different ways to organize and run an insurance company.

Describe the four functions of management.

Recognize typical functional areas for insurance operations, and classify units as line or support functions.

Describe organizational concepts such as accountability, authority, chain of command, delegation, division of labor, and responsibility.

Characterize the distinct roles of executives, middle managers, and supervisors.

Describe how financial services companies use function, product, territory, customer, and distribution channel to structure their organization.

section: Planning and Organizing in an Insurance Company
text:
Learning Objectives

Describe the four functions of management.

Recognize typical functional areas for insurance operations, and classify units as line or support units.

Start

Every company needs to define who's in charge and who performs what tasks. An organizational structure sets these rules and focuses different individuals on one set of goals. But how do these rules apply to Forthright…or to any company for that matter? It's easy to see the daily contributions of employees like Todd and Rey, but how do leaders like the CEO and President contribute?

Leaders have vast responsibility. Most managers have jam packed schedules from the time they walk into the office until the time they leave. The functions of management are important to company operations. After all, management shapes an organization's vision. This involves planning for the future, organizing personnel, directing work activity, and delegating duties.

In this lesson, we'll explore how different management functions help an organization run smoothly.

One approach to understanding management is to consider the four functions of management:

PLANNING for desired outcomes

ORGANIZING tasks, employees, and other resources

DIRECTING employees and other resources

CONTROLLING work processes to ensure that actual results correspond to desired results

We'll start with planning.

Planning, Strategy, and Tactics

We all plan for the future and set goals, but not in the same elaborate way as large financial companies. A goal (A desired future outcome. Also called an objective) is a desired future outcome.

Planning (The process of preparing for the future by establishing appropriate goals and formulating strategies and activities for achieving those goals.) consists of identifying goals, and also the strategies and tactics the company will use to achieve those goals.

Strategies—long-term, high-level plans for achieving goals.

Tactics—activities to support strategies.

But who sets strategy and who sets tactics?

I set strategy. I'm an executive. To set strategy, executives consider the company's goals, resources, capabilities, and circumstances.

Tactics are my business. I'm a middle manager. Tactics are the specific tasks and activities employees will perform to support the company's strategies and achieve its goals.

Organizing

Organizing (The management function of establishing authority, responsibility, and accountability for accomplishing various company activities, including assembling people and other company resources.)  means defining a structure for an organization to enable it to meet its goals. Companies do this by setting up specialized organizational units to perform work activities.

A distinct type of work is often called a function (A distinct type of work or an aspect of operations or management that requires specialized knowledge or technical skill.). So, when organized so that each unit performs a distinct type of work, organizational units are often referred to as functional areas.

The types of work done by the different functional areas are classified as line functions (A department or other organizational unit that produces or administers products or services.) or support functions (A department or other organizational unit that provides support services to line functions or to other support functions but does not produce or administer products. Also called a staff function.).

Forthright has a major division for each line function and structures work units according to the work they perform. Classification of units as line or support functions—and reporting relationships between units—varies by company. Notably, Investment and Actuarial may be classified as line or support functions.

Line Functions in Insurance Companies

Use the right arrows below to learn about the line functions each work unit performs.

Product Development

Takes new products from concept to launch

Distribution and Sales

Oversees or performs the company's activities for making products available for sale

Customer Service

Assists customers and financial professionals by providing information, processing policy changes, administering financial transactions, maintaining records, and in other ways

Claims

Evaluates, processes, and pays claims for contractual benefits

Marketing

Defines who the company's customers are, what type of products it should offer, and how it should bring its customers and products together

Underwriting and New Business

Analyzes the risk presented by insurance applicants to assign an appropriate premium rate classification; issues policies

Reinsurance Administration

Manages the insurance company's relations with reinsurance providers and files reinsurance claims

Actuarial

Performs numerous activities to ensure that the company conducts its operations on a financially sound basis

Investment Management

Manages the company's investable assets according to company guidelines

Support Functions in Insurance Companies

Read below to learn about the support functions each work unit performs.

Accounting and Financial Reporting

Maintains the financial records of the company's businesses, prepares reports on the company's financial condition, and files required financial statements with regulators

Corporate Communications

Presents a consistent public image and message in all communications on behalf of the company

Finance

Monitors the company's overall financial strength and performance and acquires or uses funds (capital) to help the company accomplish its strategic goals

Information Technology

Develops and maintains computer systems and oversees information management

Risk Management

Coordinates the management of risk throughout the organization

Audit

Performs examinations or reviews to verify the accuracy of procedures or records

Legal and Compliance

Represents the company in all legal matters and ensures that the company's operations comply with all applicable laws and regulations everywhere the company operates

Human Resources

Manages employee hiring, training, development, evaluation, compensation, benefit administration, and terminations

section: Planning and Organizing in an Insurance Company
text:
Treasury

Manages cash and near-cash assets

Thompson considers the company's goals, resources, capabilities, and circumstances to develop long-term, high-level plans. Thompson is most likely

An executive

A middle manager

An executive

Thompson is most likely an executive. Executives set strategy. Middle managers focus on tactics, which are the activities that support the overall strategy.

Sal helps maintain financial records, prepare reports on the company's financial condition, and file required financial statements. Sal most likely works in which functional area?

Accounting and Financial Reporting

Audit

Risk Management

Legal and Compliance

Accounting and Financial Reporting

Accounting and Financial Reporting is the functional area that maintains financial records, prepares reports on the company's financial condition, and files required financial statements.

Rodney works in an area that comes up with product ideas and then takes those ideas all the way through creation and launch. Rodney probably works in which functional area?

Actuarial

Underwriting and New Business

Marketing

Product Development

Product Development

Product Development is the functional area that takes new products from concept to launch.

Which of the following are line functions? Choose all that apply.

Product Development

Marketing

Corporate Communications

Treasury

Customer Service

Underwriting and New Business

Audit

Accounting and Financial Reporting

Product Development

Marketing

Customer Service

Underwriting and New Business

Major line functions—such as Product Development, Marketing, Customer Service, and Underwriting and New Business—produce and administer products. Support functions support each other and the line functions.

Learning Objective

Describe organizational concepts such as accountability, authority, chain of command, delegation, division of labor, and responsibility.

Forthright uses organizational structure to

Define employees' primary duties and formal rights to direct the company's resources by specifying authority (The right to direct resources, including people.), responsibility (An assigned duty or task.), and accountability (The duty to answer for one's own actions.)

Define divisions, departments, and job positions

An organization chart (A visual display of the lines of authority and responsibility within a company. )—also known as an org chart—is a visual display of the lines of authority and responsibility within a company.

Here's a mystery: Why is the classic org chart shaped like a pyramid—narrow at the top and wide at the bottom?

An org chart illustrates how authority flows from top management down to non-managerial employees. Typically, a company has more non-managerial employees than management employees, which makes the org chart wider at the bottom than at the top.

Directing

Directing (The management function of ensuring that employees perform the appropriate activities in the appropriate fashion to achieve company objectives.) involves ensuring that employees perform the right activities in the right way. Managerial activities of directing include

Leading

Motivating

Supervising

Communicating

Facilitating

Chain of Command

In a pyramid-shaped organization, authority starts at the top with one person or a very few people and flows down through a chain of command (A hierarchy of authority in an organization, starting at the top with one person or a very few people and distributed downward from the higher levels to the lower levels. Also known as hierarchy; hierarchy of authority.)  to ever-larger numbers of people.

Different companies take different approaches to chains of command. In vertical (An organization with a long chain of command and a narrow span of control.) organizations, the chain of command is long, and the span of control (The number of people or functions over which an employee has authority. )—that is, the number of people or functions over which an employee has authority—is narrow.

Click or touch in the boxes below to learn about potential benefits and drawbacks of a vertical organizational structure.

Vertical Organization Benefits

Benefits often attributed to vertical organizations include

Well-defined roles

Smooth processes

Easy supervision

Vertical Organization Drawbacks

Vertical organizations sometimes face criticism for the following perceived drawbacks:

Decisions must move through multiple rungs

Open communication is less likely, so silos (Operational unit that operates in isolation from other operating units, not understanding how their jobs relate to other operational units or how corporate goals relate to their jobs.) are more likely

Employees may feel discouraged from sharing creative solutions to problems

In flat organizations (An organization with a short chain of command and a wide span of control. Also called a horizontal organization.) (also called horizontal organizations), the chain of command is short, and the span of control is wide.

Click or touch in the boxes below to learn about potential benefits and drawbacks of a flat organizational structure.

Flat Organization Benefits

Flat organizations are often said to have the following advantages:

Limited bureaucracy, which leads to faster decision-making

Open communication

Cross-functional teamwork

Flat Organization Drawbacks

Perceived disadvantages of flat organization include

Undefined roles

Unclear processes

Difficulty holding employees accountable for results

In addition, a flat structure isn't always practical for large companies.

Companies' chains of command are based on their business needs and company culture, with many operating somewhere between vertical and flat.

The nature of an organization's chain of command determines how delegation (The process of assigning responsibility and authority to another employee.) and division of labor (The process of dividing large tasks into smaller functional components.)  are handled in that organization.

Delegation is assigning responsibility and authority to other employees—generally to employees lower in the chain of command.

Division of labor is dividing large tasks into smaller components that can be assigned to different employees.

True or false?

Authority refers to each employee's duty to answer for his own actions.

True

False

False

Authority refers to the right to direct resources, including people. Accountability refers to each employee's duty to answer for his own actions.

Derek works for a small company as a software developer. He and eight other software developers report to Liam, who is the director of product. Liam reports to the head of the company. Derek works in a company with a

Vertical  organizational  structure

Flat organizational structure

Flat organizational structure

Derek works for a company with a flat organizational structure. The company has a relatively short chain of command and Liam has a relatively wide span of control.

Delegation refers to which of the following concepts?

The process of dividing large tasks into smaller functional components

The hierarchy of authority in an organization

The process of assigning responsibility and authority to another employee

The process of assigning responsibility and authority to another employee

The process of assigning responsibility and authority to another employee is delegation.

Learning Objective

Characterize the distinct roles of executives, middle managers, and supervisors.

Now that we have introduced the functions of management, let's discuss the different levels of management. A typical organization's management consists of executives, middle managers, and supervisors.

Each level of management serves a unique purpose. Any manager at any level will normally wear more than one hat and perform more than just one function. Let's look at the differences in the jobs of these managers.

Executive Officers

The management domain of executives is sometimes called strategic management (The top level of management.)

Executives (also known as officers, or senior-level managers) perform strategic planning (Setting long-term corporate goals and the broad, overall courses of action to achieve these goals. )—setting major long-term corporate goals and broad approaches to achieve them.

A company's executives and officers are the chief executive officer, president, other chief officers, vice presidents, and any other individuals appointed as officers by the board of directors.

Common Executive Positions

Companies can have any number of executive titles depending on their business needs, but most companies have about five or fewer. Some common executive positions include the ones below. Click or touch the arrows to see them all.

Chief Operating Officer (COO)

The executive in charge of all day-to-day company operations

Chief Financial Officer (CFO)

The executive in charge of financial operations

Chief Technology Officer (CTO)

The executive in charge of the company's external technology strategy

Chief Information Officer

The executive in charge of the internal use of information and technology

Chief Marketing Officer

The executive in charge of marketing and communications to customers, clients, and/or partners

General Counsel

The attorney who heads the legal and compliance functions

Some officer positions—for example, Appointed Actuary or Certifying Compliance Officer—are required by regulation or statute.

Middle Managers

Middle managers supervise other managers and report to other managers. Middle management determines how to accomplish the specific tasks for carrying out the organization's strategic plans.

Middle management performs operational planning (Creating plans for day-to-day operations designed to support the organization's strategic plans. Also known as tactical planning.) (also known as tactical planning)—translating strategic-level plans into plans for day-to-day tactical operations within their units.

Supervisors

Supervisors (also known as first-level managers) directly supervise non-management employees and report to middle management employees. Supervisors manage the smallest units of the organization and implement tactical plans.

Supervisors practice operational (tactical) management.

Listen as an expert explains the difference between strategic and operational management.

True or false?

Operational planning consists of setting long-term corporate goals and the broad courses of action for achieving them.

True

False

False

The planning described is strategic planning. Operational planning is translating strategic plans into plans for everyday operations.

Anya recently received a promotion to claims supervisor. Anya is likely to be responsible for which of the following duties? Choose all that apply.

Reporting to middle management

Translating policy and strategic plans into tactical plans

Directly supervising non-management employees

Implementing tactical plans

Setting strategic policy and making strategic plans

Reporting to middle management

Directly supervising non-management employees

Implementing tactical plans

As a supervisor, Anya likely reports to middle management, supervises non-management employees, and implements tactical plans. Anya is probably not responsible for setting strategic policy (executives) or for translating policy and strategic plans into tactical plans (middle management).

Learning Objective

Describe how financial services companies use function, product, territory, customer, and distribution channel to structure their organization.

Companies can choose from among several types of organizational structures. In fact, most financial services companies combine two or more organizational approaches.

One way to organize a company's operations is organization by functional area—illustrated in this figure.

With organization by product (An organizational structure which arranges a company's major divisions according to the company's product lines.), each division focuses on a product line.

With organization by territory (An organizational structure which arranges a company's major divisions according to the geographic areas in which it operates.), each division serves a certain geographic area.

With organization by customer group (An organizational structure which arranges a company's major divisions according to the customer group each unit serves.), each division serves a specified customer group.

With organization by distribution channel (An organizational structure which arranges a company's major divisions according to the distribution channels that deliver products to customers.), each division works with a given distribution channel.

The following statements are about organizational structure in a typical insurance company. Choose all statements that are true.

A chief operating officer likely reports to the company's chief executive officer.

Supervisors typically report directly to middle managers.

A company that has major divisions for Europe, Asia-Pacific, and the Americas is organized by customer.

An underwriting unit is classified as a support unit.

A chief operating officer likely reports to the company's chief executive officer.

Supervisors typically report directly to middle managers.

The Rex Financial Services Company has separate companies for the United States and Canada. Within each of those companies, each has a division for group business and individual business. Then, both the group and the individual business divisions have major departments for each line function. Choose all of the approaches to organizational structure included in this description of Rex.

Organization by product

Organization by function

Organization by territory

Organization by customer group

Organization by distribution channel

Organization by function

Organization by territory

Organization by customer group

At the highest level, the company is organized by territory—in this case by country. The division for each country is organized by customer group, and the business unit for each customer group is organized by function.

Suggested Activities

Try these activities to relate the content of this lesson to the real world of your company.

How is your company organized? Find a copy of your company's organization chart. Can you find your own position on the chart?

Name some line and support units in your company.

Determine what organizational structure is used in your company. How about in your division?

Delegation refers to which of the following concepts?

An insurer’s overall direction is determined by its executives, with guidance from the board of directors. In this module, we explore how an insurer is organized and managed to balance the needs of a diverse set of stakeholder groups, and how an insurer manages the risk it must take in order to earn a return.

In this module, you’ll learn about

How an insurer’s board and executives manage the company ethically to meet the needs of various stakeholders

The ways an insurer can be organized for success

How an insurer manages the various risks it faces to earn an appropriate return
source: LOMA291_M1L3_Knowledge File_Risk, Return, and Risk Management.docx

text: To continue in business, a company needs to earn a return. That is, it needs to make money. To make money, a company has to face the risk of losing money. Risk can’t be avoided, but it can be managed. Learn about the role of risk management in an insurer’s operations.
source: LOMA291_M1L3_Knowledge File_Risk, Return, and Risk Management.docx

text:
Define risk and return, and explain the risk-return tradeoff.

Determine an investment's required return using the risk-free rate of return and the risk premium.

Describe and classify risks faced by insurance companies.

Explain control and the control cycle.

Describe techniques for managing risk, including risk transfer, diversification, and risk hedging.

Explain enterprise risk management in financial services companies.

Describe the three lines of defense, and explain how insurers use them to manage risk.
source: LOMA291_M1L3_Knowledge File_Risk, Return, and Risk Management.docx

text:
Learning Objective

Define risk and return, and explain the risk-return tradeoff.

An insurer faces risk in virtually everything it does: selling a policy, hiring an employee, and generating profit. Taking a certain amount of risk is necessary for any company.

But companies have systems to identify and manage risks. These systems protect companies as much as possible while allowing them to pursue their goals and objectives.

Managing risk and return is the ultimate balancing act. Taking too much risk leaves a company vulnerable to financial loss. On the other hand, playing it too safe limits potential returns.

In this lesson, we explore the risk and return relationship. We'll see the risks that insurance companies face and what approaches they can use to manage them.

What's an Investment?

When you hear the word "investment," you probably think of stocks, bonds, and mutual funds. In business, stocks and bonds are assets, and purchasing stocks and bonds is a form of investing.

There are many other types of investments, though.

Which actions below would be investments for Forthright Financial? Choose all that apply.

Buying a stock or bond

Launching a new product

Hiring an employee

Issuing a policy

Opening a branch office

Installing a new information system

Acquiring a line of business from a competitor

All is correct!

Investment (Any use of resources that is intended to generate a profit or positive return.) is any use of resources with the aim of earning a profit. Issuing a policy is an investment for an insurer. Hiring an employee or installing a new information system may not seem to you like an investment, but Forthright cannot earn a profit without a quality work force or adequate information systems. So they ARE investments.

Which of the investments in the previous question involves risk?

None of these

Some of these

All of these

All of these

All of these! What do we mean by risk, though?

Risk

Risk (The possibility that an investment or other venture might have an unexpected result.) is the possibility of an unexpected result.

A stock you buy could lose value

An employee you hire might perform poorly

An investor expects an investment to earn a gain…a profit…a return (The reward, profit, or compensation an investor hopes to earn in exchange for taking a risk.).

Whatever you call it, an investor expects to earn some compensation for taking a risk.

What type of unexpected outcome can an investment have? Choose all that apply.

Which of these most likely best represents Forthright's approach to risk and return as regards its assets?

Risk-Return Tradeoff

Whenever you find an opportunity for a return, you find risk. The relationship between risk and return is called the risk-return tradeoff (The relationship between risk and return that affects virtually all business transactions. In general, all other factors being equal, the greater the risk associated with an investment, the greater the expected return. The lower the risk associated with an investment, the lower the expected return.).

In general, the riskier an investment, the greater the potential return on the investment. Conversely, the less risky an investment, the lower the expected return.

Earning an adequate return requires taking some amount of risk.

Earning a huge return requires taking a huge risk.

Where there's only a small risk, there's only a small potential return.

Where there's a large risk, there's a large potential return.

Which of the following statements is true about risk and/or return in an insurance company? More than one response may be correct.

The risks insurance companies face are limited to the risks inherent in investing in securities, such as stocks and bonds.

According to the concept known as the risk-return tradeoff, in order to earn a large return, an insurer must generally accept a large risk.

According to the concept known as the risk-return tradeoff, in order to earn a large return, an insurer must generally accept a large risk.

According to concept of the risk-return tradeoff, an insurer must take on a large amount of risk in order to earn a large return. Conversely, if the insurer only accepts a small amount of risk, it can generally only expect a small return.

However, the risks insurance companies face are NOT limited to the risk of investing in securities. The term "investment" refers to any use of resources with the aim of earning a profit, including but not by any means limited to investing in securities, and all these investments are subject to risk.
source: LOMA291_M1L3_Knowledge File_Risk, Return, and Risk Management.docx

text:
Learning Objective

Determine an investment's required return using the risk-free rate of return and the risk premium.

Elements of a Required Return

A return from any source can be expressed in terms of a rate of return (The earnings on an investment expressed as a percentage of the principal. )—a percentage of the amount invested (called the principal).

Generally, an investor's required return (The return an investor needs to earn in order to make the investment, given the risk of the investment; typically found by adding the risk-free rate of return to the risk premium. )—the rate of return the investor needs to earn in order to make an investment—equals the sum of a risk-free rate (The return on a risk-free investment—the least risky investment opportunity available. See required return and risk premium.) and a risk premium (The component of investment return that compensates the investor for taking on the risk associated with a specific investment; the rate of return required in addition to the risk-free rate for the investor to make the investment.).

Risk-Free Rate + Risk Premium = Required Return

An investor expects to earn some compensation even when an investment is risk-free. The amount an investor expects to earn from a risk-free investment is called the risk-free rate.

Examples of risk-free rates include

The interest rate guaranteed on a Treasury bill

The interest rate on a savings bond

The interest rate on a bank certificate of deposit

The crediting rate guaranteed on a fixed annuity

For taking risk, an investor expects to earn an added return in addition to the risk-free rate. This added return, known as the risk premium, should be appropriate for the level of risk presented by the investment.

The required return on a risky investment is the total of the risk-free rate and the risk premium. Use the right arrows below to learn more.

Let's calculate Forthright's required return for a top-rated corporate bond.

3% Risk-Free Rate

Forthright expects to earn at least a 3% return on its investment.

3% Risk-Free Rate

3.5% Risk Premium

For this bond, Forthright requires a 3.5% rate of return in addition to the risk-free rate for the bond.

3% Risk-Free Rate

3.5% Risk Premium

____________________________________

6.5% Required Return

Forthright's required return on this top-rated corporate bond is 6.5%.

You have the following information about an investment project:

The risk-free rate in the current market is 2 percent.

The investment project is moderately risky.

Your required return is 5 percent.

In this situation, the risk premium equals

3%

5%

10%

12%

quals

3%

The risk premium is the difference between the required rate of return (5 percent) and the risk-free rate of return (2 percent).

Risk premium = 5% – 2% = 3%
source: LOMA291_M1L3_Knowledge File_Risk, Return, and Risk Management.docx

text:
Learning Objective

Describe and classify risks faced by insurance companies.

Question: How many risks do insurers face?

Answer: A lot!

Many people think that the only risk insurers face is the risk of insureds dying prematurely. Nothing could be further from the truth.

Different activities expose insurers to different types of risk. For example, an insurer encounters the risk of lower-than-expected returns when it invests in certain assets. Or let's say the company introduces a new product: it faces risks like lower-than-anticipated sales and higher-than-expected claims payments.

Let's look more closely at the different risks insurers must effectively manage.

Investment Risk

Forthright uses funds collected from customers to buy assets like bonds. Investing exposes the investor to a number of investment risks. Click or touch each heading below to learn more.

Market Risk

Market risk (A risk of unexpected outcomes due to fluctuations in the market values in a given financial market. Also known as financial market risk.) is the chance of unexpected movements in the value of an entire market for assets, such as the stock market, bond market, currency markets, or real estate market.

Example
The stock market plummets or skyrockets.

Interest-Rate Risk

Interest-rate risk (The risk that unpredictable movements or stagnation in market interest rates will cause assets to lose value or liabilities to gain value.) is the risk that unpredictable movements or stagnation in market interest rates will cause assets to lose value or liabilities to gain value.

Example
Market interest rates remain low, making it difficult for Forthright to purchase new assets that will produce the necessary return on investment.

Default Risk

Default risk (The risk that a borrower will fail to pay its obligations. Also known as credit risk.) is the risk that a borrower—like a bond issuer or a mortgage owner—will fail to repay their debt.

Example
Forthright buys corporate bonds and the issuer fails to pay interest or pay back the borrowed amount.

Liquidity Risk

Liquidity risk (The risk of being unable to readily convert an asset to cash for its underlying value.) is the risk of being unable to quickly convert an asset to cash for its underlying value.

Example
Forthright needs cash to pay benefits to customers, but it can't quickly sell a shopping mall for its true value.

Pricing Risk

When designing a product, Forthright must determine how much to charge customers so that the product is both affordable and profitable. This process, known as pricing, is a source of pricing risk.

Pricing a product requires an insurer to make assumptions. For example, an insurer must estimate future mortality rates to determine how many claims the insurer is likely to pay each year.

Pricing risk is the risk that actual results do not match the insurer's assumptions. What are some sample pricing risks?

A product might not sell well or might sell better than expected (also known as product risk).

A product's charges might not cover the costs of marketing and administering the product.

More people might surrender their policies or other contracts earlier than expected, or might exercise other contractual rights that are unfavorable to the issuing company.

Life insurance claims could occur earlier than the company expected.

Business Risk

Forthright Financial's external environment is full of risks that its employees must identify and analyze. Many of these external risks are business risks. Use the right arrows below to learn more.

What are the most important business risks?

Operational Risk

Operational risk describes the risk of unfavorable results or events originating in or affecting the insurer's operational areas. Although there are lots of different operational risks, operational risks generally fall into two categories. Click or touch in the boxes below to learn more.

Business Process Risk

Business process risk is an operational risk that results from inefficient or failed internal processes, controls, people, technology, or systems. for examples.

Examples of business process risk include

Market conduct compliance issues

Loss of a key person

Fraud

Distribution problems

Damage to a company's reputation

Event Risk

Event risk is an operational risk that results from catastrophic events. for examples.

Examples of event risk include

Technology failures

Natural disasters

Business interruptions

Examples of Operational Risk

Operational risk is the primary type of risk most employees encounter. Here are a few examples—although we could mention many more.

Human Resources
If we aren't on top of our game in HR, operations can suffer because of excessive turnover or delays in filling open positions.

Information Management & Technology
Our policy is to use the very best technology to safeguard company records. If hackers access sensitive information, our precautions have failed and we've had a breach of data security.

Underwriting and New Business
If information from an application is entered incorrectly into the product administration system, the company could charge the customer the wrong premium. Surprises in underwriting and new business are expensive!

Operational Risks in Marketing

We face other operational risks in sales and marketing. Market conduct infractions could result in hefty fines or lawsuits.

Sales producers might mislead clients.

Sales materials might be distributed before being approved by regulators.

A customer might obtain a variable product that fails to meet product suitability requirements.

We have safeguards in place to make sure these things never happen.

Operational Risks in Customer Service

How about operational risk in customer service? Customers might be frustrated by

Excessive telephone wait times

Errors in customer records

Lack of mobile account accessibility

Slow turnaround time

To complicate things, customers compare your customer service not just against that of other insurers, but against customer service in every industry.

It's a challenge to keep up with customer expectations, especially as technology advances. But without happy customers, we don't have a business.
source: LOMA291_M1L3_Knowledge File_Risk, Return, and Risk Management.docx

text:
1. Forthright experiences the results of market risk if the company

Experiences a loss in the value of bond assets after overall bond prices fall

Loans money to borrowers who fail to repay their debts to the company

Needs cash quickly, but cannot quickly sell an asset for its true value

Fails to achieve the expected volume of product sales from a new product launch

One major category of operational risk is business process risk. The other category of operational risk is

Event risk

Default risk

Competition risk

Liquidity risk

From the following risks, select the examples of operational risk. Choose all that apply.

Loss of company reputation

Fraud

Default risk

Loss of a key employee

Rating agency risk

Risk from natural disasters
source: LOMA291_M1L3_Knowledge File_Risk, Return, and Risk Management.docx

text:
Learning Objective

Explain control and the control cycle.

The control function (Management activity directed toward ensuring that an organization's mission and objectives are accomplished effectively, efficiently, and according to plan.) is one of the four basic management functions—remember? Controls and control processes are also important tools for managing risks.

A company's efforts to manage risk depend strongly on the control function. The control function includes all the company's policies, procedures, and checks designed to ensure that the company sticks to its plans and accomplishes its objectives.

Categories and Phases of Control

Think of a business process, such as underwriting an application, issuing a contract, responding to a customer inquiry, handling a claim, or any other task you encounter in your work. All of these processes are subject to controls.

Process controls are divided into three general categories, each reflecting a phase of a business process that is subject to controls. Click or touch each one below to learn more.

Steering Controls

Steering controls ( are applied before a process. Examples include

Policies (an equal employment opportunity policy)

Operating procedures

Performance standards

A code of ethics or business conduct

Budgets

Schedules

Goals and objectives

Goal setting in an employee performance appraisal system

Training

An underwriting manual

Concurrent Controls

Concurrent controls are applied during a process. Examples include

Activity logs or transaction logs that monitor company activities

Error logs

Pop-up error messages if out-of-range values are entered into a system

Supervisory approval on claim checks exceeding a specified dollar amount

Real-time monitoring of customer calls

Elapsed-time clocks running during customer service calls

A supervisor's approval signature on an expense report

A requisition voucher for supplies

Suspense account entries

Feedback Controls

Feedback controls (A control that takes the form of checks and corrections that are applied to a process at the end of the process cycle.) are applied after a process. Examples include

Audits

Account reconciliations

Performance appraisals in a performance system

Shareholder meetings

Customer satisfaction surveys

Budget analysis

Actual-to-expected (A/E) analysis of expenses

Your support for controls is critical to your company's overall success. What's YOUR role in controls? Choose all that apply

To understand controls related to your job

To support controls through your job-related actions

To encourage colleagues to respect controls

All is correct!

Each of these choices describes ways that employees support their company's controls.

The Control Cycle

"Those who cannot remember the past are condemned to repeat it." - George Santayana

Insurers use the lessons of history to improve their operations and risk management. The three categories of controls are not a straight line, but a cycle: the control cycle.

The insurer uses feedback from its control activities to improve its business processes. That's probably not a surprise. But it also uses that feedback to improve the control activities themselves.

An audit is an example of a [steering / feedback] control, which is used [during / after] a business process.

steering / during

feedback / during

steering / after

feedback / after

Which of the following is an example of a steering control? Choose all that apply.

Customer satisfaction surveys

Elapsed-time clock in phone calls

Goal setting

Policies and procedures

Employee training
source: LOMA291_M1L3_Knowledge File_Risk, Return, and Risk Management.docx

text:
Learning Objectives

Describe techniques for managing risk, including risk transfer, diversification, and risk hedging.

Explain enterprise risk management in financial services companies.

The risk-return tradeoff means that an insurer can't earn sufficient return without accepting some risk.

However, even if a company can't avoid risk completely, it can still limit its exposure to risk. Risk may be inevitable, but risk management (A systematic application of management policies, procedures, and practices to identify, quantify, model, offset, and limit the impact of unexpected outcomes, consistent with organizational objectives.) is about controlling risk to some degree.

Risk Management

Financial services companies use many approaches to managing risk, including instituting and enforcing appropriate controls.

Let's talk about some others. First: transferring risk.

Risk Transfer

Forthright uses insurance and reinsurance to transfer risks to other parties.

How Forthright Uses Insurance and Reinsurance

Insurance:

Life insurance on key employees

Property and casualty insurance for company assets

Liability insurance for key employees

Reinsurance:

Reinsurance limits Forthright's potential claims expenses on insurance business

Obtaining reinsurance gives Forthright access to outside expertise about risk management

Diversification

Another important risk management technique is diversification—spreading risks so that no one loss will take everything you have.

Here's a catchy folk saying: "Don't put all your eggs in one basket."

Forthright diversifies its

Investment risks by purchasing assets in a variety of asset classes, such as bonds, stocks, mortgages, and real estate

Pricing risks by issuing several types of products to people in a variety of risk classes, geographic areas, occupations, industries, and ages

Operational risks by maintaining several offices and storing backups of data in several places so that, in the event of an emergency, the company can continue operating

Dependence on third party reimbursements by placing insurance and reinsurance with a variety of carriers rather than just one company

Hedging Risks

Another way to manage risks involves balancing one risk with a complementary risk—called hedging. Click or touch the right arrows below to learn about hedging investment risks.

Hedging Investment Risk

Ideally, when one investment suffers negative outcomes from a risk, the other investment will experience positive outcomes from the same risk.

Click or touch the right arrows below to learn about hedging pricing risk.

Hedging Pricing Risk

To hedge pricing risk, an insurer sells another product that stands to gain when the first product is losing.

Reinsuring a portion of the risk of a block of policies is an example of the [transferring risk / hedging / diversification] risk management technique, while offering products with complementary risk profiles is an example of [diversification / hedging].

Enterprise Risk Management

How is ERM different from risk management without the "E"?

Enterprise risk management (ERM) takes an enterprise-wide view of risk management, looking at all the risks and opportunities, and taking a systematic, coordinated approach to managing them all.

ERM is usually directed from the top, with a

Risk Committee of the Board of Directors that has ultimate oversight of ERM

Chief Risk Officer (CRO) or Risk Management Director (RMD) who directs the company's ERM activities

Companies may have a team or even a formal department dedicated to ERM. Companies often employ risk analysts, who are generally trained actuaries.

Although ERM starts at the top, remember that it is an enterprise-wide practice. With ERM, every employee plays a role in managing risk. One way to achieve this goal is by establishing a risk culture.

Everyone should have an idea of how much risk the company is willing to accept, and be able to recognize potential new risks as they arise.

For example, Forthright has a fairly conservative risk culture. The investment team receives an offer to invest in a particular tech company. The investment seems to be both high-risk and offering high potential returns. This is more risk than Forthright's risk culture prefers, so the investment team declines the offer.

ERM involves reporting company risk information not just inside the company, but also to external stakeholders.

Regulators and rating agencies expect an insurer to have an ERM program. When evaluating a company, they review

The quality and effectiveness of the company's ERM program

The risks the company faces

Its strategies for managing risks

You may think that ERM is all about guarding against threats, but it's not. It's also about taking opportunities. ERM helps insurers evaluate which opportunities to pursue. With a sound ERM program and sound ERM strategies in place, a company can understand each investment in terms of its potential earnings relative to its risk. The insurer can evaluate

The risk of the investment

The potential return of the investment

Whether the potential return justifies taking the risk

The following statements pertain to enterprise risk management in a typical financial services company. Select each correct statement.

ERM targets not only risks that threaten a company's viability but also those that have the potential to enhance profitability.

ERM provides risk information to internal parties but not external stakeholders.

Currently, rating agencies expect insurers to have an effective ERM program in place.

Some companies have an ERM department.
source: LOMA291_M1L3_Knowledge File_Risk, Return, and Risk Management.docx

text:
Learning Objectives

Describe the three lines of defense, and explain how insurers use them to manage risk.

No single technique can manage every risk. That's the idea behind the three lines of defense risk management model.

Think of the three lines of defense as like barricades, or a ring of walls protecting a castle. If a risk slips past the one line of defense, the next line of defense is ready.

The three lines of defense overlap with other risk management frameworks.

Remember the organization-wide focus of ERM? The three lines of defense also has an organization-wide focus.

First Line: Operational Management

The first line of defense, or the frontline, is operational management.

Operational managers are responsible for identifying risks as they arise and taking appropriate steps to minimize the effect of those risks.

In turn, managers train employees to identify and manage risks appropriately. For example, all employees may receive training to deal with potential cybersecurity risks such as viruses, but underwriters may receive specific training to recognize the signs of a potentially fraudulent application.

The first line of defense is a real team effort: we're all on the frontline together.

Second Line: Internal Monitoring and Oversight

The second line of defense—internal monitoring and oversight—looks for signs of risks that might not be obvious on the frontlines.

A company's risk committee and compliance function are both key players in the second line of defense.

Internal monitoring double-checks risk management to make sure things are going well.

The oversight function owns aspects of the risk management process and helps set risk policy. For example, the risk committee may develop policies for data backups and restoration of email function in the event of a catastrophic event striking the home office.

Think of the second line of defense as risk specialists. Risk is our business.

Third Line: Internal Audit

The third line of defense, the internal audit, determines if the first two lines of defense have been performing to expectations.

Internal audits systematically examine the company's risk management to determine what is working and what could be improved.

Internal audits are different from the other two lines of defense in two important ways:

Objectivity. An audit measures facts and evidence only. It doesn't weigh risk against potential reward—it only reports results.

Organizational independence. An auditor reports only to the highest level of management. This helps keep the audit unbiased.

The third line of defense gives us the facts we need to be sure that risk management is working as intended.

In the three lines of defense risk management model, a product line manager and her employees are on the

First line of defense

Second line of defense

Third line of defense

text:
A customer's contact with an insurer most likely begins when they are thinking about purchasing a policy, and can last until the policyowner dies and the insurer pays the policy proceeds to the beneficiary. In between, the insurer underwrites and issues the policy, and meets the customer’s service needs while the policy is in force. In this module, you’ll learn about the tasks an insurer performs at each stage of the policy lifecycle.

In this module, you’ll learn about

How insurers engage with potential customers and distribute products to meet customer needs

How insurers process insurance applications, evaluate the risk they represent, and determine the appropriate premium to charge

How insurers try to optimize the experience of their customers

How insurers balance paying legitimate claims promptly and accurately with avoiding paying illegitimate claims

Like any business, insurers must make their products available for customers to buy. But insurance products are different from consumer products, such as food or clothing. Learn about these differences and how insurers engage with customers to teach them about products and try to win their business.

Describe the various engagement methods insurers and financial professionals can use.

Distinguish between direct distribution systems and intermediary distribution systems.

Describe the characteristics of a direct sales force and direct-to-consumer (D2C) distribution in insurance.

Explain how insurers provide sales support such as recruiting, licensing, compensation, and training to different types of agents.

List three prohibited sales practices, and describe the activities that insurers undertake to monitor the market conduct of their agents.

Discuss the different intermediaries through which some insurers distribute their products.

Identify and describe the factors an insurer considers when making decisions about which distribution systems to use.

Identify types of distribution problems and solutions.

Engagement Methods

Learning Objective

Describe the various engagement methods insurers and financial professionals can use.

Financial products don't reach the marketplace the way consumer goods do. Think about it: financial products can't sit in a display window or on a showroom floor. Without an effective distribution system, these products won't reach customers where, when, and how they want. That's why distribution is critical.

Insurers make decisions about which distribution options to use based on the customers they are trying to serve. From a customer's perspective, distribution is all about convenience. Although many customers do initial fact-finding and research on their own, they often want to talk to a knowledgeable financial professional who can assess their needs, explain products in detail, and assist them in the purchase decision. More and more, insurers strive to provide omnichannel distribution, which allows customers to choose how they wish to engage with the insurer and enables companies to create personalized interactions with customers and business partners.

Let's explore some common methods insurers use to engage with customers.

Face-to-Face

Financial professionals commonly meet face-to-face with potential customers, often referred to as prospects. Financial professionals follow a fairly typical sales process during a series of meetings.

During these face-to-face meetings, the financial professional:

Identifies the prospect's financial needs

Develops a proposal that recommends one or more insurance products to meet the identified needs

Presents the proposal to the prospect in hopes of completing a sale

If the sale is successful, assists the customer in applying for the product, submits the application to the insurer, and, in some instances, delivers the policy to the customer

Phone

Insurers and financial professionals can use telephones to share information with customers and prospects. Sometimes, the customer will initiate the contact…

I'm thinking about purchasing a new life insurance policy. I'll call the number from that Forthright ad I saw the other day.

…and sometimes the insurer or financial professional will initiate the contact.

I remember Sheryl saying she wanted to start using a fitness tracker. I should text her—maybe she'll be interested in the new Forthright Flex rider, which comes with a free subscription to a digital wellness app.

Regulatory restrictions in some countries limit the degree to which insurers can make unsolicited sales calls to prospective customers.

Direct Mail or Email

An insurer or financial professional using direct mail or email distributes insurance sales materials through a mail service directly to a list of prospective customers. These mailings can be physical letters, brochures, or flyers mailed to the prospect or emails sent to a distribution list. The target market for direct mail might be readers of a particular publication or holders of a particular credit card.

For paper mail, the sales materials usually consist of an introduction letter, a brochure that describes a particular product, an insurance or annuity application, or an inquiry form the customer can use to request further information about the product. For email, the insurer provides links to similar items on the insurer's website.

Online

Most insurers' websites provide information and self-service options and promote products that can satisfy needs. Insurers also advertise their products through third-party websites and social media. Consumers using these websites may contact the company by telephone, email, or web chat to ask questions or purchase a product. Often, insurers put these consumers in contact with a financial professional.

Financial professionals may also use websites and social media to engage with customers directly.

Print and Broadcast Media

An insurer or financial professional may use printed publications, such as magazines or newspapers, to describe a particular product and generate interest in that product. Insurers can try to reach a particular target market by printing advertisements in newspapers in certain geographical areas or in magazines that appeal to certain demographics. For example, an advertisement for an annuity product designed for people age 62 or older might appear in a magazine for retired people.

An insurer can use radio, television, or video streaming sites to disseminate an advertising message over a wide area to a large, generally undifferentiated audience. However, selecting certain programs or times of the day in which to advertise does allow an insurer some selectivity. For example, a life insurance product might be advertised on television between the hours of 8 and 10 p.m. when newly married couples or young parents are likely watching television.

Worksite Marketing

Financial professionals sometimes engage in worksite marketing to distribute voluntary benefits. Usually, the employer collaborates with the financial professional to promote voluntary benefits to employees.

Examples of voluntary benefits include

Life insurance

Disability insurance

Accident insurance

Critical illness insurance

Long-term care insurance

ID theft protection

Legal services

Financial counseling

Location Selling

Some insurance companies also sell insurance products through a method known as location-selling. These locations can be staffed by a financial professional or offer self-service options for customers.

Location-selling systems may be located in businesses such as department stores, big box stores, grocery stores, and funeral homes.

Which of the following enables personalized sales to customers through multiple, integrated communication channels?

Omnichannel distribution

Worksite marketing

Location-selling

Which of the following are examples of products that can be offered as voluntary benefits? Choose all that apply.

Life insurance

Disability income insurance

Accident insurance

Critical illness insurance

Long-term care insurance

True or False?

The first step in a series of face-to-face meetings with a prospect is for the financial professional to present the proposal in hopes of completing the sale.

True

False

Learning Objectives

Distinguish between direct distribution systems and intermediary distribution systems.

Describe the characteristics of a direct sales force and direct-to-consumer (D2C) distribution in insurance.

After using one or several engagement methods to learn more about an insurer and its products, a customer purchases a product, either with the assistance of a financial professional, or directly from the insurer without the participation of a financial professional.

Insurance companies use distribution systems to connect products or services with customers who might want or need them. A company's distribution system and all the people who make up that system are crucial to the financial success of the company. After all, what good is a product if it just sits on the shelf?

The two types of insurance distribution systems are direct and intermediary.

If the customer purchases directly from the insurer or through a member of the insurer's direct sales force, then the insurer uses a direct distribution system.

If the insurance company relies on other individuals and organizations to sell the product to the customer, then the insurer uses an intermediary distribution system.

The figure below illustrates the difference between direct and intermediary distribution systems.

Direct Sales Force

A direct sales force is a form of direct distribution in which an insurance company offers its products through financial professionals directed by the company. Click or touch each heading below to learn more.

Agents

Within the life insurance industry, financial professionals who advise clients and potential clients for individual insurance products are commonly referred to as agents. The agent and the insurer enter into an agency contract.

Two types of agents that may be part of an insurer's direct sales force are

Career agents

Multiple-line exclusive agents (MLEAs)

An insurer that maintains a direct sales force exercises considerable control over how agents' work is conducted and contributes significant financial support. Career agents and multiple-line exclusive agents typically have to satisfy minimum sales production and sometimes minimum persistency requirements in order to continue their agency relationship with a particular insurance company.

In certain circumstances, financial professionals in a direct sales force may be allowed to sell another company's products. For example, when an agent's primary company declines to insure an applicant, then the agent might be allowed to place the business with another insurer. The agency contract will specify the circumstances under which an agent may place business with another insurance company.

Salaried Sales Representatives

Some companies use salaried sales representatives to distribute certain types of products.

Most insurers handle their group insurance and group annuity sales through salaried sales representatives, who are typically referred to as group representatives. Group representatives are trained to assess and meet the needs of groups and service group products.

Direct-to-Consumer

Consumers expect engaging digital experiences when they interact with companies. In addition, insurers are looking for ways to satisfy customer needs in a cost-effective way, and technology offers possibilities for doing so.

Direct-to-consumer (D2C) sales are non face-to-face distribution programs directed by the insurance company. In a D2C interaction, the insurance company can encourage the purchase of its products through marketing directly to consumers or the consumers can recognize their needs on their own and contact the company directly.

Insurers can use data analytics to identify the characteristics of customers who are most likely to respond to a D2C approach. Data analytics can improve the insurer's response rate and potentially reduce its costs for each sale.

Example of D2C

As the example illustrates, D2C contact can take place through several of the engagement methods we discussed earlier, including online, phone, mail, email, and social media. An insurer using D2C distribution needs to make decisions about the types of products to offer through D2C, the consumers to target, and the methods it will use to engage with these consumers. Historically, insurers have offered relatively simple products through D2C.

Companies use different types of professionals to sell their products. Can you distinguish among them?

Brian Delange maintains his own office where he sells a variety of products—annuities and life, health, and p/c insurance—for XYZ Insurance Group. Brian is a

Career agent

Multiple-line exclusive agent

Salaried sales representative

Lucy Champion has a contract with ABC Life and sells primarily that company's life, health, and annuity products to individual clients. Lucy is a

Career agent

Multiple-line exclusive agent

Salaried sales representative

Natasha Platkin is paid a salary to service group products. Natasha is a

Career agent

Multiple-line exclusive agent

Salaried sales representative

D2C distribution can occur through which of the following engagement methods? Choose all that apply.

Face-to-face

Online

Phone

Mail

Email

Social media

Learning Objectives

Explain how insurers provide sales support such as recruiting, licensing, compensation, and training to different types of agents.

List three prohibited sales practices, and describe the activities that insurers undertake to monitor the market conduct of their agents.

As you learned, a direct sales force is one channel for direct distribution. A key factor in choosing a distribution channel is how much support the channel will need, and it takes a lot of effort to support a direct sales force. Insurance companies often have agency operations units dedicated to direct sales force support. These are put in place to ensure that

Strategic goals and objectives for the company's agency operations are consistent with the insurer's overall goals and objectives

Policies and procedures for agency operations are in compliance with all regulatory requirements

Performance standards are in place to measure agency operations

Home office services, including customer service, are in place to adequately support agency operations

Home office support for an insurer's direct sales channel can be divided into six primary categories. Let's start with Recruiting.

Recruiting

Insurers spend a lot of money to recruit and train new career agents. They need to make sure they spend that money on the right people, and that the right people stay. Let's look at an example.

Joe Sanchez is a typical Forthright career agent. Forthright recruited him almost three years ago. He'd been working in his uncle's auto dealership, so he had some sales skills. But Joe was looking for a change.

How did Forthright find Joe? Like most recruits, Joe sent in an application and had interviews and tests at a field office. But the home office helped by setting recruitment guidelines and encouraging agency managers to use certain recruiting tools supplied by vendors.

What types of tools are there?

Once Joe completed the screening phase, he became eligible for pre-contract training. During pre-contract training, Joe learned

The principles of life insurance and annuities

The products and practices of the hiring company

How to meet customer needs with various products

Joe performed well during pre-contract training, so he was offered a formal contract with Forthright.

Licensing

Joe became licensed by each jurisdiction in which he will sell insurance and for each line of insurance—for example, life, health, P&C—he planned to sell. As is required in the United States, the home office makes sure that he sells policies only in states where he is licensed.

To obtain a license in the United States, Joe was required to

Pay a licensing fee

Pass a written examination in each line of insurance he planned to sell

Provide assurance that he is of reputable character

Other countries have requirements that are similar to those in the United States.

Appointment

Forthright appointed Joe to be its agent by notifying the appropriate insurance regulators that it authorized him to sell for it.

Joe isn't alone, though. Anyone who sells policies on behalf of Forthright—even bank employees—must be licensed and appointed. Licensing specialists in an insurer's home office oversee agent licensing to ensure that all agents are qualified to sell the company's products and are appropriately licensed and appointed for the jurisdictions in which they are to offer products.

It's really important that home office employees in charge of handling contracting, licensing, and appointments work effectively and efficiently. If they don't, salespeople can't sell. And woe to any employee who lets a sale go through if it's in a state where the agent isn't licensed—fines, potential lawsuits, headaches all around.

Training

Because he is an affiliated agent, Forthright wants to ensure Joe is well trained. The level of training provided to agents varies depending upon the agent's experience and relationship with the insurance company. However, insurers are required by law to ensure that all of their agents are trained in market conduct laws and acceptable sales practices.

New agents like Joe typically go through an initial period of sales, product, and on-the-job training

Experienced agents receive mostly product training because they do not need training in needs analysis and general sales techniques

Insurers conduct agent training in formal classes at the home office, regional sales offices, or the agency office, or through virtual training featuring online classrooms or self-study courses.

Other Sales Support

Forthright provides various other types of sales support to agents like Joe, for example

Office space and materials in a field office

Business development support to identify qualified sales leads for the agent

Marketing support to promote the general quality of their agents

Sales aids such as introductory sales kits and sample sales presentations

Enhanced service support for highly valued agents, such as
o Exclusive telephone lines for sales and operations support
o Extended hours of telephone support
o Dedicated personnel in areas such as underwriting, customer service, and claims

ID theft protection

Advanced underwriting to help with proposals or accompany the agent to sales presentations, especially when the agent intends to use insurance products in a financial plan or in estate planning

Technology support to promote operational efficiencies

Compensation

Joe doesn't work for free, of course. Forthright motivates him to make quality sales and to keep the policies in force by paying two types of commissions: first-year and renewal.

Generally speaking, first-year commission rates for life insurance policies range from 40 to 90 percent of the policy's first-year premium. First-year commissions are designed to generate sales.

Renewal commission rates are lower than the first-year commission rate—usually 2 to 5 percent of premiums received—and they are paid only on policies that remain in force. Renewal commissions are designed to encourage agents to sell quality business that will remain in force.

Sometimes insurers pay a service fee, which is a small percentage of premiums, often 1 or 2 percent, that is payable after renewal commissions have ceased. Service fees are typically paid to the agent who is currently providing service to the customer, even if that agent did not originally sell the policy.

Annuity commission schedules often combine a one-time commission on each contribution the owner makes to the annuity with a trail commission that is paid at regular intervals and is calculated as a percentage of the accumulated contract value.

Commission rates for financial professionals who are not part of a direct field force are typically higher than the commission rates for direct field force agents. Direct field force agents receive lower commissions because they typically receive some financial benefits that are not provided to the other types of agents.

Market Conduct

Insurers must regularly monitor the sales activities of all of their agents to ensure compliance with market conduct laws. And whenever regulatory requirements change, insurers are required to communicate the changes to agents accurately and promptly.

An insurer's monitoring system should include a method for identifying and reporting agents who are found to be unsuitable to sell insurance products. Some infractions are unintentional and merely indicate a need for additional training. However, serious infractions, or a pattern of minor infractions, require disciplinary action, up to and including a termination of the agency contract.

Regulators may suspend or revoke the license of an agent who engages in conduct that violates the laws in that jurisdiction. Unlawful sales practices include churning, twisting, and rebating.

The figure below illustrates how a company supports an agent to prepare him for sales.

True or False?

Renewal commission rates are higher than the first-year commission rate.

True

False

Oliver had his contract terminated for repeatedly engaging in the prohibited sales practice known as churning. What did Oliver do?

Induced clients to replace one policy after another, multiple times, so that he could earn a series of first-year commissions on the replacements.

Misrepresented the features of a policy to induce the customer to replace an existing policy.

Offered an unlawful cash payment to encourage a prospect to purchase a policy.

Learning Objective

Discuss the different intermediaries through which some insurers distribute their products.

In an intermediary distribution system, the insurance company relies on intermediaries to communicate with and offer products and services to its customers. These intermediaries include independent agents, broker-dealers, RIAs, banks, other insurance companies, and employee benefits brokers. Insurance companies often employ internal wholesalers or contract with external wholesalers, who promote the insurer's products and provide support to intermediaries.

Independent Agents

Insurance agents whose activities are not dictated by a single insurance company are known as independent agents. Some independent agents—called personal-producing general agents (PPGAs)—enter into agency contracts with one or more insurers. Others serve as brokers by representing their own customers.

Similarities between PPGAs and brokers:

Sell more than one insurer's products

Typically are experienced financial professionals

Are largely responsible for their own office space and other expenses

Require little training other than on specific products

Differences between PPGAs and brokers:

Insurers who use brokers often try to get as many of them as they can in an area, pay them a straight commission, provide few services, and often receive relatively little business from each broker

Insurers who use PPGAs are more selective, compensate the agents more generously, provide some degree of support services, and usually get substantial amounts of business from each PPGA

Sometimes, an independent agent does not do business directly with the insurance company. Instead, the financial professional submits business to the insurance company through an additional intermediary layer known as an independent distribution network. Other terms used for an independent distribution network include independent marketing organization (IMO) and brokerage general agent (BGA). Although there may have been clear distinctions between an IMO and a BGA in the past, today the distinctions between the two types of organizations have blurred, and organizations using both names perform similar functions.

IMOs and BGAs provide services to independent agents that depend on the needs and experience of the financial professionals, and can include

Agent recruiting and appointment

Education and training

Point-of-sale support

Business development support, such as help with lead generation

Agency management support

Assistance with case submission

Specialty underwriting expertise

Here's how an independent distribution network (or IMO or BGA) helps an application get from the point of sale to new business.

Broker-Dealers

Not to be confused with an insurance broker, a broker-dealer is a financial institution that buys and sells securities either for itself or for its customers. Common securities are stocks, bonds, and mutual funds. In the United States, securities can be distributed only through a broker-dealer that is registered with the federal Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and is a member of the Financial Industry Regulatory Authority (FINRA).

In the United States, variable life insurance and variable annuity products are considered securities as well as insurance products. To distribute them, many insurers establish a subsidiary that is registered as a broker-dealer (known as an insurance broker-dealer).

Alternatively, an insurer can market the products through an unaffiliated broker-dealer. Broker-dealers range from small companies to huge, nationally known institutions.

Individuals who work for a broker-dealer selling securities and are appropriately registered with FINRA are known as registered representatives, or registered reps. An insurer's wholesalers provide education and support materials to registered reps regarding the insurer's products and promote the advantages of the insurer's products compared to other insurers' products.

Registered Investment Advisers

Registered investment advisers (RIAs) provide advice on investing in securities. Larger investment adviser firms—those that manage more than $100 million in client assets—register with the SEC, while smaller investment adviser firms register with state securities authorities.

Many RIAs charge their clients an annual fee based on the value of all the investments the adviser manages on the client’s behalf (known as assets under management, or AUM). Therefore, insurers have created products that provide for the adviser to be paid a fee that is a percentage of the account value, rather than to be compensated by commission. Some broker-dealers also switched in recent years from commissions to fee-based compensation.

Banks and Other Depository Institutions

In the United States, banks and other depository institutions generally cannot issue insurance, but they can distribute insurers' products. When banks offer insurance, a licensed insurance company must underwrite and accept the risk associated with the insurance products the bank sells. Also, banks are not permitted to make the purchase of an insurance product a condition for a customer's obtaining a loan from the bank.

Banks typically use two types of financial professionals to distribute insurance and annuity products:

Platform employees, who usually sell simple life insurance products such as term life and refer customers with complex financial needs to financial consultants

Financial consultants, who are licensed to sell securities as well as life insurance and annuities

The amount and type of sales support that an insurance company provides for the bank distribution channel varies and is often provided to banks by wholesalers. Insurance companies typically pay commissions to banks. Banks then decide how to compensate their employees for the sales.

Only bank employees who are licensed insurance agents may receive commissions for the sale of insurance. However, unlicensed bank employees who refer customers to licensed bank employees sometimes receive a nominal referral fee.

Other Insurance Companies

An insurance company can act as an intermediary distribution channel for another insurer by selling nonproprietary products. By selling nonproprietary products, an insurer can provide its customers with a full range of financial products. Insurers often distribute nonproprietary products because they are able to

Offer a new product without spending the money or time necessary to develop the product

Enter a new market quickly

Decrease the risks involved with introducing a new product by experimenting with selling that product or product line before fully committing to it

Better satisfy customers by offering a more complete product mix

The insurer that developed the product gains access to the distributing insurer's financial professionals and their clients.

Employee Benefits Brokers

As we've discussed, many employers choose to make additional insurance options available to their employees as voluntary benefits, which are paid for entirely by the employee. Insurers generally distribute voluntary benefit products through commission-based financial professionals.
