A cybercriminal who has a policyowner's PII can gain control of a policy and make changes, take out a loan against the policy, or even take a policy's entire cash value.

Using multifaceted authentication for all customer transactions can help CSRs detect attempted account takeovers.

Averting Account Takeover Fraud

A cybercriminal attempts to take over a Forthright customer's account.

The Forthright CSR sends an email or text to the customer that contains a real-time code.

In legitimate transactions, the customer supplies the code to the CSR. In fraudulent ones, the customer is alerted to the attempted account takeover.

Account takeover fraud is averted.

Insurers use a number of techniques to prevent and detect fraudulent acts. Which of these is a technology that allows insurers to spot potential fraud by analyzing large amounts of data on past incidents of fraud in comparison with the data from a set of transactions currently under review?

Cybersecurity initiatives

Predictive analytics

Antifraud software

Multifaceted authentication

Data breaches occur when cybercriminals gain access to a company's information about its customers, including personally identifiable information (PII). Cybercriminals can use PII to engage in

Account takeover fraud

Phishing

Securities fraud

Social engineering

section: Why Is the Customer's Experience Important?
text:
Learning Objective

Describe how managing customers' experiences benefits customers and companies.

Everyone knows that providing excellent service is important. But why?

\

Providing excellent customer service is important because it (choose all that apply)

Increases productivity

Results in cost savings

Enhances a company's image and reputation

Enables a company to attract and retain better employees and financial professionals

Enables a company to gain a competitive edge

Attracts new customers

Builds customer loyalty

Retains customers

Results in greater profitability

Providing excellent customer service is important because it (choose all that apply)

Customer Experience Management (CEM)

More and more in the twenty-first century, customer service is considered one part of the larger concept of customer experience.

Customer experience is the impression that a customer forms over time as the result of a series of encounters with the company. The customer experience is a product of the customer journey, which is the path each individual travels with the company, including every interaction across every touchpoint and every communication channel. An insurance customer's journey can last for many years and include many touchpoints, from the first time that the customer goes online to research insurance until, many years later, the customer or the customer's beneficiary receives the policy benefit or annuity contract value.

Most companies consider managing the customer experience to be a necessity to compete in today's business climate. Customer experience management (CEM) is a business strategy through which an organization manages all aspects of its interactions with current and potential customers. The strategy seeks to optimize the customer's interactions at every stage of the customer journey, including

Shopping (recognizing a need, researching companies and products, comparing, getting a quote)

Purchase (applying for and purchasing the policy)

In-Force (maintaining, refining, and reviewing the policy)

Claims (filing a claim, approval, payment)

Note that the customer service department is primarily involved at the in-force stage. Customer experience management involves more than the customer service department: it is an enterprise-wide initiative.

Customer experience management also seeks to optimize customer interactions across all communication channels through omnichannel service. To study customers' interactions with a company, insurers create customer journey maps depicting customers' typical touchpoints with the company and the possible communication channels through which customers might experience each touchpoint.

Companies also create customer personas, which are fictional representations of real-life customers belonging to different market segments with different needs and interests. For example, a retiree persona might prefer different communication channels than a Generation X persona or a Millennial persona. Companies can create different customer journey maps for different customer personas and use them to create personalized customer experience for each customer.

Voice of the Customer (VOC)

To create customer personas and journey maps, companies need to understand their customers. To do this, companies capture the voice of the customer (VOC) by gathering customer data. (Click or touch each box below to learn more.)

Active Voice of the Customer Data

Active VOC data is feedback that the company has solicited from customers, such as responses to survey questions regarding interactions with the company.

Passive Voice of the Customer Data

Passive VOC data is unsolicited customer behavior or feedback—information that customers reveal without being asked. For example, companies gather data about how customers behave when they visit the company website.

Companies also analyze customer calls to the contact center to gather VOC data. Customer service managers have traditionally listened to and analyzed customer service calls; many now also use speech analytics—software that analyzes not only the words and phrases that customers use, but also their tone of voice and voice changes that can provide information about their emotional states—as a source for collecting passive VOC data.

A longstanding customer service principle is that customer service is the responsibility of every employee at a company, not just "customer-facing" employees. Customer-facing employees interact directly with external customers; for non-customer-facing employees, their customers are internal customers. Employees need to provide high-quality service to their internal customers because that service eventually affects the experience of external customers. For example, what if an IT technician is slow in repairing a customer-facing employee's computer? Fewer customer-facing employees may be available to assist external customers, and customers will have to wait longer for service.

Customer Loyalty

From the customer's point of view, the benefit of CEM is receiving products and services that meet their needs. From the company's point of view, the goal is to maximize customer loyalty. Each successful touchpoint has the potential to increase a customer's loyalty. Among the many advantages to a company of customer loyalty are the following:

New business through referrals and positive reviews in public forums

A dependable revenue stream

Opportunities for additional purchases

Data that can be used to further improve the customer experience

In an insurance company today, the activities of the customer service department are typically part of an enterprise-wide strategy that is driven by data and technology. Given the numerous channels that customers can use to communicate with companies in the twenty-first century, customer service employees strive to provide a consistent, high-quality experience to all customers, no matter how they choose to interact with the company.

How Technology Affects Cx

Technology has become an important part of life for most people. For most companies, it is also an important way of doing business.

section: Why Is the Customer's Experience Important?
text:
Seamless Processes

Insurers provide effective customer service and encourage customer loyalty by providing a seamless customer service process through a variety of service options. A seamless process is a smooth process designed so that a customer is not inconvenienced by—or even aware of—the steps involved in fulfilling the customer's request. Many automated systems, such as workflow systems and document management systems, are essential technologies that together allow an insurer to provide a seamless customer service process.

Personalized Customer Service

Technology also helps in providing personalized customer service through a variety of service options. Most insurers offer customers a choice of self-service, human-assisted service, or a combination of self-service and human-assisted service. Some people want customer service to be conveniently available anytime and anywhere, and technology lets insurers offer self-service options to meet this need.

Data Collection

Technology also allows insurance companies to gather useful VOC data about customers. Companies that don't collect and analyze data about their customers can only make assumptions about what their customers want and need. Companies that continually monitor the activities of their customers and their competitors are able to gain a deeper understanding of their customers' wants and needs, develop better ways to satisfy those needs, and improve the customer experience.

Artificial Intelligence (AI)

Insurance companies increasingly use artificial intelligence (AI) technology to provide self-service options while streamlining company operations. One type of AI commonly used is a chatbot. Chatbots are robotic CSRs or agents that can recognize and interpret human language and provide information. Customers communicating with a chatbot may be unaware that they are not talking to an actual person.

A customer's feeling of attachment to or preference for a company's people, products, or services is

Service recovery

Customer retention

Customer loyalty

Customer satisfaction

True or False?

Journey maps follow real customers.

True

False

Indicate whether each of the following is an example of active or passive VOC data collection.

Collecting data about the frequency of complaints by using speech analytics software on a sampling of recorded customer phone calls

Active

Passive

Having customers rate customer service after a call to the contact center

Active

Passive

Learning Objective

Describe how insurers monitor and evaluate their customer service.

In an insurance company, managers evaluate customer service by using qualitative and quantitative performance measurements. Performance measurement is process through which a company

Decides what activities are key to the achievement of the company's goals and objectives

Decides how to measure the performance of those activities

Decides what performance standards it hopes to achieve

Gathers the measurements

Communicates the results

Which of these is an example of a qualitative performance measurement?

The abandonment rate is below 5 percent.

CSRs handle all telephone calls with courtesy and professionalism.

CSRs respond to all written requests for beneficiary changes within two days of receipt.

CSRs process 90 percent of all transaction requests within one week of receipt.

Qualitative Performance Measurements

Certain types of customer satisfaction surveys, monitoring, and mystery shopping are examples of qualitative customer service performance measurements. Click or touch each heading below to learn more.

Customer Satisfaction Surveys

A customer satisfaction survey helps a company determine whether its products, services, and prices are meeting customer expectations. A well-designed survey can also measure customer loyalty.

Some insurers send a short general survey to randomly selected customers. Other insurers conduct a short survey after each transaction. For example, a customer may be asked to complete an automated survey at the end of a telephone call. Customers may be asked to comment on an individual CSR's timeliness, accuracy, and professionalism. In addition, customers may be asked if the CSR made them feel welcome or valued during the interaction.

Qualitative survey questions are open-ended, such as "What was your reason for calling today?" Open-ended questions can reveal not only how customers feel about an interaction (or the company), but also why they feel that way.

Monitoring

Monitoring is a process used to review and evaluate the quality of customer service interactions either as they happen or after the fact. Managers may listen to live or recorded customer service calls between customers and CSRs to see what is happening.

Many companies monitor all of their contacts with customers—not just those involving telephones. Complaints, for example, provide qualitative data in the form of letters, emails, social media posts, and phone calls. Online data collection methods are especially useful for monitoring complaints.

Monitoring complaints that come from customers, employees, financial professionals, regulatory agencies, and consumer groups helps a company identify recurring service problems, service gaps, and process breakdowns. An insurer can use the information obtained from complaints to improve its processes and its communication with customers, with the goal of improving the customer experience.

Mystery Shoppers

Another way to gather qualitative information about CSRs' performance is through mystery shoppers. Mystery shoppers are either employees unknown to the CSR or independent contractors that conduct transactions as if they were real customers. At the end of the transaction, the mystery shopper records details about how the CSR managed—or failed to manage—the interaction on a standardized response form. The questions included in the form depend on the specific responses the company wants to evaluate.

Start

This CSR knows her procedures, and she handled the "customer's" request properly and thoroughly. She showed empathy to the mystery shopper, as well. Empathy is the ability to understand another person's emotional state and imagine how you would feel in a similar situation. That caring attitude creates satisfied and loyal customers.

Quantitative Performance Measurements

Insurance companies use quantitative performance measurements to evaluate aspects of customer service such as speed, accuracy, and timeliness. Other quantitative performance measures assess productivity, which is often an indication of how quickly and efficiently a process is working.

Quantitative performance measurements are useful because they generate concrete information about people and their behaviors that can be analyzed, summarized in the form of numbers, and easily communicated to others.

Example

The source, number, frequency, and nature of customer complaints help detect customer service problems or trends.

Forthright compares these quantitative measurements with established benchmarks to identify areas needing improvement.

The most frequently used quantitative performance measurements are surveys. In surveys designed to gather quantitative data, a company asks a question—usually multiple choice—and it gets an answer in the form of a number, such as a service rating on a 5-point scale or a percentage of customers satisfied with how a CSR addressed their needs or concerns. Click or touch in the boxes below to learn more.

Quantitative Survey Question

On a scale of 1 to 5, with 1 representing poor service and 5 representing exceptional service, how would you rank the service received during this call?

Qualitative Survey Question

Based on the current call, how would you improve the service you received?

Many surveys have both types of questions because quantitative data provides numerical results that reveal what people think about a company, and the open-ended qualitative questions allow customers to give details about why they feel that way.

Beneficiary Melodia Jimenez recently called Forthright to file a life insurance claim. Below are questions on the survey she received from Forthright after the call. For each question, indicate whether the survey question is an example of a qualitative or quantitative performance measurement.

How satisfied are you with the service you received during your recent call with Forthright?

Very satisfied

Somewhat satisfied

Neither satisfied nor dissatisfied

Somewhat dissatisfied

Very dissatisfied

Qualitative

Quantitative

How could Forthright Financial improve your experience filing a claim?

Metrics

Which of the following are metrics that a company might use to measure success? Choose all that apply.

Call turnaround time

Customer retention rate

Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)

Error rate

Successful insurance companies periodically evaluate their current processes and operations to identify ways they can improve and provide an exceptional customer experience.

A company's metrics are the quantitative performance measurements that it chooses to monitor to measure the success of its processes and key tasks. The types of metrics a company employs indicate the company's priorities as well as reflect on the company's performance.

Successfully implemented metrics

Provide focus for the company, department, or employee

Help company leaders make important decisions

Identify ways to improve the performance of the company and its employees

Operational Metrics

Operational metrics measure the efficiency and effectiveness of company processes. Such metrics often help measure the success or failure of customer contact centers.

The operational metrics commonly used by insurance companies to measure customer service performance include accessibility, timeliness, accuracy, productivity, and quality. Click or touch each heading below to learn more.

Accessibility

One important customer service operational metric is accessibility—how easily customers can reach someone at the insurance company. Accessibility measures do not evaluate whether the customer's request was completed. They only measure whether someone answered the telephone or acknowledged an email and how long the customer waited for a response. Examples of accessibility measures include

Service level. The percentage of inbound customer contacts the CSR answers within a specified time frame—for example, 90 percent within 20 seconds.

Abandonment rate. The percentage of inbound telephone calls that are automatically placed on hold and then terminated by the caller before the call is answered by someone who can meet customer needs.

Number of blocked calls. The number of inbound telephone calls that cannot get through to the customer contact center and instead encounter a busy signal or are transferred to voicemail or to a message to call back later.

Average speed of answer. The average time in seconds to answer a telephone call or, depending on the context, the average time in seconds or minutes that telephone callers are on hold before being connected to someone who can meet customer needs.

Misdirected calls. The number or percentage of inbound telephone callers who are transferred to the wrong department.

Timeliness

Timeliness (or turnaround time) is a general term for the time it takes to complete a customer-initiated request or transaction, such as answering a question, underwriting an application, processing a claim, issuing a check, or processing a withdrawal or an account transfer.

Insurance companies often use timeliness metrics as an indicator of how efficient their service providers are. Companies can gather additional information by measuring response time and first-contact resolution rates.

Productivity

Productivity is often an indication of how quickly and efficiently a process is working. Examples of productivity measures include processes completed, cost-per-call, and contacts-per-service provider.

Quality

The quality rate indicates the number of particular customer service transactions that result in no reported errors. The inverse of the quality rate is the error rate, which indicates the percentage of transactions that result in errors.

Both are often expressed as a percentage of the total number of transactions. For example, if 99 out of 100 transactions have no reported errors, the quality rate is 99 percent, while the error rate is 1 percent.

Service Metrics

Service metrics are measures of how happy customers are with the service a company provides.

Insurers use data to measure customers' satisfaction with the service they receive from the company. Common service metrics life insurance companies use to measure customer satisfaction are retention rates, Customer Satisfaction Score, and Net Promoter Score. Click or touch each heading below to learn more.

Retention Rates

An insurer can evaluate service quality in terms of the number of customers who are satisfied enough to remain with the company. Two commonly used measures of retention used by life insurance companies are the customer retention rate and the lapse rate.

The customer retention rate evaluates the amount of business that remains in force through a certain period of time. The inverse of the customer retention rate is the lapse rate, which indicates the percentage of business in force at the beginning of a certain time period that terminates during the period.

Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)

Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT) is a measure of a customer's satisfaction with the services received from a company, expressed as a percentage. For example, CSAT may be the percentage of all customers who complete an after-call survey who say they were completely satisfied with the service they received. CSAT is measured using highly customized surveys designed to help insurers identify their specific strengths and weaknesses.

Net Promoter Score (NPS)

Net Promoter Score specifically measures customer loyalty. Net Promoter Score (NPS) asks customers to rank their likelihood of recommending a company or product on a scale of 1–10 and then subtracts the percentage of detractors (customers who ranked their likelihood of recommending as 0–6) from the percentage of promoters (customers who ranked their likelihood of recommending as 9 or 10).

Benchmarking

Using relevant metrics, insurers measure any improvements to or deterioration in service through benchmarking. Benchmarking is the process of

Identifying the best outcomes that other companies have achieved for an activity or process

Determining what practices produced those outcomes

Implementing the best practices to equal or surpass the best outcomes

Benchmarking surveys are often conducted by independent third parties to give insurers an idea of what other companies' goals are and how successful they are at meeting those goals. In effect, benchmarking allows companies to improve service by implementing processes and procedures that have already been developed and shown to be effective.

Insurers choose specific operational and service metrics to serve as benchmarks. For example, if Forthright's CSRs have an error rate (an operational metric) of 3 percent, the company would compare its error rate to the average error rate for all life insurance companies. If the average error rate is only 1 percent, Forthright may set a benchmark of 1 percent for its error rate. Over time, Forthright would take steps to decrease the number of CSR errors, using the error rate metric to gauge progress toward the benchmark.

From January 1 to December 31, Forthright Financial had a customer retention rate of 90 percent. This means that the lapse rate was

10 percent

90 percent

Determine whether each metric is an operational metric or service metric.

Operational Metric

Service Metric

Average speed of answer

Operational Metric

Service Metric

Accuracy

Operational Metric

Service Metric

Net Promotor Score

Operational Metric

Service Metric

Quality rate

Operational Metric

Service Metric

A customer's contact with an insurer most likely begins when they are thinking about purchasing a policy, and can last until the policyowner dies and the insurer pays the policy proceeds to the beneficiary. In between, the insurer underwrites and issues the policy, and meets the customer’s service needs while the policy is in force. In this module, you’ll learn about the tasks an insurer performs at each stage of the policy lifecycle.

In this module, you’ll learn about

How insurers engage with potential customers and distribute products to meet customer needs

How insurers process insurance applications, evaluate the risk they represent, and determine the appropriate premium to charge

How insurers try to optimize the experience of their customers

How insurers balance paying legitimate claims promptly and accurately with avoiding paying illegitimate claims

If a policy stays in force until the death of the insured, the insurer has an obligation to pay the policy proceeds promptly and accurately. However, the insurer also has an obligation NOT to pay illegitimate claims. See how insurers balance these two obligations.

Explain the purpose of effective claim administration, and describe the staff involved in processing claims.

Identify the steps in the claim process, and explain how to determine whether death benefits are payable.

Determine the correct amount and correct recipient of a death benefit, and describe how the benefit can be paid.

Identify the factors that are likely to trigger a claim investigation, and describe different types of claim fraud and measures insurers can take to prevent them.

Describe the Model Unfair Claims Settlement Practices Act, and give examples of unfair claim practices that would result in a violation of this act.

Explain various ways that insurers improve and evaluate claim processing efficiency.

Learning Objective

Explain the purpose of effective claim administration and describe the staff involved in processing claims.

Through claims, a company fulfills its promise to policyowners and beneficiaries—prompt payment of policy benefits when an insured event occurs.

Effective claim administration requires satisfying two somewhat contradictory objectives.

Claims should be processed as rapidly as possible to meet the needs of beneficiaries and comply with claims processing regulations.

Companies must ensure that they are paying only valid claims. A small number of life insurance claims are submitted under erroneous interpretations of policy provisions. An even smaller number of claims are submitted fraudulently.

Inaccurate or ineffective claims practices can cost an insurer financially and in reputation. That's why companies establish standard procedures to ensure effective claims processing. In this lesson, we discuss how a company reviews claim information and determines whether benefits are payable.

Claim Philosophy and Authority Levels

Effective claim processing begins with a claim philosophy and established claim practices that guide the claim department's operations.

Forthright's Claim Philosophy

To act promptly, fairly, and courteously in paying all eligible claims and denying all ineligible claims.

Can all claim analysts process all kinds of claims?

Yes

No

It depends

Claim paying authority is tiered. A claim analyst's authority gradually increases based on his experience and training. The following is an example of how tiered authority works. Of course, the specific amounts may vary from company to company.

Amount of Claim Approval Authority

| Employee | Routine Claims | Higher-Risk Claims |
|---|---|---|
| Analyst Trainee | $25,000 claim amount | No authority |
| Analyst | $200,000 claim amount | No authority |
| Senior Claim Analyst | $600,000 claim amount | $600,000 claim amount |
| Claim Supervisor | $700,000 claim amount | $700,000 claim amount |
| Claim Manager | $1,000,000 claim amount | $1,000,000 claim amount |

Note that claim analysts are authorized to approve higher claim amounts for routine claims that are not within the contestable period than they can approve for higher risk claims. Higher risk claims involve the accidental death benefit provision, the incontestability provision, or suspicion of fraud or unusual legal complications, which usually must be handled by more senior claims staff.

True or False?

The goal of claim administration departments is to pay all claims immediately.

True

False

Learning Objective

Identify the steps in the claim process and explain how to determine whether death benefits are payable.

The process begins when the claimant—usually the beneficiary or policyowner—notifies the insurer of a loss.

The insurer then asks the claimant to complete a claim form, or claimant's statement, that provides information about the claim and authorizes medical caregivers, government agencies, employers, and others to provide the insurer with information relevant to the claim.

The basic claim form requires that the claimant provide the date, place, cause, and circumstance of the insured's death. If the death occurred within a specified period after policy issue, typically two years, or if the death was accidental or unusual in any way, the insurer may require that the claimant complete more details on the claim form or complete a specialized questionnaire.

Once the claims department receives the claim form, it begins the claim process. We will discuss the following steps in the process.

Determining if Benefits Are Payable

Here's what a claim analyst must verify to determine whether death benefits are payable:

The policy was in force when the loss occurred.

The deceased is the insured.

The loss occurred.

The policy covers the loss.

The analyst also examines whether the policy is contestable and, if so, whether the application for insurance contains any material misrepresentations.

Policy Status, Deceased's Identity, and Proof of Loss

Determining whether the policy was in force when the insured died—had premiums been paid?—and whether the deceased was the insured are typically straightforward.

Determining whether the loss occurred may be trickier. The claimant must supply proof of loss. In the United States, an official death certificate is the accepted proof of loss. But, a death certificate can't be issued if there's no body.

So what happens if the body's missing?

When an insured disappears, the claimant is unable to present proof of the insured's death. Whether or not the disappearance can be explained generally determines how the claim analyst proceeds. Click or touch each heading below to learn more.

Explainable Disappearance

The claim analyst may presume the insured to be dead if a specific peril can reasonably explain the disappearance, such as a plane crash in the ocean with no bodies found.

He probably would accept the claimant's statement and accident reports as proof of death and would continue with the claim evaluation.

Unexplainable Disappearance

If no specific peril can reasonably explain the disappearance, the claim analyst usually denies a life claim that is filed immediately after the disappearance.

An insurer cannot approve a claim until it receives sufficient proof of the insured's death.

In the United States, if the insured hasn't reappeared after a certain period (five or seven years, depending on the jurisdiction), the claimant can petition the court for an order presuming the insured's death.

A court order stating that the insured is presumed dead establishes proof of the insured's death. If the policy has been kept in force during the disappearance, the claim analyst usually will accept the court order as proof of loss and will continue the claim evaluation.

Policy Exclusions

A claim analyst next determines whether the policy covers the loss. One reason it may not is a policy exclusion, such as the suicide exclusion.

When an insured person dies during the suicide exclusion period, the claim analyst determines whether the cause of death was suicide. If the insurer can prove it was, it refunds the premiums paid, less any outstanding policy loans.

Sometimes when completing an application for insurance, the applicant gives false information or leaves out important information. If such a misrepresentation is important to the insurer's evaluation of the risk, it's called a material misrepresentation.

Most misrepresentations are minor—the applicant states that the insured broke his left arm, when actually it was the right arm. Here are some examples of material misrepresentation.

| Type of Information | Example of Material Misrepresentation |
|---|---|
| Health | Failure to disclose a known medical impairment that adversely affects life expectancy |
| Occupation or avocation | Failure to disclose a dangerous job or hobby |
| Habits | Failure to disclose past abuse of alcohol or controlled substances |
| Number of traffic violations | Misstatements of the number or severity of moving violations or collisions |
| Other insurance applications denied or rated | Concealing information about insurance applications that have been denied, assigned to a high-risk class, or charged an additional premium |

Normally, if an insured dies during a life insurance policy's contestable period and if the claim analyst finds out that the application contains a material misrepresentation, the insurer may rescind (void) the policy. The legal process of voiding an insurance contract because of material misrepresentation is known as rescission. The insurer refunds the premiums paid minus any policy loan outstanding. In the United States, life insurance policies typically provide a contestable period of two years. The contestable period may vary by state.

In many jurisdictions, the cause of the insured's death does not have to be related to the misrepresented information for the policy to be rescinded.

Think you can be a claim analyst? Analyze a few claims and decide which ones are payable.

November 17, 2013, Sarah Lawson, the policyowner-insured of an individual life policy, disappeared under unexplainable circumstances. Seven years later, on December 1, 2020, there was still no evidence of Ms. Lawson's reappearance, and a court issued an order presuming her death.

On December 15, 2020, the beneficiary of Ms. Lawson's policy submitted a claim for the policy proceeds. The insurer most likely will accept the court order as proof of loss and continue with the claim evaluation process, regardless of whether premiums for the policy were paid during the entire period of disappearance.

True

False

Antoine Blake purchased a whole life policy from the Barnard Insurance Company on June 12, 2017. The policy contained a typical two-year suicide exclusion period. On August 23, 2020, Mr. Blake committed suicide. At the time of his death, no policy loans were outstanding.

In this situation, the beneficiary of Mr. Blake's policy was entitled to receive

No payment of any kind

A refund of all premiums paid only

The policy's net cash surrender value only

The policy's full death benefit

Toby Barnard was analyzing a claim submitted during the policy's contestable period. He found out that the insured had not reported that he'd had a heart attack 18 months prior to policy issue. Had this information been known, the insurer wouldn't have issued the policy. The cause of death was cancer.

Toby was most likely able to deny the claim and rescind the policy due to material misrepresentation in the application.

True

False

section: Whom to Pay, How Much, and How
text:
Learning Objective

Determine the correct amount and correct recipient of a death benefit and describe how the benefit can be paid.

Determining the Policy Proceeds

After the claim analyst verifies a valid claim, the next step is to calculate the benefit payable. Most people think this is equal to the face amount of the policy, but that's not always true.

The face amount is the basic death benefit, but several different amounts could be added to or subtracted from it to get the final proceeds payable to the beneficiary. For example, the basic death benefit of some universal life insurance policies is equal to the policy's face amount plus the accumulated cash value plus applicable interest. The death benefit available under a variable universal life insurance policy fluctuates according to the performance of a separate account fund. Other factors may increase or decrease the amount of the proceeds payable.

| Additions | Subtractions |
|---|---|
| Premiums that the policyowner paid in advance | Outstanding policy loans |
| Accumulated policy dividends | Accrued policy loan interest |
| Policy dividends that the insurer declared but that remain unpaid | Premiums due and unpaid |
| Paid-up additional coverage that the policyowner purchased | Accelerated death benefits the insurer has paid |
| Accidental death benefits that may be payable |  |

The determination of whether accidental death benefits are payable can be challenging. An accidental death benefit is a supplementary benefit under which the insurer pays an amount of money in addition to the basic death benefit if the insured dies as a result of an accident.

Example

The life insurance policy insuring Vinny Acworth's life included an accidental death benefit. Mr. Acworth was hiking in the mountains along a high ledge and fell to his death. If Mr. Acworth slipped and fell, his death would be accidental, and policy proceeds would include the accidental death benefit. However, if Mr. Acworth's death was the result of a suicide (he jumped), no accidental death benefit is payable.

When evaluating a claim involving an accidental death benefit, all facts of the case are taken into consideration. The claim analyst may order autopsy reports, medical records, and police reports to gather enough information to determine whether the insurer is liable to pay accidental death benefits. If the benefits are payable, the insurer adds that amount to the policy's face amount.

Example

Assume that Mr. Acworth's death was ruled accidental and that the following information applies in his situation:

$100,000 face amount of policy

$50,000 accidental death benefit

$1,000 policy loan outstanding

$50 accrued policy loan interest

$500 accumulated policy dividends

The correct benefit amount payable for this policy would be $149,450.
$100,000 + $50,000 + $500 – $1,000 – $50 = $149,450

Adjusting for Misstatement of Age or Sex

Sometimes the insured's age or sex is misstated on the application. As a result, the insurer charges the incorrect premium for the policy. How does the insurer handle this error? The answer lies in the misstatement of age or sex provision found in most life policies. Let's look at an example.

When Allison Veasey applied for an insurance policy on her life from the Diamond Insurance Company, she mistakenly stated on her application that her age was 47, when her true age was 49. As a result, the insurer charged a lower premium than it would have charged if it had known her true age. A year after the policy went into effect, Ms. Veasey died. The policy beneficiary submitted a claim, and a claim analyst at Diamond discovered the misstatement of age on the policy application. What will Diamond do?

Rescind the policy on the basis of a material misrepresentation in the application and retain all premiums paid

Rescind the policy on the basis of a material misrepresentation in the application and refund all premiums paid

Reduce the amount of the proceeds to the amount that the premiums would have purchased for a 49-year-old insured

Note that if an insurer discovers a misstatement of age or sex while the insured is still alive, it will often offer the policyowner the option of adjusting the face amount or of maintaining the same face amount and adjusting the premiums payable for the policy.

Determining the Proper Payee

After figuring out the amount of policy proceeds, the claim analyst decides who is entitled to the proceeds. Sometimes claim analysts receive conflicting information about who is entitled to death benefits. At other times, they must decide how a claim should be paid.

In most cases, the insurer pays the death benefits to the primary beneficiary or beneficiaries named by the policyowner. If no primary beneficiary is alive at the time of the insured's death, the insurer will pay the benefits to the contingent beneficiary, or second contingent beneficiary if the contingent beneficiary is deceased. If a contingent or second contingent beneficiary cannot be paid, the proceeds will be payable to the policyowner. If the policyowner is deceased, then the proceeds will be payable to the policyowner's estate.

The claim analyst will do whatever he can to ensure that he identifies the proper payee, but there are instances when the beneficiary designation is ambiguous or two payees seem to have rights to the proceeds. The insurer will encourage the parties involved to resolve the issue, but occasionally, when the parties cannot agree, the insurer will take legal action through a process known as interpleader. The insurer goes to court, tells the court that it can't determine the proper recipient of the proceeds through no fault of its own, and asks the court to determine who has rights to the proceeds. Interpleader eliminates the risk that the insurer may have to pay proceeds more than once.

Determining the Proper Payment Method

A beneficiary can receive policy proceeds in a lump sum or under one of several settlement options.

Let's see what you already know about settlement options. Identify each settlement option described below.

section: Whom to Pay, How Much, and How
text:
Policy proceeds and interest are paid in equal installments for a specified period of time.

Life Income Option

Retained Asset Account (RAA) Option

Fixed-Period Option

Interest Option

Fixed-Amount Option

Policy proceeds and interest are paid in a series of equal payments for as long as the proceeds last.

Life Income Option

Retained Asset Account (RAA) Option

Fixed-Period Option

Interest Option

Fixed-Amount Option

Policy proceeds and interest are paid in a series of payments over the payee's lifetime.

Life Income Option

Retained Asset Account (RAA) Option

Fixed-Period Option

Interest Option

Fixed-Amount Option

Policy proceeds are deposited into an interest-bearing checking account in the payee's name.

Life Income Option

Retained Asset Account (RAA) Option

Fixed-Period Option

Interest Option

Fixed-Amount Option

The insurer invests the policy proceeds and periodically pays interest on these proceeds to the payee.

Life Income Option

Retained Asset Account (RAA) Option

Fixed-Period Option

Interest Option

Fixed-Amount Option

Annuity Death Benefit Process

Processing annuity death benefit claims is very similar to processing life insurance death benefit claims but differs in some ways. Use the right arrows below to look at the steps in this process.

1. Authenticating and Documenting the Claim

As with life insurance claims, annuity death benefit claims start with a claimant's statement and official death certificate.

2. Determining the Amount of the Death Benefit

The death benefit amount is usually the annuity's accumulated value on the day the insurer receives required documentation.

3. Addressing Applicable Tax Issues

Unlike life insurance death benefits, annuity death benefits typically result in taxable income to the beneficiary.

The insurer reports the total payment, the taxable portion of the payment, and any taxes withheld to the beneficiary and to tax authorities.

4. Paying the Death Benefit

The insurer pays the annuity death benefit as a lump sum or according to a settlement option chosen by the contract owner or beneficiary.

Yunel Mendoza was the insured under a $100,000 life insurance policy that included an accidental death benefit. After Mr. Mendoza died in an accident, a claim analyst approved the claim and determined the following amounts pertaining to the policy:

$300 in premiums paid in advance

A $3,000 outstanding policy loan

$200 in accrued policy loan interest

$5,000 paid up additional coverage

$400 in declared but unpaid policy dividends

$100,000 accidental death benefits

What are the total proceeds payable to the beneficiary?

$201,700

$202,500

$205,700

When Mickey Evans dies, his life insurance policy proceeds can't go to his named beneficiary, his wife Carla, because she is also dead. He named their adult son Alex as the contingent beneficiary, and his adult daughter Abigail as the second contingent beneficiary. Both children are still alive. In this case, who will receive the proceeds of his policy?

Alex

Abigail

Alex and Abigail will each receive half of the proceeds

Learning Objective

Identify the factors that are likely to trigger a claim investigation, and describe different types of claim fraud and measures insurers can take to prevent them.

Now that you've learned about the claim process, let's discuss claim investigations. What if a claim analyst can't determine Forthright's liability because

The claimant's statement and proof of loss document don't provide enough information or

The correct payee isn't apparent…or maybe…

Something just seems wrong

Any of these situations could trigger an investigation. Let's find out why.

Claim Investigations

If a claim analyst suspects material misrepresentation or fraud, the claim analyst launches a claim investigation, which is the process of obtaining the additional information necessary to make an appropriate claim decision. Although most claim investigations involve short, simple searches such as checking a medical record, a few can be quite extensive. For example, in more complicated cases, a claim analyst might call upon the special investigative unit (SIU), which is composed of representatives of the claim, legal, and internal audit departments, as well as independent investigators.

Sources of additional information for a claim investigation can include:

Medical records

Motor vehicle records

Criminal court records

Autopsy Reports

Investigative consumer reports

Any other source that might help

Some investigations are quick and simple. Others are extensive. Select all factors that might make the claim investigation extensive.

Death is unusual given the insured's age

Mysterious death

Death occurred during suicide exclusion period or contestable period

Policy has an exclusion rider

Death is from natural causes

Policy has a relatively high face amount

Death happened abroad

When investigating claims, Forthright must

Follow all privacy laws

Obtain information by only lawful, reasonable, and ethical means

Protect the confidentiality of information

In many jurisdictions, conducting an investigation under a false pretext is prohibited.

In addition to complying with legal requirements, insurers like Forthright must also carefully weigh the potential benefits from a claim investigation against the costs. The costs of a claim investigation—staff salaries and the costs related to independent investigators—can be quite expensive. Generally, the more complex the investigation, the higher the cost involved.

Claim Fraud

Only an insured or a beneficiary can commit claim fraud.

True

False

There are also numerous types of fraud for insurers to address.

Examples of Claim Fraud

A man purchases life insurance on several homeless men, names himself as beneficiary, and then receives the policy proceeds after the insureds die.

A claim analyst issues claim checks for the death benefits on life policies covering two nonexistent children. She invents police reports, doctors' reports, and even autopsy reports supporting the claims.

A life insurance applicant pays a healthier person to take his medical examination so the applicant will be accepted by the insurer.

A claim analyst approves a claim for life insurance benefits on a friend's mother, even though he knows the mother is still alive.

A beneficiary claims that the insured died as a result of an accident in an attempt to receive the policy's accidental death benefit, even though the insured died of natural causes.

Preventing Claim Fraud

Some jurisdictions require insurers to take certain measures to prevent, detect, investigate, and prosecute claim fraud, and some states require insurers to form SIUs to investigate suspected cases of fraud. Claim analysts and SIU staff members receive training in detecting and investigating insurance claim fraud and in applying the provisions of unfair claim practices statutes. In addition, many state insurance departments have established their own fraud investigation units. Insurers also establish continuous education programs so employees can maintain awareness of existing and new fraudulent activities to minimize their impact on insurance operations.

Many insurers use data analytics to help them detect claim fraud. By analyzing the large amount of data they own relating to past claims, insurers can identify situations that indicate a heightened risk of fraud. For example, an insurer might discover that claimants who act in a certain way are more likely to be committing claim fraud. Such insights can help insurers identify which claims to investigate further. Insurers can also deploy software that automatically flags cases that exhibit any warning signs of claim fraud that the insurer has previously identified. As in many other areas, computers can generally perform this kind of task more accurately and consistently than humans.

Another way insurers can prevent claim fraud is through cross-departmental collaboration. Here, an expert explains how the claims and underwriting departments collaborate to prevent claim fraud.

Although verifying proof of loss is routine in most cases, a claim analyst would typically conduct further investigation in some situations. Identify whether the analyst would investigate further in each situation described below.

The insured was the only one aboard a boat that sank in calm waters 100 feet from shore, and the insured's body was not recovered.

Investigate further

Approve the claim

The insured disappeared under unverifiable circumstances but the claimant submitted a court order stating that the insured is presumed dead.

Investigate further

Approve the claim

The insured died outside of the country in which the policy was issued.

Investigate further

Approve the claim

The insured died by suicide after the policy's suicide exclusion period expired.

Investigate further

Approve the claim

Use the following information to answer the questions below.

Fred Kingsley, a claim analyst, received a claim for the proceeds of a $500,000 life insurance policy. Although the claim included a claimant's statement and death certificate as proof of loss, Mr. Kingsley suspected claim fraud and initiated a claim investigation.

In this situation, claim fraud could have been committed by (choose all that apply)

The policy beneficiary

☐ The medical provider who signed the death certificate

☐ The financial professional who sold the policy

Mr. Kingsley from our previous question can protect the privacy of the insurer, the policy beneficiary, and other people involved in the claim investigation by concealing the actual purpose of the investigation from people he speaks to as part of the investigation.

True

False

Learning Objective

Describe the Model Unfair Claims Settlement Practices Act and give examples of unfair claim practices that would result in a violation of this act.

Failing to promptly acknowledge receipt of claim-related communications

Indicating the coverage under which each payment is being made

Insisting on a reasonable claim investigation before affirming or denying a claim

Attempting to settle claims based on an application that was materially altered without the policyowners' knowledge

Requiring a proof of loss form as well as subsequent verification that duplicates the original proof of loss

Can you recognize the examples of unfair claim practices? Choose all that apply.

Examples of Unfair Claim Practices

Most states have laws based on the Model Unfair Claims Settlement Practices Act from the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC).

These laws describe specific actions that are considered unfair claim practices if done (1) in conscious disregard of the law or (2) so frequently as to indicate a general business practice.

Prohibited practices include

Failing to promptly acknowledge receipt of claim-related communications

Failing to affirm or deny coverage of claims within a reasonable time after the claim investigation is completed

Attempting to settle claims based on an application that was materially altered without the policyowners' knowledge

Requiring a proof of loss form as well as subsequent verification that duplicates the original proof of loss

Compelling claimants to initiate lawsuits to recover death benefits by offering substantially less than the amounts due

Knowingly misrepresenting to claimants and insureds relevant facts or policy provisions relating to coverages at issue

Refusing to pay claims without conducting a reasonable investigation

Failing to provide forms necessary to present claims within fifteen (15) calendar days of a request with reasonable explanations regarding their use

Making Ethical Claim Decisions

Click or touch the right arrows below for an example of ethical decision making.

"Hey, John. I need to improve our department's results. I'll just keep this claimant's statement in a drawer. If another claim comes in on the same case, I'll create a file and pass it to you. Then I'll need you to deny the claim. If we get a third claimant's statement, then we'll open an investigation. No need to mention this to anyone else."

It seems that John has an ethical dilemma. Deep down John knows that his manager's order isn't the right thing to do. Potential consequences that could arise from this unethical action include

Regulatory fines

Financial professionals placing business elsewhere

Disciplinary action for the employee

Legal action against the company

Rating downgrade

Select the interaction between a claim department and a customer that would be considered an unfair claim settlement practice under the (NAIC) Model Unfair Claims Settlement Practices Act.

Claim analyst Marie Kensington hired an outside investigator to verify information related to a claim.

Claim analyst Garth Giske frequently authorizes claim settlements that are substantially less than the actual amount due.

After reviewing a claim for accidental death benefits, claim analyst Maxine Harmon determined that the claimant had misinterpreted the policy's accidental death benefit provision and denied the claim.

Trent Jeffers, an analyst trainee, unknowingly failed to complete his first claim investigation within the required amount of time after receiving proof of loss.

Learning Objective

Explain various ways that insurers improve and evaluate claim processing efficiency.

An insurer expects its claim staff to process claims quickly and thoroughly. Usually, insurers set goals for a certain number of claims to be processed within a certain time frame. However, meeting that time frame cannot be done at the expense of accuracy or quality.

The Role of Automation

Companies can use technology to automate existing tasks. Computers can perform many tasks quickly, cheaply, and with consistent quality. The best candidates are generally highly repetitive tasks with well-defined rules, such as routine, uncomplicated life insurance claims.

When computers automatically pay claims that meet certain requirements and are below a specified dollar amount, claim department staff are freed up to concentrate on investigating less routine claims for fraud or unintentional mistakes.

Claim Audits

Insurers conduct internal audits of approved and denied claims, and are also subject to external audits of claim practices.

"One way we know the claim department is running efficiently is when reinsurers audit its procedures, and its quantitative and qualitative measurements are right on target, particularly when it comes to balancing accuracy with speed."

As this graphic illustrates, an insurer can face consequences if it pays a claim too slowly or too quickly.

Companies can also evaluate claim processing efficiency by reviewing market conduct exam results. For example, imagine that our fictional company, Forthright Financial, underwent a market conduct exam. Here are some factors that states generally verify during a market conduct exam.

Forthright's Claim Practice Market Conduct Exam Results

The company:

Adheres to policies and procedures

Processes claims according to policy terms

Investigates and settles claims on time

Adequately and accurately documents files

Accurately calculates claim payments

Pays claims to the correct payees

Doesn't violate the Model Unfair Claims Settlement Practices Act

One benefit of automated claims processing is that companies can rely on computers to process complex claims.

True

False

Market conduct exams evaluate how well an insurer's claim department handles various aspects of claim processing, including (choose all that apply)

Processing claims according to policy terms

Investigating and settling claims on time

Accurately calculating claim payments

Paying claims to the correct payee

Ensuring that staff comply with the Model Unfair Claims Settlement Practices Act

Some functions primarily provide support to other functions. In this module, we’ll discuss three of the most important support functions that are essential to an insurance company’s success.

In this module, you’ll learn about

How insurers use research and data to develop their marketing mix

The process insurers use to create successful new products

How the legal and compliance functions work together and separately to keep the insurer on the right side of the law

Marketing is so much more than advertising. It’s a research-driven discipline designed to help a company get the right products to the right customers at the right place through the right distribution systems. Learn how insurers carry out the various aspects of the marketing discipline.

Identify and describe the elements of a marketing plan.

Identify and describe the four variables that make up the traditional marketing mix and the newer "Four Cs" model.

Distinguish among three promotional tools insurers use to help them convey their messages to customers.

Describe how insurers use market segmentation and target marketing to identify the customers most likely to benefit from their products.

Distinguish among three primary types of target marketing strategies.

Describe different sources that insurers use for obtaining market information.

Identify elements in the internal and external marketing environments.

Describe marketing controls.

Explain the duties of the marketing department and what other functional areas contribute to marketing activities.

Learning Objective

Identify and describe the elements of a marketing plan.

Identify and describe the four variables that make up the traditional marketing mix and the newer "Four Cs" model.

Our new business results have not been living up to projections lately. We've got to revisit our marketing strategy.

Start

Forthright targets the affluent market with individual life insurance and annuities. Because these products are complex, we rely on personal selling. Our financial professionals need to be able to explain our products clearly and simply or else the customers simply won't buy them.

Customers choose Forthright for our great service, trustworthiness, and financial strength. So, we emphasize image advertising—campaigns to enhance our overall image. And our corporate communications department makes sure that the press coverage of Forthright is as positive as possible.

Another key here is in a product's financial design. Some customers want a fast accumulation of cash value. Others want to be able to withdraw their money from the start with no penalty. Customers want options. And if we add high fees and charges to our products, customers are going to get turned off. These factors are all part of marketing here at Forthright.

Wait....What does all that have to do with marketing?

You might think marketing is just advertising, but there's more to it than that. You might think marketing is just selling, but it's more than just that, too. Here's a definition.

"Marketing is the activity, set of institutions, and processes for creating, communicating, delivering, and exchanging offerings that have value for customers, clients, partners, and society at large."

American Marketing Association, 2007.

Let's look more closely at the elements of marketing.

The Marketing Plan

First, the marketing department must develop a marketing plan, which is a written document that states the marketing goals for a product or product line. Marketing plans vary among companies, but typically contain

An executive summary, or a brief highlight of the purpose, recommendations, costs, and intended results

A situation analysis, or an explanation of the environmental factors affecting marketing operations

Marketing objectives

Marketing strategies

Tactical/action programs, or a description of the activities to be carried out

Budgets

The evaluation methodology, or the controls the company will use to analyze progress toward goals

The marketing plan should be communicated to everyone involved in carrying out the marketing strategy.

The Marketing Mix

To market effectively, marketing departments need to understand the details of

Product

Price

Promotion

Distribution

These four variables work together to attract customers, trigger buying, and help companies meet business goals.

Product

Let's start with product, which refers to the goods, services, or ideas that a seller offers to customers to satisfy a need. An individual company's objective is to provide products that meet the needs of its customers. Thus, the company markets a variety of products geared toward satisfying its customers' needs.

By now, you probably know the products the insurance industry offers: life insurance, annuities, retirement products, medical coverage, and disability insurance. Some companies (especially big ones) offer all of these products in several different flavors. But not all companies offer all of these. Some specialize in a few products, or even just one.

Here are some of the traditional life and health insurance product types a company might specialize in.

A company's product mix—the assortment of products a company offers—plays a big role in its marketing profile and its success. Product development, which we discuss in the next lesson, is a huge factor in determining the company's product offerings.

Product development is guided by the company's product strategy, which is formulated at the highest levels.

Financial Design

Now let's look at the next variable.

Although sometimes referred to as “price,” for insurance products, it's more useful to talk about financial design—which encompasses all the financial features of a product.

Which of these do you think are potential features of insurance product financial design? Choose all that apply.

Required premium payments

Mortality charges

Charges for features, benefits, guarantees, etc.

Fees for services, transactions, etc.

Charges (if any) for early surrender or withdrawal

How fast the cash value accumulates

Investment or interest rate minimum guarantees

Guaranteed income, accumulation, or withdrawal benefits

Different customers have different needs. Insurers can meet those needs with different financial designs, but, whether they are buying cookies, insurance, or an annuity, customers want to feel

They are getting the best value available

If they are paying more—such as a higher premium or extra charges for additional features—then what they are getting is worth the extra money

We'll learn more about financial design elsewhere in the course. For now, let's move on to the next variable.

Distribution

Let's think about cookies. You can buy them in several places: the supermarket, the bakery, or a café, for example. As a cookie manufacturer, you can choose the "place" where you will sell your cookies.

For insurance, we use the term "distribution" more often than "place," because the means of distributing insurance products does not necessarily correspond with a specific type of place, as it does with many products.

As you learned earlier in the course, some of the distribution methods insurers use that involve face-to-face meetings with the client include

Career agents

PPGAs

Independent agents

Broker/dealers

Registered investment advisors (RIAs)

Banks and other depository institutions

Other insurance companies

Employee benefits brokers

Insurers also use direct-to-consumer distribution methods, such as

Email

Company website

Phone

Mail

An insurer's distribution choices are an important part of its marketing mix.

Promotion

Promotion refers to the collection of activities that companies use to make customers aware of their offerings and to influence customers to purchase, and distributors to sell, a product. Insurers typically use three types of promotion.

First, personal selling involves conversation between a sales agent and one or more prospective customers, either face to face or over the phone. Insurers use personal selling extensively, because it is an effective way to convey information about a complex product. Why?

The customer can ask questions to clarify information

The financial professional can customize the message to fit the situation

Here's another type of promotion: advertising. I think we all know advertising when we see it, but could you define it?

Advertising is any paid form of nonpersonal communication or promotion about a company or its products or services that an identified sponsor generates and transmits through any type of media.

Insurers typically use advertising media such as television, radio, direct mail, the internet, social media, magazines, newspapers, billboards, and posters. Advertising media can reach a high number of people but in many cases is not customized for individuals. The internet can allow for some degree of customization, however (for example, based on previous purchases or demographic profile).

Institutional Advertising

One way insurers reach prospective customers is through institutional advertising, also called image advertising, which seeks to promote a general image of the company rather than to explain a specific product. For example, insurers may use institutional advertising to try to convey an image of strength, trust, reliability, longevity, or stability.

Product Advertising

Insurers also use product advertising to promote a specific product. Complex insurance products can be difficult to explain adequately in an advertisement. Product advertising can be aimed at financial professionals or the end customer.

Let's look at our third type of promotion. Publicity is a form of nonpaid communication about a person, place, thing, or cause. For example, companies often provide news releases to the media. Publicity is economical (free, that is!), but the insurer has limited control over precisely what information the media chooses to transmit and in what way.

Another Marketing Mix Model: The Four Cs

Experts have long used the variables of product, price, promotion, and distribution to classify marketing activities. However, many marketers recently have started to use a new model called the “Four Cs” to describe the marketing mix. These marketers consider the Four Cs model to be more applicable to service industries and more customer centric. The Four Cs are (click or touch the right arrows to see them all)

Customer. The insurer analyzes who its customers are, what their needs are, and what products it can provide to meet those needs.

Marketing Mix Objectives

In all cases, the elements of the marketing mix need to (click or touch each heading to see more):

Fit together so that each one supports the others

The type of product a company offers determines the type of distribution methods the insurer uses. The type of distribution an insurer chooses for a product, in turn, helps determine the product's price.

Align with the company's overall strategic goals

If a company's strategic goal is to be an industry leader in sales of term life insurance, the marketing mix should include specific strategies for customer, cost, convenience, and communication that will allow the company to sell term life insurance policies to customers more successfully than its competitors can.

Support the company's positioning strategy

Through positioning, an insurer attempts to distinguish itself from other insurers in the marketplace by building a company image or product image that contrasts with the images competitors offer. An insurer may position itself on the basis of company or product attributes, types of products offered, price and quality of products, markets serviced, or distribution characteristics.

Support the company's branding strategy

Companies develop branding strategies to distinguish themselves among competitors. A company's brand—such as its name, slogan, and visual elements like a logo or color scheme—can draw customers to that company. To develop brand loyalty among customers, insurers need to create positive reactions to the brand by delivering positive customer experiences.

Insurers often substitute the term "financial design" for the marketing mix variable commonly known as

Product

Price

Place

Promotion

"Distribution" is a term commonly used in insurance for the marketing mix variable known as

Product

Price

Place

Promotion

Select the type of promotion that matches each description.

A form of nonpaid communication about a person, place, thing, or cause.

Advertising

Publicity

Personal selling

Nonpersonal communication or promotion about a company or its products or services that an identified sponsor generates and transmits through any type of media.

Advertising

Publicity

Personal selling

Conversation between a financial professional and one or more prospective customers.

Advertising

Publicity

Personal selling

True or false?

Institutional advertising is intended to present the details of a specific product.

True

False

When an insurer ensures that customers can learn about and purchase its products in the ways that best meet their needs and preferences, which of the Four Cs is the insurer addressing?

Customer

Cost

Convenience

Communication

When a company uses the marketing mix to create a unique market identity in the customer's mind, the company is engaging in

Segmentation

Positioning

Mixing

Learning Objective

Describe how insurers use market segmentation and target marketing to identify the customers most likely to benefit from their products.

Distinguish among three primary types of target marketing strategies.

Knowing about the marketing mix is a good start, but there's a lot more to marketing. For example, how does a company choose its customer base?

Companies target individuals who have needs the company can feasibly meet. They consider how turning these people into customers can contribute to the company's earnings, growth, and overall financial strength.

Market Segmentation

We know we can't meet the needs of every customer in a profitable way—no financial services company can—so we need to decide who our target customers are. First, we divide the total market into a series of smaller submarkets with unique sets of needs. This process is called market segmentation.

A simple form of market segmentation is to divide the total market for insurance products into two categories:

The consumer market

The organizational market

I can see how that would narrow things down some, but isn't a segment made up of "individuals" still a little too big?

Below are two possible market segments for a new life insurance product.

Segment A: Women between the ages of 35 and 45.

Segment B: Divorced, single mothers between 35 and 45 years old who earn between $50,000 and $75,000 a year.

Which of these segments includes more potential customers?

Segment A

Segment B

Now, which of the segments described above provides more detailed information to use in developing an effective, meaningful marketing mix?

Segment A

Segment B

Target Marketing

Evaluating and selecting specific market segments to determine which are the most attractive is called target marketing. Companies usually select a target market based on such factors as

Size

Growth potential

Distribution and service costs

Current and expected competition

Fit with the company's overall objectives and position

The number of market segments a company chooses depends on the target marketing strategy the company uses. Three options are undifferentiated marketing, concentrated marketing, and differentiated marketing.

Do you think you can recognize each approach from its name?

An insurer offers only mortgage protection life insurance policies for new homeowners.

Which approach is this insurer using?

Undifferentiated marketing

Concentrated marketing

Differentiated marketing

Here is an illustration of concentrated marketing.

An insurer offers traditional whole life, universal life, and term life products to different segments, using a different marketing mix for each product.

Which approach is this insurer using?

Undifferentiated marketing

Concentrated marketing

Differentiated marketing

Here is an illustration of differentiated marketing.

An insurer markets its small face amount term life insurance product to all potential life insurance customers, without distinguishing among segments of that market. It uses the same distribution channels, pricing structures, and promotions for all customers.

Which approach is this insurer using?

Undifferentiated marketing

Concentrated marketing

Differentiated marketing

Here is an illustration of undifferentiated marketing.

Below are characteristics of different target marketing strategies. Select each characteristic you think is a benefit of the strategy. You can choose more than one answer.

Following an undifferentiated marketing strategy allows insurers to (choose all that apply)

Realize cost savings

Lower product prices

Better satisfy customers

Following a concentrated marketing strategy allows insurers to (choose all that apply)

Compete effectively with limited resources

Reduce the company's product risk

Develop expertise in a single market segment

Following a differentiated marketing strategy allows insurers to

Realize substantial cost savings from the creation of multiple products

Diversify product risks

Better satisfy the needs of customers

EndiFirst Insurance needs to limit marketing costs for its new product, but it believes the product should appeal to many different types of customers. EndiFirst should consider using a(n)

Undifferentiated marketing strategy

Concentrated marketing strategy

Differentiated marketing strategy

Raylite is trying to market its simplified issue product to millennial new parents. At the same time, Raylite wants to market its long-term care insurance to long-time customers who have existing life insurance policies. Raylite should use a(n)

Undifferentiated marketing strategy

Concentrated marketing strategy

Differentiated marketing strategy

Learning Objective

Describe different sources that insurers use for obtaining market information.

Identify elements in the internal and external marketing environments.

So, how does a company decide what market segments it will focus on?

To help it make this type of decision, a company collects information about several things: the market segment, itself, its competitors, the economy, regulations, and customers. Information about all of these is at the heart of making effective marketing decisions.

Let's take a look at the sources of this information.

Sources of Marketing Information

Click or touch each heading below to learn about sources of marketing information.

Internal Databases

Internal databases contain internal records and reports from throughout the company. These reports provide detailed information about a company's sales, expenses, profitability, products, promotions, distribution, customers, and markets.

Website and Social Media Analysis

Insurers can gain important insight into customer needs, interests, and attitudes by analyzing data about customer behavior on the company's website and on social media. Insurers may collect clickstream data to gain insight into how to best meet customer product needs or improve the customer experience. Many companies also pay attention to what customers and others say about the company or its products on social media, blogs, and online forums.

Competitive and Market Intelligence

Insurers need information, often called competitive intelligence, about their competitors. Specifically, insurers want to know about their competitors' marketing mixes, target markets, service quality, market shares, marketing strategies, and product mixes.

In addition, insurers need information about ongoing developments in the marketing environment, often called market intelligence. Specifically, insurers want to identify issues that might impact a market's potential. Insurers obtain most of their competitive and market information from publicly available sources, like company websites or financial services publications.

Marketing Research

Through marketing research, an insurer or independent company collects, interprets, and reports information related to specific marketing problems or opportunities. It provides information about customers, products, and distribution channels on an as-needed or one-time basis.

The Marketing Environment

Insurers analyze marketing information within the context of the company's current marketing environment. The marketing environment consists of all of the elements in the company's internal and external environments that affect the company's ability to carry out its marketing activities.

A company's internal environment consists of elements within the company's control, such as financial, physical, technological, and human resources; organizational structure; and the marketing mix.

A company's external environment consists of elements outside the company's control, such as economic, competitive, regulatory, taxation, and social factors.

Examples of the types of information Forthright uses for marketing are listed below. Read each example and determine which answer choice is the most likely source of each piece of information.

Customers in Forthright's market have responded to surveys indicating that adding guarantees to variable annuities makes them more attractive than other investment products.

Internal database

Marketing research

Competitive and marketing intelligence

Forthright has maintained a 5% average profit margin on its annuity products over the past 5 years.

Internal database

Marketing research

Competitive and marketing intelligence

None of Forthright's competitors offers the same terms on its living benefit guarantees as Forthright's new product offers.

Internal database

Marketing research

Competitive and marketing intelligence

Learning Objective

Explain the duties of the marketing department and what other functional areas contribute to marketing activities.

Describe marketing controls.

One last thing: Sometimes I see Damon from the Actuarial Department meeting with Beth from Marketing. What are they talking about?

The Marketing department handles some of the work we have discussed, exclusively or at least primarily:

Advertising

Obtaining marketing information

Segmenting markets

Target marketing

However, the Marketing department also makes valuable contributions to other activities we've mentioned. Click or touch each heading below for more information about these activities.
