Developing Strategy

Executive management gets input from Marketing when developing the overall marketing strategy.

Product Development

Actuaries and other departments get input from Marketing when developing products.

Product Financial Design

Actuaries get input from Marketing when working on a product's financial design.

Distribution

Departments handling sales and distribution typically have close ties with Marketing.

Corporate Communications

The corporate communications department generally handles news releases, but they may collaborate with Marketing to ensure a cohesive brand.

Marketing Controls

Marketing managers need a way to measure whether they are achieving the goals set out in the marketing plan. For example, did sales of indexed annuities increase as a result of an advertisement targeted to high net worth individuals?

Companies typically state performance standards in the marketing plan goals, and gauge whether they achieve their goals by comparing actual performance with the standard. Here are some tools insurers use to measure marketing performance. Use the arrows to navigate.

Sales Analysis

Using sales analysis, a company examines its sales numbers to evaluate current performance. Current actual sales are often compared to forecasted sales, sales in previous years, competitors' sales, or other performance standards.

If a performance does not match the standard, the insurer investigates to determine the reasons for the discrepancy, and adjusts accordingly. Examples of adjustments that may need to be made include

Changing tactical/action programs or implementation strategies

Developing more precise methods of collecting or analyzing performance data if the current way does not reflect true performance

Reviewing performance standards to ensure that they are realistic

Developing new products, new marketing strategies, or new marketing goals

Select the functions for which each area has primary responsibility. More than one response may be correct.

Marketing area

Market research

Advertising

Product financial design

News releases

Corporate communications area

Market research

Advertising

Product financial design

News releases

Actuarial area

Market research

Advertising

Product financial design

News releases

The process of determining which costs are associated with particular activities to help managers decide if a cost is worth the value the activity provides is called

Sales analysis

Expense analysis

Profitability analysis

Examples of possible adjustments that may need to be made if marketing goals are not met include (choose all that apply)

Changing tactical/action programs or implementation strategies

Developing less precise measures of collecting or analyzing performance data

Reviewing performance standards to ensure that they are realistic

Developing new products, new marketing strategies, or new marketing goals

Examples of possible adjustments that may need to be made if marketing goals are not met include (choose all that apply)

Some functions primarily provide support to other functions. In this module, we’ll discuss three of the most important support functions that are essential to an insurance company’s success.

In this module, you’ll learn about

How insurers use research and data to develop their marketing mix

The process insurers use to create successful new products

How the legal and compliance functions work together and separately to keep the insurer on the right side of the law

To survive and thrive, an insurer needs an ever-evolving portfolio of products that’s responsive to the changing environment and to customers’ changing needs. But insurance products are complex, expensive to develop, and subject to a litany of regulations designed to protect customers. Learn about the process insurers go through to create products that will succeed in the marketplace.

Explain the importance of product development to an insurance company's success.

Describe the overall product development process.

Explain how insurance companies generate and screen ideas for new products.

Describe the five elements of a comprehensive business analysis, and identify the responsibilities of an insurer's functional areas in this process.

Explain the technical product design stage of product development.

Describe Day 1 and Day 2 functionalities that must be put into place during product implementation.

Describe how insurers use transaction processing systems, expert systems, and other information systems.

Describe how insurers monitor, evaluate, and use feedback to improve both their product offerings and their product development process.

Learning Objective

Explain the importance of product development to an insurance company's success.

Product development is a pretty straightforward concept: it's a company's process for creating new products to sell. A company needs to sell products to stay in business, after all.

But why is product development so complicated for insurers? Why do they work so hard to establish the right process to develop new products? And why do so many companies have teams that do nothing but develop products, and yet even more employees who help with the process?

A flawed product development process has real consequences. A company may take a product to market quickly, but maybe it sells poorly, or something else goes wrong. Sometimes a product can even sell exceptionally well, but cause a loss for the company, thanks to a problem with the financial design.

Developing a new product is risky, so companies carefully weigh the risk versus the reward. The effort must justify the time and expense involved. As you'll see, insurers consider many factors before introducing new products.

Consider the following scenario.

This new product from Gigantic could be great. I like Forthright's products better overall, but they seem slow lately with product development.

Start

Why can't Forthright just push out new products more quickly? There are several reasons. A good product has to satisfy a number of objectives, which may pull the project in different directions.

Let's look at some factors that Forthright would consider before introducing a new product.

Characteristics of an Effective Product Development Process

Product development affects revenue. The majority of an insurer's revenues come from product sales. New products are essential to product sales because old products become less and less attractive and eventually become obsolete.

Also, the product development process needs to be efficient. "Speed to market"—how quickly a new product can be developed and offered for sale—is an important factor in the competition with other companies for revenues.

Product development involves many expenses.

Large numbers of personnel are involved in product development.

New product ideas must be meticulously researched.

Systems and processes may need to be re-tooled to handle a new product.

Compensation for financial professionals is a large cost element.

Companies must commit reserves and capital to support the new product.

Many new products continue to be improved after they are introduced to the market.

So, an effective product development process helps the insurer manage the cost of developing new products and also helps ensure that resources are not wasted on unsuccessful products.

Like any commitment of resources, developing a new product is an investment, so it involves risk. One risk is that the new product might not achieve adequate sales volume. Alternatively, the product might sell well, but not earn an adequate profit, or any profit, or it might lose money.

As always, the higher the potential return, the greater the associated risk.

An effective product development process helps the insurer achieve its desired risk profile.

Select each statement that correctly describes why an effective product development process is essential to an insurer's financial success.

Profitable new products are essential for an insurer’s revenue prospects.

New products often involve considerable cost to the insurer.

Releasing a new product is risky.

section: The Product Development Process and Product Planning
text:
Learning Objectives

Describe the overall product development process.

Explain how insurance companies generate and screen ideas for new products.

A Society of Actuaries report found that most insurance companies have a dedicated product development team. Most often, at large and medium-sized insurance companies, product development even has its own department. Source

All product development teams rely on expertise from across the organization, but the number and order of the steps can vary. It's important for a company to implement the steps that best work for that organization. Let's see how these steps in a typical product development process work together to produce the desired results.

Overview of the Product Development Process

Product development goes through a typical process cycle. Starting with generation of new product ideas, the cycle continues through analysis of promising ideas, design of products that are selected from among those promising ideas, through product launch, and then through a feedback and adjustment process which leads back to new ideas.

The product development process at most companies can be broken down into these five steps.

Use the right arrrows below to learn more about each step in the process.

Product Planning

The product development team gathers ideas for product concepts that seem to fill a customer need and does initial screening and testing on those ideas.

Product Planning

Insurers continuously search for ideas for new product concepts that will meet the needs of the company and its target markets. This is the first step in the product development process: product planning.

The first step in product planning is idea generation. For example, insurers monitor which of their own products—and which products offered by competitors—are successful. They also conduct market research.

Developing a "new" product encompasses a range of projects, including a simple rate change to modify an existing product, a product revision, and a full-blown new product. Each of these projects requires new regulatory filings—even modifications to existing products that had already undergone filing.

Rate change—Changing a fee or charge on an existing product

Product revision—Any change that lies between a rate change and a new product, such as adding a rider to an existing policy

New product—Developing a significant new product feature or function and a unique product identifier

Sources of New Product Ideas

You might ask why we would develop a completely new product if we already have a product we can modify. Wouldn't modifying an existing product be cheaper and quicker?

Customer needs can change as the environment changes. We need to do whatever we can, within reason, to make sure our products meet those needs. We get a lot of new product ideas from changes to the law, new regulations, and changes to the tax code. We can even get ideas from watching what our competitors are doing—and whether or not it's working for them.

Generating Ideas

The actual process of generating ideas involves people from throughout the company, not just the team that will eventually design the project.

Research teams investigate the market's shifting needs and competitors' products.

Actuaries may discover an opportunity in the course of assessing probabilities.

The sales department and top financial professionals give feedback on what customers want or don't want.

Marketing teams propose ideas that emphasize the strengths that the company is known for.

Ideas for new products or enhancements and modifications to existing products can come from a variety of sources. Of the following possible sources of new product ideas, which four sources do you think provide companies with the most new product ideas?

Customers

Financial professionals

Product development team

Actuaries

Marketing research

Marketing intelligence / Competitors' products

Policy services department

Agency / Sales department

Consultants

Marketing specialists

Idea Screening

Once a pool of new product ideas is generated, each idea must be screened to identify which ones to study further.

Of course, idea screening helps the product development team to select promising ideas, but it also helps to conserve resources by eliminating ideas with poor potential.

Select each factor that Forthright would use to screen ideas. Choose all that apply.

Marketability to target markets

Compatibility with the company’s goals and market position

Potential profitability

Desirability and understandability to target customers

Compatibility with the company’s distribution channels

Compatibility with the company’s information systems

Concept Testing

Some companies screen ideas through concept testing, which consists of describing product ideas to financial professionals or potential purchasers and seeing which ideas have the greatest appeal.

Some companies test product concepts using

Focus groups—small group interviews in which participants discuss their opinions

Large-scale quantitative research studies, which use structured data-collection methods and carefully defined samples

True or False?

A change in a fee or charge on an existing product qualifies as a "new" product.

True

False

Marketing research is the primary source for new insurance product ideas.

True

False

Concept testing using __________ involves a formal collection of structured data from large, carefully defined samples.

Quantitative research

Focus groups

Idea screening

Learning Objective

Describe the five elements of a comprehensive business analysis and identify the responsibilities of an insurer's functional areas in this process.

Each product idea that survives the initial screening process undergoes a comprehensive business analysis to determine the product's feasibility and marketability.

Comprehensive business analysis

Creates a foundation for the later stage of technical product design

Builds a case for company decision makers that the proposed product is worth developing

Elements of Comprehensive Business Analysis

Click or touch each heading below for information about comprehensive business analysis.

Market Analysis

A market analysis is an evaluation of all factors that might affect product sales. The market analysis attempts to demonstrate that market demand exists to support a proposed product.

Product Design Objectives

Product design objectives are the product's basic characteristics, benefits, fees and charges, any limitations on issuing policies, and operational and administrative requirements such as underwriting guidelines. These objectives guide technical product design later.

Feasibility Study

A feasibility study evaluates the operational and technical viability of producing the product and the impact of the new product on the company's existing products.

Marketing Plan

As described in the previous lesson, a marketing plan provides a detailed description of the marketing goals for a product or product line, and describes the strategies and the implementation and control efforts the company intends to use to achieve those goals.

Marketing Projection

A marketing projection is an estimate of future sales, including sales volume (number of units sold), sales revenue (dollar volume of sales), expenses, and profits. Early estimates are rough but become more refined as the process progresses.

Product Proposal

A product proposal is a document presented to the appropriate executives—for example, the risk committee—for a go/no go decision on the idea. It gathers and condenses information from the previous documents.

The product proposal contains enough information for the senior executives to make an informed decision about whether to go ahead with the project. When working on the remaining steps in the product development process, teams use the product proposal as a guide. A product proposal describes the following aspects of a product:

The product's purpose

Key product features

The target market for the product and projected sales volume

Differentiation from other products

Plans for promotion, advertising, distribution, and sales

Requirements for administrative resources, including technology

Probability projections and capital needs such as investment changes

Regulatory considerations

Here are just a few of the tasks that need to be completed during comprehensive business analysis. Read each task and determine what area would be responsible for that task.

Reviews the proposed product to ensure that it complies with all legal and regulatory requirements

Assesses whether the company's current systems can accommodate the new product

Comprehensive Business Analysis Responsibilities

As we said, those were just a few of the tasks to be completed. Here is a more complete list.

Marketing

Conducts a market analysis to determine whether customers want the new product

Prepares a marketing plan and marketing projections

Gathers and analyzes information about potential target markets and competitors' products

Evaluates how the new product might affect in-force business and sales of the insurer's current products

Determines the most appropriate distribution channels and advertising and sales strategies

Works with the compliance area to determine the best test markets in terms of legal requirements and prohibitions

Agency Operations

Determines if changes are necessary for the current sales force to sell the new product effectively

Helps determine the proper training materials for financial professionals

Must be very responsive to the communications that the marketing department sends out for both test phase and ultimate launch

Actuarial

Develops product design objectives with advice from other functional areas, including marketing, underwriting, and compliance

Performs preliminary calculations to determine if the new product can be priced to be competitive and profitable within a reasonable time frame

Underwriting

With advice from actuarial, establishes initial underwriting guidelines

Investments

With advice from actuarial, determines what types of investments are needed to support the expected payment of benefits under the new product and also to add to the company's profits

Claim Administration

Examines claim assumptions made by actuarial staff in product design

Determines whether current claim systems and staff can adequately administer new product

Customer Service

Considers whether current staff and procedures are adequate to support new product

Estimates any additional staffing costs for new product

Information Technology

Assesses whether the current information systems can support the new product

Estimates any costs necessary to upgrade or outsource systems

Accounting

Reviews financial reporting requirements that the insurer must meet in developing and selling the new product

Evaluates how the new business will be reflected in the company's financial statements

Legal/Compliance

Assists in developing the product design objectives

Reviews the product to ensure that it complies with all legal and regulatory requirements in the jurisdictions in which it will be sold

Determines policy filing requirements and whether the product will be prohibited in any jurisdictions

Advises marketing during the development of product advertisements

Learning Objective

Explain the technical product design stage of product development.

After the risk committee or other executive group approves a proposal for a new insurance product, the product development team moves on to technical product design.

In this stage, the product development team uses the more general product proposal as the basis for a highly detailed product design. Two primary elements included in the technical product design are the product's financial structure and the contract forms for the product.

The technical product design phase typically involves a three-part process:

Establishing a financial design

Testing a financial design

Budgeting and scheduling

Establishing a Financial Design

The process of establishing a financial design is extremely technical. Actuaries—highly trained experts in the mathematics of insurance—create the financial design of a new insurance or annuity product by answering questions involving the product's

Premium rates

Account or transaction fees and charges

Surrender charge schedules

Cash value growth schedules or formulas

Underwriting standards, tables, and risk classes

Reinsurance strategy and retention limit

The goals of financial design are

Ensuring that the company will be able to cover future benefits and expenses, even in crisis conditions

Creating a package of benefits and costs that will have marketplace appeal for purchasers and financial professionals

Providing an adequate return to the company’s owners

Testing a Financial Design

How do actuaries test proposed financial designs? Primarily by using financial models: computer programs that describe the product mathematically. A model shows how the financial values below interact over a long future period.

Financial models show

Cash inflows: premiums, deposits, charges, fees, investment income, etc.

Cash outflows: benefits, surrenders, expenses, etc.

Profitability

Models also "stress test" the product's financial design, which means testing product performance in extremely unfavorable conditions.

Select each negative condition that can impact an insurance or annuity product.

Budgeting and Scheduling

Once the technical design team has figured out the what of the product design, it's time to figure out the how and when.

The budgeting portion of this phase involves preparing a budget for the human, financial, and computer resources needed to implement the product.

The scheduling portion of this phase traditionally involves developing a project schedule. However, an insurer may use forms of project management, such as agile product methodology, that do not follow a fixed schedule in great detail.

In general, the goals of project scheduling are to identify which tasks need to be completed sequentially and which may be completed concurrently, and to assign team members to tasks accordingly.

Learning Objectives

Describe Day 1 and Day 2 functionalities that must be put into place during product implementation.

Describe how insurers use transaction processing systems, expert systems, and other information systems.

Once you have a product, you still need to get it to the customer. Introducing the product—or placing it on the market—requires a lot of care. You have to make the internal changes necessary to support it properly.

Most insurers divide product introduction into two parts.

One part involves product implementation, which includes putting all the necessary administrative structures and processes in place.

Another part is the product launch, when the company finally makes the product available to customers.

"We start some product implementation tasks while the technical product design stage is still in progress. We want to make sure the support structure is already in place when the product is ready to go to market."

Day 1 and Day 2 Functionality

Insurers want to get the product into customers' hands as soon as possible. After all, time is money! Insurers can speed up the implementation process by determining the new product's

Day 1 functionality—the "gotta haves"; the elements a company needs to have in place and functioning when the first contract is sold

Day 2 functionality—the elements that can be delayed until after Day 1

Introducing the product in states where product approval was delayed

We can talk about which activities would take place on Day 1 or 2 for "most companies," but each company must decide for itself which activities to delay until Day 2.

The more activities the company delays until Day 2…

...the more quickly the company can get the product to market

BUT

...the more risk the company takes that something will go wrong.

First, we'll take a closer look at several critical activities that must be completed by Day 1.

Policy Filing

U.S. regulations require insurers to file new product contract forms and any other legally required documents with regulatory agencies in each state in which the insurer will be offering the products. This process is called policy filing.

During policy filing, state insurance regulators review the contract form. Among other things, they check that it includes all the necessary information identifying the insurer offering the product. They make sure it includes all the policy provisions required in that state and that it does not include any provisions prohibited in that state.

They also ensure that the policy satisfies current readability requirements. These requirements ensure that the policy can be understood by people who are not legal experts, and are concerned with sentence length, word length, and the amount of technical and legal language included in the contract.

SEC Registration

U.S. insurers also need to file a registration statement for variable products with the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC).

Although the issuer must register a security with the SEC, the SEC does not approve the product or judge its merit.

Disclosure Documents

Disclosure is another essential Day 1 requirement. Insurers are required to provide customers with several disclosure documents. In the U.S., insurers need to disclose their policies and procedures for gathering, storing, using, and sharing personally identifiable financial and health information

One disclosure document is intended for customers: the buyer's guide. An insurer must provide a buyer's guide provided to all potential insurance purchasers. The buyer's guide has general information about life insurance and the different types and costs of insurance.

Another disclosure document that must be provided to all potential insurance purchasers contains specific information about an insurance product the customer is considering. This product-specific document is called a policy summary

A third disclosure document, which the insurer must provide to potential variable insurance and variable annuity purchasers, is the prospectus. The prospectus contains specific information about a security being offered for sale.

Information Systems

Bringing necessary systems on line is a critical Day 1 activity. The IT staff frequently needs to make changes to the company's existing systems to support the new product. Sometimes, they have to develop or purchase new systems.

With the systems changes, the operational areas frequently must modify the processes and procedures that employees follow. These process changes have to be identified and documented, so the operational staff can be trained properly.

It can take months to get everything done, so systems activities need to be started as early as possible in the development schedule.

Example of Potential Systems Changes

If a new product includes five riders, and all the insurer's other products have only three available riders, then the policy administration system needs to be modified to accept additional riders. The insurer's website and other systems may also need changes.

As the example implies, the introduction of a new product almost certainly necessitates changes to the insurer's admin system. A policy administration system—often simply referred to as the admin system—is used to record information about the insurer's customers and the products they own and to record transactions related to those products. An admin system is an example of a transaction processing system, which performs high-volume, routine, and repetitive business transactions, including

Application processing

Policy issue and administration

Premium billing

Claim administration

Salary and commission payment

An insurer may use multiple admin systems, each supporting a different area (underwriting, claims) or a different type of product. Insurers sometimes use admin systems purchased from one of the several IT vendors who service the industry or they might develop their own systems internally.

In addition to the admin system, here are other common types of systems that may need to be updated to reflect a new product.

Common Systems for Day-to-Day Tasks

Click or touch in the boxes below to learn more.

Document Management Systems

Document management systems store, organize, and retrieve documents that have been (1) created electronically and converted to digital images by computer or (2) created on paper and converted to digital images through imaging, or scanning.

Workflow Management Systems

Workflow management systems automatically route work items, such as documents, to staff members for processing, placing the items in a work queue so that the jobs are available when staff members are ready to work on them.

Common Systems to Aid Decision Making

Click or touch in the boxes below to learn more.

Business Intelligence Systems

A business intelligence system analyzes data and information captured and processed by transaction processing systems and produces reports to help management and employees evaluate business situations, make decisions, and solve problems.

One form of business intelligence is business analytics, which includes a variety of technologies, applications, and processes as well as statistical and quantitative analysis. Business analytics provide detailed information such as product sales patterns to help management make decisions or solve problems.

Expert Systems

An expert system stores knowledge in the form of rules and applies those rules to make inferences and decisions similar to the way a human expert does. Business intelligence helps people make decisions, but expert systems suggest decisions themselves. Expert systems can be used for

Life insurance underwriting

Claim administration

Detection of claim fraud

Training for Employees and Financial Professionals

Another critical activity in new product implementation is training.

Working with the training area, the product development team makes sure that employees and financial professionals receive the training they need to sell and administer the new product. They also create the training materials that are needed.

Employees and sales staff need to know about the product's features and the legal and regulatory requirements that affect the product.

Issue instructions show the requirements various functional areas need to follow when selling or administering the product

Training sessions instruct home office employees on product features, new administrative procedures, and new information management systems and processes

Training seminars and classes show financial professionals how to sell the product

Promotional Materials

Last but not least in product implementation: promotion and sales support materials. The insurer

Plans, creates, and arranges to run its promotional campaign

Designs, creates, and distributes materials for sales and support staff to use in selling the product, including product information, sample sales presentations, and introductory sales kits

The legal department reviews all advertisements and sales support materials. Most states also require insurers to file these materials with the appropriate regulatory body.

Product Launch

Finally, when the insurer has completed at least the Day 1 implementation activities, the product is ready to launch. In addition to actually making the product available for sale, product launch involves "going live" with all the preparations that were made during product implementation.

Examples:

IT and business process changes are put into operation

Advertising and sales support materials are distributed

Press releases and promotional materials are sent to media contacts

But who do you think the insurer most wants to get excited about the product?

That's right: financial professionals. The insurer works hard to catch financial professionals' attention, teach them about the new product, and generate enthusiasm in order to encourage them to recommend the product to their clients.

Learning Objective

Describe how insurers monitor, evaluate, and use feedback to improve both their product offerings and their product development process.

Even if a product is "Day 1 complete" and "Day 2 complete," that doesn't mean the product development team's job is complete.

After introducing a new product, the company needs to monitor whether it performs as expected, and if not, why not.

Remember the control cycle we talk about numerous times in this course? It applies to product performance, too.

Product Evaluation

Here are a number of performance indicators insurers are likely to evaluate. Click or touch each heading below to learn more.

Financial Performance

Insurers evaluate a product's financial performance, including

The value of contracts sold

Product profitability

Mortality and morbidity rates

Performance of Distributors

Insurers also evaluate the performance of distributors, including

Number of contracts sold

Number of new customers

Sales through various distribution channels

Persistency

Marketplace Reaction

Insurers evaluate the marketplace reaction of customers and competitors, including

Demographics of purchasers

Surrender or withdrawal rates

Whether the new product augments or replaces existing products

Can we do anything about a product's poor performance? Or do we just have to treat it as a learning experience and try harder next time?

Start

The approach we use to save a product depends on what we think the problem is. Use the right arrows below to learn more.

If we think the product is the problem, we can redesign the product's features or revise the product's pricing guidelines.

If a product can't be fixed—that is, if its failure stems from factors outside our control—we may need to eliminate it.

Poor initial sales results don't always mean trouble. New product sales usually grow slowly because (1) customers need time to become familiar with the product; and (2) financial professionals need time to understand the product and feel comfortable selling it, especially if it's different or more complex than the insurer's other products.

When a product is taken off the market, the policies already sold can remain in force for a long time. And unless we sell those policies to another insurer, we're obligated to provide service and benefits to those customers.

Product Development Process Evaluation

The control cycle can be used for another purpose:

To identify strengths and weaknesses of the product development process.

Knowing what works—and what doesn't—helps:

Improve the success rate of future product development projects;

Reduce product development costs;

Improve new product performance; and

Shorten the time needed to bring new products to market.

Some functions primarily provide support to other functions. In this module, we’ll discuss three of the most important support functions that are essential to an insurance company’s success.

In this module, you’ll learn about

How insurers use research and data to develop their marketing mix

The process insurers use to create successful new products

How the legal and compliance functions work together and separately to keep the insurer on the right side of the law
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

text: An insurer must comply with an increasing number of laws and regulations in carrying out its business. In addition, insurers need advice and representation related to legal disputes, corporate transactions, and other issues. Learn how legal and compliance carry out their related, but separate responsibilities.
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

text:
Distinguish between the legal and compliance functions, and describe the roles and responsibilities of each area.

Describe the primary responsibilities of the legal department.

Describe the primary responsibilities of the compliance department.

Explain the difference between solvency laws and market conduct laws, and explain several market conduct laws that insurers must comply with.

Describe the purpose of a market conduct examination and how the compliance department is involved in market conduct examinations.

Distinguish between two types of market conduct examinations in the United States.
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

text:
Learning Objective

Distinguish between the legal and compliance functions, and describe the roles and responsibilities of each area.

You probably noticed that the marketing and product development lessons contained a lot of talk about regulations:

Product approval

Required disclosure forms

Ads approval

But how do insurers know if they're following the rules and doing things the right way? Legal and compliance play a big role. These functions sound the same, but they're not.
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

text:
Insurance companies typically have a legal department, also known as a law department, to handle issues related to law. In some companies, the legal department handles both legal and compliance functions. Smaller companies that operate in fewer jurisdictions are more likely to use this type of organizational structure.

Larger insurance companies are more likely to maintain separate legal and compliance departments. Yet even small companies sometimes separate the legal function from the compliance function organizationally to emphasize the importance of compliance and to ensure the independence of the compliance function.

Other insurers, typically very large insurers, may establish a compliance department responsible for ensuring corporate compliance with laws and regulations, but still delegate responsibility for each division's compliance to compliance personnel in that division. For example, a company that is organized by product line might delegate compliance responsibility to individuals in each of the company's product lines.

Generally, the person in charge of an insurance company's legal department is known as the general counsel or chief counsel. The general counsel usually reports directly to the company's CEO and advises the company's board of directors on important legal issues affecting the company. Attorneys and other legal department staff report to the general counsel.

In some companies, the general counsel is also the company's chief compliance officer. The chief compliance officer (CCO) is responsible for overseeing and managing the company's compliance with regulatory requirements and internal policies and procedures. If compliance activities are part of the legal department's duties, the general counsel also manages the compliance managers and specialists. If the CCO is a separate position from the general counsel, the chief compliance officer may report to the general counsel or to the CEO.
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

text:
Learning Objective

Describe the primary responsibilities of the legal department.

Which of the following activities do you think the legal department of an insurer would be involved in? Choose all that apply.

Incorporation, Licensing, and Corporate Changes

Insurance laws in the United States and Canada typically require that insurers be organized as corporations. As a corporation, an insurer can be a party in a legal action, enter into contracts, and own property. Click or touch each heading below to learn more.

Domestic Corporations

The jurisdiction in which an insurer incorporates, or forms the corporation, is known as its domicile, and the insurer is known as a domestic corporation of that jurisdiction.

Once incorporated, the company's legal department must obtain a certificate of authority, or license, which is a document that grants the insurer legal authority to conduct an insurance business in the specified jurisdiction. As a general rule, most jurisdictions require insurance companies and insurance financial professionals to be licensed by or registered with a specific regulatory agency before conducting business in that jurisdiction.

An insurer can sell only the products or services authorized by its certificate of authority or license. If a company's board of directors decides to expand into additional product lines or jurisdictions, the legal department oversees the application process for additional licenses.

Multinational Corporations

A corporation that operates in more than one country—known as a multinational corporation—faces unique legal issues in that it must comply with the laws of its country of domicile and also the laws and regulations of each country in which it conducts business. The legal department advises the board of directors on the best way to enter into business in a foreign market.

The legal department also handles many aspects involved in expanding or reducing operations. For example, an insurance company can expand operations by acquiring or merging with another company. In an acquisition, one company gains ownership control by buying most or all of the other company's stock or assets. In a merger, a company agrees to unite with another company to form a new company. In both cases, the companies can gain access to additional products, customers, distributors, and markets.

On the other hand, an insurer may decide to sell blocks of business or spin off a company to exit a line or lines of business. For example, a company preferring to exit the individual life insurance market could sell its block of individual life insurance policies through assumption reinsurance to another insurer. The company could also separate from one of its divisions or a company that it owns through a spinoff. The independent company created in the spinoff would own the original company's individual life insurance business. The legal department would be involved in these transactions as well.

Watch this animation for a summary of the corporate transactions we have discussed in this section.

In addition, the legal department is available to advise the board of directors on any number of issues that arise during the course of the company's operations. For example, the board may want the legal department to review a contract with a foreign company to ensure that it doesn't violate any trade agreements before the contract is signed.

Representing the Insurer in Legal Proceedings

At times, a person or organization may initiate a legal proceeding, known as a lawsuit, against a life insurance company, or an insurer may institute a lawsuit against another person or organization. For example, insurers deny a small percentage of life insurance claims for reasons such as the policy was not in force at the time of the insured's death, the deceased was not covered under the policy, or the cause of death was excluded from the insurance coverage. If a policyowner or beneficiary disputes an insurer's decision to deny a claim for benefits, the insurer's claim department, in conjunction with the legal department, works with the claimant to settle the dispute. If the dispute cannot be settled, and a lawsuit is filed, the legal department oversees the litigation process.

A lawsuit is an action brought before a court of law by a party claiming that they have been harmed in some way by another party. The process or act of presenting the dispute to a court of law for a resolution is known as litigation. In such situations, the legal department represents the insurer, or arranges for an independent law firm, often known as outside counsel, to represent the insurer in the litigation process. If the company chooses outside counsel for representation, the insurer's legal department communicates with and oversees the outside counsel. Attorneys who handle litigation for an insurance company, whether company employees or outside counsel, are responsible for

Initiating or responding to a lawsuit

Researching the facts of a case

Taking statements and depositions from involved parties

Consulting with company employees before they testify in court

Researching relevant court cases

Hiring expert testimony, if needed

Representing the insurer in all aspects of the litigation process, including correspondence and court appearances

Litigation can be time consuming and costly. Additionally, a public trial can result in negative publicity that may harm the company's image. For these reasons, the legal department typically tries to settle legal disputes without going to court if at all possible. Formal or informal negotiations to resolve the dispute are known as alternative dispute resolution (ADR) methods. Two common methods of ADR are mediation and arbitration. Click or touch in the boxes below to learn more.

Mediation

Mediation is a process in which an impartial third party, known as a mediator, facilitates negotiations between the parties in an effort to create a mutually agreeable resolution of the dispute.

If the parties cannot resolve their dispute through mediation, they typically have the right to go to arbitration or to continue with litigation.
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

text:
Arbitration

Arbitration is a process in which an impartial third party, known as an arbitrator, evaluates the facts in the dispute and renders a decision that usually is binding on the parties.

An appeal of an arbitrator's decision is generally possible only if the arbitration was done improperly.

Working with Internal Functions

The legal department interacts with and provides support to every internal function within an insurance company. Some of the legal department's specific responsibilities include the following (click or touch the right arrows below to learn more).

Human Resources

Advises on employment laws, particularly those related to employee rights, benefits, and nondiscrimination in employment practices.
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

text:
Learning Objectives

Describe the primary responsibilities of the compliance department.

Explain the difference between solvency laws and market conduct laws, and describe various laws that insurers must comply with.

Why is compliance important for an insurer? An insurer that fails to comply with a law or regulation may suffer significant financial consequences. Which of the following consequences can occur when an insurer is found to not be in compliance with applicable laws and regulations? Choose all that apply.

The compliance department, in conjunction with the legal department, monitors all applicable laws and regulations, communicates compliance requirements to employees through policies and procedures, and makes sure that company employees are following the company's established policies and procedures. Actions of the compliance department may include

Disseminating regulatory information to employees

Overseeing the licensing, training, and conduct of financial professionals

Approving advertisements and sales literature

Protecting customer privacy and confidentiality

Coordinating internal and external audits

Coordinating market conduct examinations

Working with regulatory agencies to demonstrate the company's compliance with applicable laws

Managing a fraud prevention unit

In the United States, the states are the primary regulators of insurance; and they have insurance laws designed to protect the public trust. These laws can be divided into two general types. Click or touch in the boxes below to learn more.

Solvency Laws

Solvency laws ensure that insurers are financially able to meet their debts and pay policy benefits as they come due.

Market Conduct Laws

Market conduct laws ensure that insurers conduct business fairly and ethically.

Employees who handle an insurer's financial matters generally manage solvency compliance. Compliance department employees generally are responsible for ensuring that the company complies with market conduct laws.

Areas of State Market Conduct Compliance

Market conduct requirements seek to ensure that insurance companies treat policyowners fairly with respect to marketing and sales practices, policy provisions, claim handling, and customer service. In the United States, market conduct laws are often based on model regulations proposed by the National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC), a private, nonprofit association that promotes uniformity of state insurance regulation. Click or touch each heading below to learn about areas with which insurers in the U.S. need to comply with state laws.

Product Development

Advertising

Financial Professionals and Sales Activities

Underwriting Practices

Claims Practices

Complaint Handling

Privacy

Electronic Signatures, Contracts, and Documents

Cybersecurity

Insurance Fraud

Federal Regulation of Insurance

While the states have primary authority for regulating insurance, insurers are also subject to federal regulation. Here are a few of the areas governed by federal laws with which insurers must comply:

Functional Regulation
The Gramm-Leach-Bliley (GLB) Act permits financial firms to affiliate in ways that result in a more competitive and integrated financial services industry. In addition, the GLB Act requires companies to maintain policies to safeguard the security and confidentiality of customer information and requires states to have uniform or reciprocal insurance agent licensing laws.


Accountability and Consumer Protection
The Dodd-Frank Wall Street Reform and Consumer Protection Act (Dodd-Frank Act) is a U.S. federal law that improves accountability and transparency in the financial services system and protects consumers from abusive practices.

Use of Consumer Credit Information
The Fair Credit Reporting Act is a U.S. federal law that regulates the reporting and use of consumer credit information. It seeks to ensure that consumer credit reports contain only accurate, relevant, and current information.


Terrorism and Money Laundering
The USA PATRIOT Act is a federal law designed to prevent, detect, and prosecute terrorism and international money laundering. According to the act, U.S. insurers that sell products that can be used to launder money, such as individual permanent life insurance, individual annuities, and individual products that have a cash value or investment feature, must have an anti-money-laundering program.

Regulation of Securities

The U.S. federal government has the primary authority to regulate the securities industry. Examples of securities include stocks, bonds, mutual funds, variable life insurance, and variable annuities. Federal securities laws provide a framework that includes registration requirements; regulation of the purchase and sale of publicly traded securities; reporting and filing requirements; and dispute resolution requirements, periodic examinations of registered entities and individuals, and the right to take action against those who violate laws. The Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is the federal agency responsible for administering federal securities laws.

Regulatory Compliance Management Programs

Most insurers establish regulatory compliance management programs that focus on prevention, education and training, and monitoring. Although we present these three initiatives individually here, they overlap in many ways and ideally are integrated throughout business processes.

Prevention

To have a successful regulatory compliance management program, management must commit to an ethical work environment. Compliance and ethics are related concepts. A company that operates within a framework of integrity and ethical values also maintains a culture of compliance. Use the right arrows below for examples of a culture of compliance.

The company has in place hiring practices that reinforce the company's commitment to compliance.

In a culture of organizational compliance, every employee views compliance as his own responsibility.

For a regulatory compliance management program to be effective, compliance personnel must be knowledgeable about the regulatory requirements of all jurisdictions in which the company operates. Personnel must also continually monitor the regulatory environment for changes that might impact insurer operations. If the right policies and procedures are in place, and if employees follow these policies and procedures, then problems with noncompliance are significantly reduced or eliminated.
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

text:
Finally, an insurer must establish internal control systems that can assess how well employees are adhering to the company's compliance policies and procedures. Internal controls may be as simple as establishing a procedure for a compliance officer to sign off on marketing materials before they are used, or as complex as installing a computer program that can detect confidential information in an employee's email.

Education and Training

To be effective, a regulatory compliance management program must educate employees about the laws and regulations relevant to their work activities. For example, in the United States, state insurance laws describe the minimum information that insurers must maintain in their complaint records and place specific requirements on the way insurers handle customer complaints. Just recording a customer's complaint isn't enough to satisfy these laws. Customer service representatives and any other employees who are likely to receive customer complaints must be trained in a company's complaint handling procedures. In addition, all company employees must be aware of and understand the company's policies and procedures for complying with these laws, so that they do not inadvertently violate a law.

Much market conduct legislation applies to the insurance sales process. Because insurers are generally held responsible for the actions of their financial professionals, insurers spend a significant amount of time and money training financial professionals in compliance issues.

Many companies provide their employees and financial professionals with compliance manuals containing information about the company's compliance programs and the market conduct rules they must follow. Ideally, such manuals provide employees with specific examples of compliance and noncompliance so that employees can better identify compliance risks in their day-to-day work activities. In addition, a regulatory compliance manual must be updated as regulatory requirements change, and such changes should be clearly communicated to employees.

Here, an expert explains the role that compliance plays in training financial professionals.

Monitoring

An insurer's regulatory compliance management program must include monitoring. Why? To ensure that the company (1) responds quickly to issues before they become bigger problems, (2) identifies training needs of employees, and (3) improves the quality of compliance procedures.

A common way to monitor compliance is through auditing. An internal audit is an examination of a company's records, policies, and procedures that is conducted by a person who works for the insurer, typically in the compliance area. An internal audit examines a specified area of a company's operations rather than the entire company. The results of an internal audit are reported directly to the audit committee of the insurer's board of directors.

Audits conducted by parties not associated with the insurance company are called external audits or independent audits. Insurance laws in many countries require external audits of an insurer's market conduct.
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

text:
Learning Objectives

Describe the purpose of a market conduct examination and how the compliance department is involved in market conduct examinations.

Distinguish between two types of market conduct examinations in the United States.

Periodically, in the United States, insurance regulators conduct examinations of the operations of life insurance companies. A market conduct examination is a formal investigation of an insurer by one or more state insurance departments. The purpose of the market conduct examination is to determine whether the insurer's market conduct—its nonfinancial operations—is in compliance with applicable laws and regulations.

There are two types of market conduct examinations. See if you can figure out which kind is described below.

hrght examining all nonfinancial aspects of its operations.

Some state insurance departments periodically examine each insurer licensed by the state. For example, a state may examine all insurers operating within the state every two to three years. Other states conduct examinations when they identify specific complaints about an insurer's market conduct activities or have a specific concern about an insurer's operations.

One of the primary roles of the NAIC is to advise state insurance regulators on the most effective and efficient methods for overseeing the market conduct of insurance companies. The NAIC encourages multistate examinations as a way for state insurance departments to share costs. However, states reserve the right to independently conduct their own reviews.

Both target and comprehensive examinations are designed to ensure that the

insurer has standards in place for the activity being examined

insurer's standards comply with applicable regulatory requirements

activity being examined is carried out according to established standards

State laws require that insurers maintain various business records for a specified time and allow examiners to review these business records as well as other documents. Market conduct examiners also want to know whether an insurer has established an internal audit plan by which it can detect and correct compliance problems.

Market conduct examiners review the insurer's audit plan and audit reports, as well as all accompanying procedures manuals. Market conduct examiners review how management uses the information in an audit report. For example, does management respond to recommendations in an audit report by adopting new procedures or modifying existing procedures?

Most market conduct examinations today are target examinations that examine one or more lines of business or specific areas of an insurer's nonfinancial operations, such as its advertising materials. Target examinations are conducted whenever an insurance department thinks them necessary, and often result from customer complaints or recent changes in applicable regulations.

What Happens After the Completion of a Market Conduct Exam
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

section: The insurer may be required to provide periodic evidence of compliance with any order contained in the report.
text: The more you understand what legal and compliance do, the better you'll be at avoiding potential legal liability for your company. Remember, compliance isn't just a function within your company: it's every employee's responsibility.
source: LOMA291_M3L3_Knowledge File_The Legal and Compliance Function.docx

text:
Policyowners and beneficiaries depend on their insurer to meet its obligations when they come due. The insurer’s financial area has a large part of the responsibility of ensuring the company remains strong for its customers. In this module, we’ll discuss the variety of activities that take place in the financial management area and we’ll explore the financial management goals that are common to all insurers.

In this module, you’ll learn about

The different aspects of an insurer’s financial operations, including accounting, financial reporting, treasury, investing, and auditing

The goals of an insurer’s financial operations, including maintaining solvency, achieving profitability, and managing capital

---- - -- - --

As a financial services company, an insurer has to manage not only its own funds but also the funds of its customers. Learn about the various functions that help an insurer stay on a sound financial footing and that contribute to its profitability.

Describe how an insurance company's chief executive officer (CEO) and senior financial management direct the company's financial operations.

Describe the responsibilities of the accounting and financial reporting function.

Identify internal and external users of accounting information, and describe how financial accounting and management accounting meet their different needs.

Describe how insurers use budgets and cost accounting as control tools.

Describe the treasury operations activities of cash management and liquidity management.

Describe the investment management function, including the role of the investment committee and the purpose of the investment policy.

Describe the different types of securities, and distinguish between debt securities and equity securities.

Distinguish between an insurer's general account and separate account portfolios.

Explain the importance of the audit and internal control function, and describe other players in an insurer's financial operations.

Learning Objective

Describe how an insurance company's chief executive officer (CEO) and senior financial management direct the company's financial operations.

At a typical insurance company, which of the following areas are part of financial operations? Choose all that apply.

Accounting and financial reporting

Treasury operations

Information technology

Investment operations

Internal audit

Forthright's financial operations unit not only tells the world about the company's success but it also plays a crucial role in ensuring that success. Financial operations assists management by doing things like providing financial reports, safeguarding and managing assets, and developing investment strategies for the company. This unit must respond to the demands of the company's board of directors, regulators, investors, and other stakeholders.

In this lesson, we'll explore who does what in Forthright's financial operations unit and the role this area plays in making crucial financial decisions.

Board and Executive Roles

The financial management function

Provides information to stakeholders

Ensures that financial obligations are met

Ensures compliance with complex financial regulations

Generates adequate returns for the company's owners and customers

Many players are involved in the financial management of Forthright. Let's meet some of them now.

The Role of the Board of Directors

At Forthright, financial management starts with the board of directors. Two standing committees of the board have a primary role in financial operations.

Investment committee: The investment committee sets Forthright's investment policy and oversees investment operations.

Audit/risk committee: The audit/risk committee directs Forthright's audit and internal control function.

The Role of the CEO

As CEO, I approve our budget numbers. Then I manage our resources—in line with budget guidelines and all current laws and regulations.

Technically I'm responsible for directing financial objectives and strategies, but I delegate most of these responsibilities to senior financial officers.

However, make no mistake, the buck stops here! We are a publicly held company. Investors, customers, and regulators can, and should, hold me responsible for our financial results.

The Role of the CFO

As CFO, I report to Grace Avon, our CEO. I work closely with company directors to develop corporate goals and strategies. I coordinate, monitor, and direct Forthright's financial activities.

That covers a lot of territory. In the financial world, information quality and accuracy are critically important—I work closely with IT to make sure we get that right. That information has to be communicated to company managers and external parties.

When we need to raise funds—that's also my job.

Last, but not least, strong financial controls and measurement tools are absolutely essential.

Achieving financial goals often requires a collaborative effort from executives in different roles.

Listen to an expert discuss how the CFO and chief operating officer (COO) work together to achieve company goals.

Financial Management Operating Units

Specific job titles and organizational structures vary according to a company's unique needs, but all insurers must perform certain core financial functions, as shown below.

True or false?

An insurance company's investment committee directs the company's internal audit and control function.

True

False

Learning Objectives

Describe the responsibilities of the accounting and financial reporting function.

Identify internal and external users of accounting information, and describe how financial accounting and management accounting meet their different needs.

Who directs the accounting and financial reporting function? The Controller.

My title at Forthright is Controller. Basically, I'm responsible for the timely and accurate collection and reporting of all of Forthright's financial data. What does that mean? Let's take a closer look at the accounting function.

The accounting function

Records, tracks, and reports on financial transactions

Coordinates the budget process and oversees expense analysis

Prepares financial statements and reports for external users

Gathers, records, analyzes, and distributes financial information to company managers

We produce many reports for many different parties. The type of report we create depends a lot on "who wants to know?"

Stakeholders from inside and outside the company use accounting information to make decisions. Who are these "customers" of the accounting department?

Of the following stakeholders, select the ones Forthright would consider to be internal users of its accounting information. Choose all that apply.

Regulators

Rating agencies

Board of directors

Policyowners

Managers

Investors

Taxing authorities

User Information Needs

Different parties have different needs for accounting information. Among other things, managers within the organization may use accounting information to

Assess their departments' day-to-day operations

Judge whether they are meeting their financial goals

Plan for future needs

Now let's look at external users. Click or touch the right arrow to meet several users.

As a policyowner, I want to know that Forthright will have enough money to pay its bills. I want to be sure that it is financially stable. Will Forthright be able to pay my claim 25 years from now?

Financial Accounting and Management Accounting

Companies use two different types of accounting to meet the diverse needs of the users of their financial information:

Financial accounting focuses on the needs of external users such as regulators, creditors, investors, rating agencies, and tax authorities.

Management accounting focuses on the needs of internal users such as company managers.

Financial Accounting

Financial accounting starts with recording financial transactions. The end goal is the production of the company's financial statements.

Wait!

You said that financial accounting is for external users—does this mean that internal managers don't use financial statements?

NO. I use financial statements to evaluate Forthright's performance. Financial statements show me whether we're meeting our goals and using our resources as best we can.

Financial statements summarize a company's financial transactions. These financial transactions can be product-specific items—premiums, commissions, investment transactions, marketing and product development expenses.

But there are also those items common to all businesses—payroll, disbursement of funds for operational expenses, like rent, keeping the lights on, and taxes.

Tax Accounting

Large insurers often have a separate function that handles tax reporting, which has its own set of reporting rules. In smaller companies, the accounting function typically is responsible for tax reporting.

For insurers, one unique aspect of tax accounting is premium taxes. Forthright pays premium taxes on the premiums paid to Forthright within a particular jurisdiction. Premium taxes are calculated as a percentage of these premiums.

That covers financial accounting. Let's contrast that with management accounting.

Since we've already discussed financial accounting, can you figure out which of the choices below describes management accounting?

Helps external parties understand the company's financial situation and performance

Helps company managers make the best use of internal resources

Has a primary goal of producing financial statements for the company

Let's try one more. True or false?

Management accounting is limited to analyzing past company performance.

True

False

Management Accounting

Management accounting can focus on different parts of the business, such as

Product lines or products

Specific business functions

Specific operating units

I use management accounting tools to

Measure the profitability of products and services

Analyze operating costs and manage expenses

Budget resources to support goals

Evaluate the efficiency of procedures and personnel

The person who manages accounting and financial reporting is generally the

CFO

Controller

CEO

One type of tax paid by insurers is premium taxes. Most jurisdictions assess an insurer's premium taxes as a

Percentage of premium income received

Percentage of assets held

Flat amount for each company

(Financial / Management) accounting reports on past performance and the current situation. (Financial / Management) accounting is designed to help managers make the best use of resources.

(Financial / Management) accounting reports on past performance and the current situation. (Financial / Management) accounting is designed to help managers make the best use of resources.

Financial / Financial

Financial / Management

Management / Financial

Management / Management

Learning Objective

Describe how insurers use budgets and cost accounting as control tools.

Now let's talk about two of the most important tools of management accounting: budgets and cost accounting.

Budgets

A budget is a plan of action, expressed in monetary terms, that covers a specified time period.

Remember controls? A budget can be used as a steering control, a concurrent control, and a feedback control.

Steering Controls: Managers develop revenue and expense projections for a specific period, usually one year. They use these projections to create a budget, which provides financial goals and guidance for activities during the year.

Concurrent Controls: During the year, managers compare their actual results to budgeted projections, and then alter their activities accordingly. For example, if expenses are ahead of budget at mid-year, the company or a unit might cut back on certain expenses for the rest of the year.

Feedback Controls: At year end, managers compare actual operating results to the budgeted numbers. Deviations between the two sets of numbers guide future activities and future budgets.

Budget Variances

At the end of the year, a difference between actual results and the budget is known as a budget variance. Managers probe budget variances to find what caused them. When they understand the causes, they can make adjustments for the future.

Let's look at an example.

I'm seeing some budget variances for this quarter. Take a look at this.

| Operating Unit | Revenues | Expenses |
|---|---|---|
| Unit A | On target | Lower than budget |
| Unit B | Higher than budget | Lower than budget |
| Unit C | On target | On target |

Which unit was the most successful?

Unit A

Unit B

Unit C

It depends

Suppose Barry, from Sales, comes in over budget on revenues. Should he be proud of this? Maybe, but there are some questions to ask. Like

…How did he achieve the high revenue?

…Did he sell products appropriately?

…Did external factors contribute?

Or consider the opposite. Should managers be happy if expenses are under budget? Maybe, but...

…Was quality or service sacrificed in cutting expenses?

…Could the money allocated to that unit have been better used elsewhere?

You can see how questions arise in either situation. A well-prepared budget allows a business to think about its goals and the best way to achieve them.

It's critical that budgets provide reliable estimates for revenues and expenses. Whether a variance is positive or negative is less important than the “Why?” You can't tell what effect a budget variance will have until you look at what is behind the numbers.

Cost Accounting

Cost accounting is a system for accumulating and categorizing expense data. The cost accounting information generated by the accounting area helps Forthright answer questions like these:

What are our costs for a specific line of business?

What will our future costs be for a specific product?

What department, product, or line of business generated a specific cost?

Managers use the answers to these questions to manage their expenses and allocate resources.

In your company, you might hear terms like ''expense analysis,'' ''expense accounting,'' and “cost allocation” used to describe cost accounting functions.

An Ethical Dilemma

(Click or touch the arrows to the right.)

Hi Terrence, I want to explain a couple of things about this expense report. I want to make sure it goes through, because I really need this check.

If Terrence does not fulfill the responsibilities of his position, his job could be at risk.

Just because an amount is in the budget doesn't mean that this amount must be spent. All employees are responsible for controlling expenses and ensuring that resources are put to their best use.

Ultimately, the employee's success is tied to the company's success.

True or False?

A budget is both a steering control and a feedback control.

True

False

Managers should aim for their actual expenses to be below budget.

True

False

section: Treasury Operations and the Investment Management Function
text:
Learning Objectives

Describe the treasury operations activities of cash management and liquidity management.

Describe the investment management function, including the role of the investment committee and the purpose of the investment policy.

I work in treasury operations, which is all about cash management: cash going in and cash going out. If it has to do with cash receipts, payments, or banking—that's Treasury Ops stuff. I report to Forthright's Treasurer. At Forthright, Treasury Operations reports up to the CFO—which is pretty typical.

Treasury Operations

Controls cash flows in and out of the company

Involves cash receipts, payments, and banking

Is headed by the Treasurer, who reports to the CFO

Treasury Operations

Treasury operations manages cash and ensures that Forthright also earns the best possible return. Click or touch each Treasury function below to learn more.

Manages Liquidity

Ensures that cash is available to meet Forthright's contractual obligations

Tracks Cash Flow

Receives and disburses money

Authorizes cash disbursements

Records and allocates cash transactions

Banking

Sets up bank accounts

Reconciles bank statements

Manages lockboxes for premium payments

Earns a return on cash

Returns on cash?

You bet! Even cash is invested overnight or over the weekend. The rate of return may be small, but it's a lot of money. We can't afford to have it just sit there.

Treasury

Invests excess cash in very short-term arrangements (overnight loans)

Arranges for very short-term borrowing as needed (overnight borrowing)

Managing Investments

How insurers manage their investments affects not only policyowners and stockholders, but also other financial market participants.

All investments involve potential risks and returns. Financial managers identify risk and return characteristics and cash flow patterns and then build portfolios of investments that are likely to produce the desired results.

Let's look at the organization and operation of the investment department and how the investment staff adheres to certain guidelines when making decisions.

Investment Organization

Investment operations can be organized as a separate department—as it is at Forthright. Other options are that investment operations can be

A subsidiary corporation

A major division within a broader corporation, serving both the life insurance area and other business units

Outsourced to another company

Some companies have a Chief Investment Officer (CIO), who reports directly to the CEO. At Forthright, the investment function reports to the CFO. Whatever the organizational scheme, the direction for the investment function starts at the top—with the board of directors.

This figure illustrates the interplay between the investment committee and a company's investment operations.

Investment Policy

An investment policy is a set of rules and guidelines that specifies

Long-term investment objectives for the company

Acceptable investment strategies for achieving investment goals

Acceptable types of investments

Controls and risk management procedures (like policies defining who is authorized to make what trades)

Limits on specific investments, prohibited assets, and trading activities

Consistent standards for measuring investment performance

The CIO or CFO makes recommendations to the board regarding investment policy. When investment decisions are made, he makes sure they are in line with investment policy and regulatory requirements. He also communicates to the accounting and actuarial areas the current and expected rates of return on the company's investments.

So what does everyone else do? Click or touch in the box below to see.

Other Jobs in Investment Operations

Other jobs in an insurance company's investment department include

Investment analysts, who conduct research into specific investment opportunities

Economists, who forecast and track economic trends

Portfolio managers, who coordinate investment strategies for specific investment accounts

Traders, who buy and sell securities such as bonds and stocks

The Treasury Operations area is responsible for (choose all that apply):

Lending cash overnight

Borrowing money for the long term

Borrowing cash overnight

Lending money for the long term

Of the following activities, select the ones that are generally the responsibility of the investment committee of the board of directors. Choose all that apply.

Setting long-term investment objectives for the company

Conducting research into specific investment opportunities

Buying and selling securities

Placing limits on the types of investments that the company may hold

section: Securities in the Insurer's General and Separate Accounts
text:
Learning Objectives

Describe the different types of securities, and distinguish between debt securities and equity securities.

Distinguish between an insurer's general account and separate account portfolios.

Insurers invest primarily in financial securities such as high-quality bonds, commercial mortgages, and corporate stock. A financial security represents either an investor's

Ownership interest in a business or property—equity security

OR

Loan of funds to a debt issuer—debt security

Help! What is a mortgage? What is a bond? What is corporate stock?
